speaker: General Secretary
prompt: The opposition will boycott unless you hand the election to a neutral caretaker administration, as the old custom demanded. "Concede the caretaker," the Secretary warns, "and you might lose. Refuse, and you win a vote the world calls a sham."

left.label: Refuse the caretaker, hold the vote under your government.
left.tooltip: A contest I run is a contest I survive.
left.reveal: You keep control of the machinery and win the seats. Turnout is thin, the opposition cries fraud, and your mandate carries an asterisk abroad.

right.label: Concede a neutral caretaker administration.
right.tooltip: A legitimate loss beats an illegitimate win.
right.reveal: You gamble on a clean vote. The world applauds the rare concession; your own machine panics that you have bet the house on the people.

speaker: General Secretary
prompt: Having kept control of the vote, the count in a dozen swing seats is 'adjustable' — the returning officers await a signal. A clean count risks your majority; a managed one cements it but leaves fingerprints.

left.label: Order the count managed in the swing seats.
left.tooltip: A majority is not left to chance.
left.reveal: Your majority is secured and unmistakably engineered. Observers document the irregularities; the result is yours and rotten at the root.

right.label: Let the count stand, whatever it gives.
right.tooltip: A stolen seat is a debt that comes due.
right.reveal: You accept the honest tally and a slimmer majority. Governing is harder; the legitimacy you keep may matter more than the seats you lost.

speaker: Election Commission Liaison
prompt: The terms of the election commissioners are up and you choose their replacements. The liaison presents two lists: pliable loyalists who will see nothing, or respected independents who might rule against you when it counts.

left.label: Appoint the loyalist commissioners.
left.tooltip: An umpire I pick does not call me out.
left.reveal: The Commission becomes an extension of your office. It serves you flawlessly — and the institution's credibility, once spent, will not return when you need it.

right.label: Appoint credible independents.
right.tooltip: A referee everyone trusts protects the winner too.
right.reveal: You stack the body with respected names. They will sometimes rule against you, but the elections they bless will be believed.

speaker: Street Operations Coordinator
prompt: The opposition has called a week of hartals and transport blockades; petrol bombs are landing on buses and the economy is freezing by the day. The coordinator wants to flood the streets with your own cadres to 'break' the blockade by force.

left.label: Deploy party cadres to break the blockade.
left.tooltip: Meet their streets with ours.
left.reveal: Your cadres clear the roads in running battles. Commerce resumes amid the wreckage, and the cycle of street violence now belongs to you both.

right.label: Keep cadres home, secure transport with police.
right.tooltip: Two mobs are worse than one.
right.reveal: You refuse to militarise your supporters and let the police protect the routes. The blockade drags on and the economy bleeds, but no partisan blood is on your hands.

speaker: Chief Whip
prompt: Your majority is wobbling; a bloc of independent MPs will cross to your side — for ministries, contracts, and quiet immunity from their pending cases. The Whip says it is the cheapest way to lock the floor of the house.

left.label: Buy the defectors with posts and immunity.
left.tooltip: A secured floor is worth a few favours.
left.reveal: The MPs cross and your majority is iron. The cabinet bloats with bought men, their cases frozen, and the public reads the price tag.

right.label: Refuse to trade immunity for votes.
right.tooltip: I will not staff a government with men I had to bribe.
right.reveal: You decline the deal and govern on a knife-edge. Every vote is now a fight, but no minister sits in your cabinet to dodge a courtroom.

speaker: Religious Front Emissary
prompt: A powerful religious movement offers its street power and its voters — in exchange for a blasphemy law, control of textbook content, and a quiet end to certain women's-rights reforms. The emissary smiles: "We can fill any square in the country."

left.label: Take the alliance and its demands.
left.tooltip: Their squares are full and my margins are thin.
left.reveal: Their mobilisation is yours now, and so are their conditions. The curriculum narrows, reformers despair, and your secular base feels sold.

right.label: Decline and hold the secular line.
right.tooltip: I will not trade women's rights for a turnout.
right.reveal: You refuse their terms. They can now march against you instead of with you, but the constitution's spine, and your liberal base, hold.

speaker: Tribunal Prosecutor
prompt: The tribunal trying the 1971 war criminals is both a genuine reckoning and a useful weapon against today's opposition. The prosecutor asks how far to push — strict evidence and slow justice, or fast verdicts that thrill the base and decimate your rivals.

left.label: Push fast, sweeping verdicts.
left.tooltip: Justice delayed is the guilty escaping.
left.reveal: The convictions land quickly to roaring crowds, and your opposition is gutted. Legal observers question the haste, and the verdicts carry that shadow.

right.label: Insist on slow, airtight due process.
right.tooltip: A reckoning built on shortcuts collapses.
right.reveal: You demand rigorous trials. The base grows impatient and the weapon is blunter, but the verdicts that come will withstand history's scrutiny.

speaker: Student Wing President
prompt: Your student wing controls the campuses through guest-room torture, tender extortion, and seat-control of the halls. The president wants more autonomy and more money. A reformist faction inside the party wants the wing reined in before it becomes a scandal.

left.label: Empower the student wing further.
left.tooltip: The campuses are a fortress I cannot lose.
left.reveal: The wing tightens its grip and delivers the campuses. Its abuses grow more brazen, a powder keg awaiting one viral video.

right.label: Rein it in and curb the worst leaders.
right.tooltip: A wing that tortures students will one day burn me.
right.reveal: You discipline the bosses and cut the extortion. The campuses loosen from your grip, but you have defused a bomb under your own house.

speaker: Party Elders' Council
prompt: The grey-haired council that has run the party for decades wants you to anoint your child as heir-apparent now, to 'preserve unity'. The reformers warn that a dynastic coronation will hollow the party of every talent who is not your blood.

left.label: Anoint your child as successor.
left.tooltip: A clear heir prevents a war of knives.
left.reveal: The dynasty is secured and the council placated. Capable non-family leaders drift away, and the party's depth thins to a single bloodline.

right.label: Open the succession to merit.
right.tooltip: A party is not an inheritance.
right.reveal: You refuse to crown an heir and invite a real contest. The family is wounded and the council nervous, but the party keeps its best minds.

speaker: Attorney General
prompt: A graft case decades old has finally ripened against the opposition leader. Convict her and she is barred from the next election; the bench will rule whichever way you nod. "One judgment," the Attorney General murmurs, "and your only rival watches the vote from a cell."

left.label: Push the conviction and the ban.
left.tooltip: Remove the rival, remove the race.
left.reveal: She is sentenced and disqualified. Her party erupts and the chanceries issue statements, but the ballot now has no name on it but yours.

right.label: Let the case run honestly, ban or no ban.
right.tooltip: A throne with no challenger is a target.
right.reveal: You refuse to script the verdict. She may yet stand against you, but no one can say you jailed your way to power.

speaker: Attorney General
prompt: With the leader jailed, her elderly frame ailing in custody, the prison doctor warns that a death behind your bars would make her a martyr forever. Her family begs for hospital release; your hardliners call it weakness.

left.label: Keep her confined, deny the release.
left.tooltip: A martyr in a cell is safer than a saint set free.
left.reveal: You hold firm and the cell stays locked. Her health worsens in the headlines, and every cough becomes an indictment of you.

right.label: Grant medical release on humane terms.
right.tooltip: I will not let a corpse defeat me.
right.reveal: You let her convalesce under guard at home. The base grumbles that the snake is loose, but you have denied her the one victory captivity offered.

speaker: Telecom Regulator
prompt: On the eve of the vote the regulator can throttle mobile data nationwide — 'to stop rumours,' officially; to blind the observers and opposition pollwatchers, in truth. A dark net makes a managed count invisible.

left.label: Throttle data through election day.
left.tooltip: What the cameras cannot upload did not happen.
left.reveal: The country goes dark and the count proceeds unwatched. The result is clean-looking and universally disbelieved; the outage itself becomes the story.

right.label: Keep the networks open.
right.tooltip: A vote no one can film is a vote no one believes.
right.reveal: You let the signal flow. Every irregularity is livestreamed, but so is every honest queue, and the result keeps its credibility.

speaker: District Organiser
prompt: In the marginal districts the organiser proposes the old arithmetic: envelopes of cash, sarees, and tin sheets distributed ward by ward the night before polling. "Hunger votes for whoever fed it last," he shrugs.

left.label: Fund the ward-level handouts.
left.tooltip: An empty stomach has a short memory.
left.reveal: The trucks roll out and the margins firm up. The treasury thins, the habit deepens, and your win smells of the bazaar.

right.label: Ban handouts, run on delivery instead.
right.tooltip: Buy a vote once and you rent it forever.
right.reveal: You forbid the envelopes and campaign on roads and clinics built. The organisers fret over thin margins, but the votes you win, you keep.

speaker: Electoral Roll Officer
prompt: The rolls in your strongholds carry tens of thousands of ghosts — the dead, the duplicated, the never-born. The officer can purge them for a credible list, or leave them as a reserve of votes that never argue back.

left.label: Leave the ghost voters on the rolls.
left.tooltip: A loyal voter need not be a living one.
left.reveal: The phantom names stay, ready to be voted. Auditors will find them eventually, and when they do the whole tally is tainted.

right.label: Purge the rolls to a clean count.
right.tooltip: Ghosts vote, but they also haunt.
right.reveal: You scrub the list to the living. Your stronghold margins shrink honestly, and the register can finally survive a recount.

speaker: Nominations Board Chair
prompt: Party tickets for the winnable seats are the most valuable commodity in the country. The chair can auction them to the highest bidders — funding the whole campaign overnight — or award them on grassroots strength and clean records.

left.label: Sell the tickets to the richest bidders.
left.tooltip: A campaign runs on cash, not character.
left.reveal: The war chest fills in a week with businessmen-MPs who bought their seats. They will legislate for their own ledgers, and the grassroots see who was passed over.

right.label: Award tickets on grassroots merit.
right.tooltip: A bought candidate serves the buyer, not the booth.
right.reveal: You hand seats to organisers who earned them. The campaign chest stays lean, but your benches fill with people the wards actually know.

speaker: Media Adviser
prompt: A loyal tycoon offers to buy up the three biggest private TV channels and run them as your chorus, drowning out the critical press. The adviser calls it 'narrative dominance'; the cost is the last pretence of a free media.

left.label: Bless the takeover of the channels.
left.tooltip: He who owns the screen owns the story.
left.reveal: The airwaves turn to praise overnight. Dissent migrates to the shadows of the internet, and 'press freedom' becomes a foreign talking point against you.

right.label: Decline; let the channels stay independent.
right.tooltip: A choir of yes-men cannot warn me of fire.
right.reveal: You wave the tycoon off. The critical broadcasts sting nightly, but they are also the only honest mirror your government has.

speaker: Cyber Cell Commander
prompt: Your party's online cell wants a budget to flood the networks with fake personas — smearing critics, manufacturing trends, and burying inconvenient news under coordinated noise. The commander promises 'we win the feed before they wake up.'

left.label: Fund the troll army.
left.tooltip: The first lie of the morning sets the day.
left.reveal: Ten thousand sock-puppets march on command. The discourse rots, journalists are doxxed, and when the operation leaks it confirms every accusation of manipulation.

right.label: Shut the cell down.
right.tooltip: An army of lies eventually turns its guns inward.
right.reveal: You disband the operation. Your opponents own more of the timeline now, but your name is off the next disinformation exposé.

speaker: Coalition Manager
prompt: Your largest junior partner threatens to walk unless it gets a deputy-premiership and three more ministries — a price that would let its leader posture as co-ruler. Lose them and your majority cracks; keep them and you feed a rival under your roof.

left.label: Pay the price and keep the coalition whole.
left.tooltip: A government standing is worth a crowded cabinet.
left.reveal: You hand over the posts and the floor holds. Your partner struts as near-equal, and your own MPs resent the ministries they were promised.

right.label: Call their bluff and let them walk.
right.tooltip: I will not be governed by my own junior.
right.reveal: You refuse, and they storm out. The arithmetic tightens to a knife's edge, but every remaining minister knows you do not bend to blackmail.

speaker: Chief Whip
prompt: A no-confidence motion is on the order paper, and a dozen of your backbenchers are wavering. The Whip can break it with a mix of threats, frozen contracts, and midnight visits — or you can win the floor by actually answering the grievance behind the revolt.

left.label: Crush the revolt with the Whip's methods.
left.tooltip: Discipline is taught in the dark.
left.reveal: The waverers fall back into line, cowed. The motion dies, but a dozen MPs now serve you out of fear, and fear is a thin glue.

right.label: Address the grievance, win the vote openly.
right.tooltip: A backbencher heard is a backbencher kept.
right.reveal: You meet the rebels, concede a real reform, and win the floor in daylight. It cost you policy ground, but the loyalty you bought is genuine.

speaker: Constitutional Adviser
prompt: Your jurists can draft an amendment scrapping term limits altogether, clearing the way for indefinite rule. The two-thirds majority is within reach. The adviser notes the obvious: it is also the clearest possible signal that you intend never to leave.

left.label: Abolish term limits.
left.tooltip: Reform takes longer than a term allows.
left.reveal: The amendment passes and the horizon clears. At home it reads as ambition unmasked; abroad, as one more strongman erasing his own exit.

right.label: Leave the limits in place.
right.tooltip: A door I cannot lock behind me keeps me honest.
right.reveal: You let the constraint stand. The dynasty's grip looks less total, but you have spared yourself the label of president-for-life.

speaker: Home Secretary
prompt: You have the legal pretext to ban a troublesome rival party outright — dissolve it, seize its offices, bar its symbol. It would erase a threat in a stroke. It would also tell every other party that the law is just your hammer.

left.label: Ban the rival party.
left.tooltip: A weed pulled does not seed.
left.reveal: The party is dissolved and its cadres scattered or jailed. Your dominance is near-total, and so is the proof that you fear a fair fight.

right.label: Let it stand and beat it at the polls.
right.tooltip: A banned party becomes a legend; a beaten one becomes a footnote.
right.reveal: You leave the rival legal and defeat it in the open. It survives to fight again, but your win carries no asterisk.

speaker: Party Treasurer
prompt: The industrialist who has bankrolled three of your campaigns now wants his return: a state bank licence, a tax holiday, and a port concession. The treasurer reminds you the next campaign cannot be fought without him.

left.label: Grant the patron his rewards.
left.tooltip: The hand that feeds expects to be kissed.
left.reveal: He gets his licence and his concession. The campaign chest is safe for another cycle, and a single oligarch now owns a wing of your economy.

right.label: Refuse and find cleaner money.
right.tooltip: A party owned by one man has only one voter.
right.reveal: You turn him down and scramble for funding elsewhere. The next campaign will be leaner and harder, but no single patron holds your leash.

speaker: Ward Boss
prompt: Your most effective ward boss runs his neighbourhood through extortion, land-grabbing, and a leash on the local police. He delivers turnout like clockwork. A rival within the party says he is a scandal waiting to detonate.

left.label: Keep the strongman and his turnout.
left.tooltip: A made ward is a won ward.
left.reveal: He keeps delivering, and keeps grabbing. His enemies pile up, and one of his victims will eventually find a camera.

right.label: Cut him loose and clean the ward.
right.tooltip: A boss who owns the booth will one day own me.
right.reveal: You strip him of cover and let the cases proceed. The ward's turnout machine sputters, but a liability has been removed before it blew.

speaker: Women's Wing Chief
prompt: The reserved women's seats are yours to fill. The wing chief urges you to seat genuine grassroots women organisers; the fixers want them handed to wives and daughters of male donors as a courtesy. The seats are a signal either way.

left.label: Reward donors' kin with the seats.
left.tooltip: The seats are a thank-you, not a ladder.
left.reveal: Donors' wives take the benches and vote as told. The women's wing seethes, and the reform you boast of abroad rings hollow at home.

right.label: Seat real women organisers.
right.tooltip: A reserved seat should hold a leader, not a placeholder.
right.reveal: You elevate women who built the wards. The donors grumble at the snub, but your benches gain voices that actually represent someone.

speaker: Chief Whip
prompt: One of your own MPs — popular, principled, and increasingly loud about corruption — is being courted by the opposition. The Whip offers two routes: ruin him pre-emptively with a leaked file, or win him back with a real hearing for his complaints.

left.label: Pre-empt him with a smear file.
left.tooltip: A loud man is silenced before he sings.
left.reveal: The file leaks and his reputation buckles before he can cross. He is neutralised, and every honest MP learns what conscience costs in your party.

right.label: Win him back with a genuine hearing.
right.tooltip: An honest critic kept is worth ten bought loyalists.
right.reveal: You hear him out and act on one grievance. He stays, vocal but yours, and the benches see that integrity has a place after all.

speaker: Rally Coordinator
prompt: The party's national council is your chance for a million-strong show of force in the capital. The coordinator wants every district to bus in cadres — paid, fed, and housed at the party's cost — to fill the maidan and overawe rivals.

left.label: Stage the million-strong spectacle.
left.tooltip: Power unseen is power doubted.
left.reveal: The maidan overflows and the footage dominates the week. The cost is staggering and the city paralysed, but no one questions your reach.

right.label: Hold a lean, disciplined council instead.
right.tooltip: A crowd I had to rent proves nothing.
right.reveal: You convene a focused, modest gathering. The spectacle is smaller, but the treasury and the city's patience are spared, and the agenda actually gets done.

speaker: Tribunal Prosecutor
prompt: A war-criminal's final appeal is exhausted and the gallows wait. Carrying out the sentence on the eve of the election would electrify your base and dare the opposition to defend a collaborator. The prosecutor asks: justice on its own clock, or yours?

left.label: Time the execution for maximum advantage.
left.tooltip: Justice and the calendar can keep step.
left.reveal: The hanging lands days before the vote and the base roars. The cynicism is noted at home and abroad — a reckoning turned into a campaign prop.

right.label: Let the sentence run on the law's own clock.
right.tooltip: A gallows is not a campaign poster.
right.reveal: You let the process conclude when it concludes. The base would have preferred the theatre, but the verdict keeps its dignity intact.

speaker: Culture Minister
prompt: A proposal lands to rename the largest airport, a university, and a string of bridges after your family's founding figures. It would stamp the dynasty on the map for generations. Critics call it the cult swallowing the state.

left.label: Approve the renamings.
left.tooltip: A name on the map is a claim on the future.
left.reveal: The signs change across the country. Your lineage is everywhere now — and so is the evidence, for critics, of a personality cult.

right.label: Decline the wholesale renaming.
right.tooltip: A dynasty secure in itself need not shout.
right.reveal: You let the airports keep their names. The family's elders sulk at the modesty, but the state stays larger than your bloodline.

speaker: Polling Agent Coordinator
prompt: On polling day, your cadres in a contested district can seize the booths in the last hour, eject the rival's agents, and stuff the boxes. The coordinator says it locks twelve seats. It also produces twelve seats no one will believe.

left.label: Order the booth captures.
left.tooltip: The last hour decides the count.
left.reveal: The boxes fill and the seats are yours. Phone videos of ejected agents go viral by nightfall, and the dozen seats become a dozen scandals.

right.label: Forbid it; hold the booths clean.
right.tooltip: A captured booth is a confession on film.
right.reveal: You order the agents to stand down and the count to stand. You may lose some of the twelve, but you keep the only thing a margin is worth — belief.

speaker: Party Elders' Council
prompt: The party's gerontocracy clings to every committee chair while a generation of capable forty-somethings stalls beneath them. The elders warn that pushing them out invites chaos; the young warn that keeping them invites irrelevance.

left.label: Keep the old guard in their chairs.
left.tooltip: Loyalty earned over decades is not lightly retired.
left.reveal: The veterans stay and the peace holds. The talented young drift toward NGOs and business, and the party ages with its leaders.

right.label: Promote the next generation now.
right.tooltip: A party that cannot renew itself buries itself.
right.reveal: You move the forty-somethings into real power. The elders are wounded and noisy, but the benches suddenly have a future tense.

speaker: Parliamentary Affairs Minister
prompt: The Speaker's chair is yours to fill. A partisan Speaker would gag the opposition, ram bills through, and rule every point of order your way. A genuinely neutral one would let the house breathe — and occasionally let your rivals land a blow.

left.label: Install a partisan Speaker.
left.tooltip: The chair should sit on my side of the aisle.
left.reveal: Bills sail through and the opposition is muzzled at every turn. The house becomes a rubber stamp, and parliament's last shred of theatre dies.

right.label: Appoint a credibly neutral Speaker.
right.tooltip: A fair umpire makes my wins count.
right.reveal: The house gets a Speaker both sides respect. Your rivals get their moments, but the chamber's rulings carry weight again.

speaker: Overseas Wing Secretary
prompt: Your overseas chapters can funnel large, lightly-documented donations home through hundi channels. The secretary calls it patriotic generosity; the law calls it illegal foreign funding. It would fatten the chest and tie you to money you cannot explain.

left.label: Accept the diaspora hundi money.
left.tooltip: A son abroad still pays his mother's bills.
left.reveal: The funds arrive off the books and the chest swells. Somewhere a paper trail survives, and when it surfaces it will read as foreign money buying your government.

right.label: Refuse the undocumented channels.
right.tooltip: Money I cannot name is a leash I cannot see.
right.reveal: You insist on clean, declared donations only. The chest grows slower, but no foreign-funding exposé waits in your future.

speaker: Minorities Cell Convenor
prompt: The Hindu and minority vote has long been a reliable bloc for you against the religious right — but only if you protect it. After a spate of temple attacks, the convenor warns that token sympathy will not hold them; visible protection will, at a cost with the majority bloc.

left.label: Offer words, avoid antagonising the majority.
left.tooltip: A bloc that has nowhere else to go waits patiently.
left.reveal: You issue statements and little else. The minorities feel taken for granted, and the next attack erodes a loyalty you assumed was permanent.

right.label: Protect them visibly, prosecute the attackers.
right.tooltip: A protected vote is a kept vote.
right.reveal: You deploy security and jail the ringleaders. The hardline preachers rage, but the minority bloc rallies to you with real gratitude.

speaker: Strategy Director
prompt: To make an uncontested vote look competitive, the director proposes fielding 'independent' dummy candidates loyal to you — fake opposition to manufacture the appearance of a real race after the true opposition boycotts.

left.label: Field the dummy candidates.
left.tooltip: An empty stage needs painted rivals.
left.reveal: The ballot fills with your own men pretending to oppose you. Observers see through it instantly, and the charade becomes shorthand for the whole farce.

right.label: Run the real race, boycott and all.
right.tooltip: A staged fight fools no one and shames me.
right.reveal: You let the contest be what it is — thin, but honest. The optics of a low-turnout win are bad, but not as bad as being caught faking your own opposition.

speaker: Cabinet Secretary
prompt: The bureaucracy is full of officers who served the last government. The secretary offers a sweeping reshuffle — sideline the suspect, promote the loyal — to make the machine answer to you alone. It would be efficient, and it would gut the service's neutrality.

left.label: Purge and pack the civil service.
left.tooltip: A machine must answer to one hand.
left.reveal: Loyalists rise and capable neutrals rot in OSD limbo. The government does as told, and the institution's professionalism bleeds out with the purged.

right.label: Keep the service neutral and merit-based.
right.tooltip: A bureaucracy I gut once will fail me later.
right.reveal: You promote on competence and let officers serve the office, not the person. Some will drag their feet on your worst ideas — which is rather the point.

speaker: Party Historian
prompt: The anniversary of the party's founding massacre approaches. The historian wants the commemoration weaponised — every speech aimed at branding today's opposition as the heirs of the killers. It rouses the faithful and poisons the well further.

left.label: Weaponise the commemoration.
left.tooltip: Yesterday's blood is today's mandate.
left.reveal: The speeches land like blows and the base is electrified. The opposition is painted as murderers' heirs, and any hope of national reconciliation dies on the dais.

right.label: Commemorate with dignity, not as a cudgel.
right.tooltip: A grave is not a campaign stop.
right.reveal: You honour the dead without naming the living as villains. The base wanted more fire, but the day keeps its solemnity and you keep the high ground.

speaker: Law Minister
prompt: Two seats on the apex court are vacant and a major case on your government's legality is pending. The minister can elevate two reliable judges in time to hear it — or appoint on seniority and merit and let the case fall where it may.

left.label: Pack the bench before the case.
left.tooltip: A verdict is safest decided before the hearing.
left.reveal: Your judges take their seats and the ruling goes your way. The judgment is sound on paper and stillborn in credibility; the court is now visibly yours.

right.label: Appoint on merit, accept the risk.
right.tooltip: A court I own cannot protect me from the one who owns me next.
right.reveal: You elevate the senior, respected names. They may rule against you, but a judiciary that can say no is the only one whose yes means anything.

speaker: District President
prompt: The grassroots of a key district are in open revolt against their sitting MP — your man — for years of arrogance and graft. They threaten to sit out the campaign. You can back the incumbent and his machine, or back the workers and dump him.

left.label: Stand by the incumbent.
left.tooltip: A sitting MP's machine is not lightly broken.
left.reveal: You back your man and the machine grinds on. The workers feel betrayed and stay home, and the seat that looked safe wobbles.

right.label: Dump the MP, back the grassroots.
right.tooltip: A party that won't hear its base goes deaf.
right.reveal: You drop the incumbent and let the workers pick. He fumes and threatens to bolt, but the district roars back to life around its own choice.

speaker: Establishment Minister
prompt: Tens of thousands of government jobs are about to be advertised. The minister can quietly reserve a large share for party families and youth-wing cadres — buying a generation of loyalty — or run them on open merit and let the chips fall.

left.label: Reserve the jobs for the faithful.
left.tooltip: A job given is a vote owned for life.
left.reveal: Cadres' sons fill the rolls and a generation is bound to you. The merit-passed-over seethe, and the civil service inherits a cohort hired for loyalty, not skill.

right.label: Hire on open merit.
right.tooltip: A government staffed by favours governs like one.
right.reveal: You run a clean recruitment. The youth wing howls at the lost spoils, but you gain a competent intake and the credit for a fair process.

speaker: General Secretary
prompt: A senior leader who broke with you years ago — taking a faction with him — now seeks to return. He brings votes and organisers, but also ambition and a grudge. The Secretary asks whether to welcome him or let him rot outside.

left.label: Welcome him back with honours.
left.tooltip: A returned rival fights for you, not against.
left.reveal: He rejoins to fanfare, faction and all, and your numbers swell. He also resumes manoeuvring from the inside, an old knife back in the drawer.

right.label: Leave him in the cold.
right.tooltip: A man who left once leaves twice.
right.reveal: You refuse the reunion. His faction stays outside and occasionally bites, but your inner circle is spared a practised intriguer.

speaker: Religious Front Emissary
prompt: The religious bloc you allied with returns for more: the public execution of a blasphemy convict and a ban on a women's rights NGO. The emissary reminds you their street power kept you standing last winter.

left.label: Concede the new demands.
left.tooltip: An ally's price rises with his usefulness.
left.reveal: You give them the ban and the spectacle. The streets stay yours for now, but each concession shifts the country further from the constitution you swore to.

right.label: Refuse and absorb their anger.
right.tooltip: An ally who owns my conscience is my master.
right.reveal: You decline, and they threaten to turn their squares against you. The winter ahead is colder, but you have stopped feeding the thing that would devour you.

speaker: Leader of the House
prompt: With the opposition boycotting parliament entirely, you can ram through a year's worth of contentious bills against empty benches — no debate, no scrutiny. The leader of the house calls it efficient; the press calls it one-party rule by another name.

left.label: Pass the bills against empty benches.
left.tooltip: An absent opposition forfeits its say.
left.reveal: Laws pour through unopposed in days. The efficiency is real and the legitimacy is gone; you have legislated alone and it shows on every statute.

right.label: Slow down, leave room for return.
right.tooltip: Laws made in an empty room echo hollow.
right.reveal: You hold the most divisive bills and leave the door ajar for the opposition's return. Less gets done, but parliament keeps a pulse.

speaker: Election Tech Adviser
prompt: The Commission wants to roll out electronic voting machines nationwide. Your technicians can configure them to be auditable and transparent — or quietly opaque, with no paper trail to contradict the reported count. The opposition already distrusts the boxes.

left.label: Deploy opaque machines, no paper trail.
left.tooltip: A count with no receipt cannot be challenged.
left.reveal: The machines report whatever they report and nothing can check them. The opposition's distrust hardens into certainty, and every result is now disputed by design.

right.label: Insist on an auditable paper trail.
right.tooltip: A vote no one can recount is no vote at all.
right.reveal: Every machine prints a verifiable receipt. The technology stops being a conspiracy and starts being a count people might actually believe.

speaker: Field Security Chief
prompt: The chief proposes a quiet campaign of pressure on the opposition's polling agents in the marginals — late-night visits, false cases filed, families warned — so their booths go unwatched on the day. No watcher, no challenge.

left.label: Approve the intimidation campaign.
left.tooltip: An unwatched booth is an obedient booth.
left.reveal: The agents melt away and the marginals go unobserved. The pattern is unmistakable to anyone looking, and many are looking.

right.label: Forbid it; let their agents work.
right.tooltip: Fear at the booth poisons the whole well.
right.reveal: You order the pressure stopped. The opposition watches every box, catches you on nothing, and the contest is cleaner than your fixers wanted.

speaker: Chief Whip
prompt: A charismatic opposition MP — clean, articulate, a future leader on their side — is privately willing to cross to you, for a ministry and a free hand in his district. Poaching him weakens them and strengthens you, but signals that everything is for sale.

left.label: Poach him with a ministry and a free hand.
left.tooltip: Their best man is better as mine.
left.reveal: He crosses to fanfare and the opposition loses a future. He governs his district as a fief, and the message lands: in your house, talent is bought.

right.label: Decline; let him rise where he is.
right.tooltip: A man I had to buy will be bought from me.
right.reveal: You pass on the poach. He stays a thorn across the aisle, but your benches are not staffed by mercenaries, and your district bosses keep their turf.

speaker: Election Commission Liaison
prompt: The liaison can rig the small things that move the illiterate vote: your symbol first on every ballot, the opposition's buried, your candidates listed at the top. Tiny thumbs on the scale, individually deniable, collectively decisive.

left.label: Stack the ballot in your favour.
left.tooltip: A small edge, multiplied by millions.
left.reveal: Your symbol sits first on every paper and the marginal voter's thumb drifts your way. It is petty and effective, and observers log every tilted ballot.

right.label: Insist on a neutral, randomised ballot.
right.tooltip: A win bought by ballot order is not a win.
right.reveal: You order the symbols randomised and fair. You forgo a quiet edge, but the ballot itself stops being evidence against you.

speaker: City Campaign Manager
prompt: The capital's mayoral race is a referendum on your rule, and your candidate is a lacklustre loyalist. The manager can pour state resources into rigging it safe, or you can swap in a genuinely popular outsider who might owe you less.

left.label: Rig the loyalist's win with state muscle.
left.tooltip: The capital cannot fall to a rival.
left.reveal: Your man takes the city hall on a tide of state largesse and managed counts. He governs the capital you can no longer claim was freely won.

right.label: Field a popular outsider instead.
right.tooltip: A mayor the city chose serves the city — and reflects on me.
right.reveal: You back a candidate people actually like. He owes you little and may freelance, but a clean win in the capital buys you legitimacy money cannot.

speaker: Street Operations Coordinator
prompt: A renewed blockade has turned lethal — buses torched with passengers inside, a child among the dead. The coordinator wants to answer fire with fire and send your cadres to torch the opposition's offices in return. The cycle would consume the season.

left.label: Retaliate in kind against their offices.
left.tooltip: Blood answers blood, or it never stops.
left.reveal: Your cadres burn back and the season descends into mutual arson. The economy seizes, the morgues fill, and both sides own the ashes.

right.label: Hold your cadres; let the law pursue the arsonists.
right.tooltip: I will not turn a blockade into a civil war.
right.reveal: You refuse to escalate and let the police hunt the bombers. The blockade still bites, but the bloodshed has only one author, and it is not you.

speaker: Party Historian
prompt: A grand mausoleum-museum complex for the party's founder is proposed at vast expense, to be the obligatory pilgrimage of every schoolchild and official. The historian calls it national memory; critics call it the cult cast in marble.

left.label: Build the grand complex.
left.tooltip: A founder's shrine anchors a movement forever.
left.reveal: The marble rises and the pilgrimages begin. The reverence is enforced and total, and the treasury and the country's pluralism both pay for it.

right.label: Fund a modest memorial instead.
right.tooltip: A legacy that needs a palace was never solid.
right.reveal: You approve a dignified, smaller tribute. The family's elders feel slighted, but the funds and the breathing room for other histories survive.

speaker: Labour Wing Secretary
prompt: Your party's labour wing wants to lead the garment workers' wage agitation — a million votes if you deliver, but a collision with the factory owners who fund you. The secretary asks whose side the party is really on.

left.label: Side with the factory owners.
left.tooltip: The cheques come from the gate, not the floor.
left.reveal: You quietly throttle the wage push to keep the owners happy. The chest stays full, and a million workers learn whose party this really is.

right.label: Lead the workers' wage demand.
right.tooltip: A million floor votes outweigh a hundred gate cheques.
right.reveal: Your wing fronts the agitation and wins a real raise. The owners fume and trim their donations, but the workers' districts are yours for a generation.

speaker: Strategy Director
prompt: A by-election in a swing seat is a live test of your standing. The director can sandbag it with the usual machinery for a safe win that tells you nothing, or let it run clean as an honest barometer — and risk a humiliating loss that emboldens the opposition.

left.label: Sandbag the by-election safe.
left.tooltip: A loss now is a flood later.
left.reveal: You secure the seat the old way and avoid the bad headline. You also blind yourself to how thin your support has worn, governing on a number you faked.

right.label: Let it run clean as a true test.
right.tooltip: Better to read the ground than to pave over it.
right.reveal: You run it honestly. Win or lose, you finally know where you stand — and if you lose, you learn it now, while there is time to act.

speaker: Law Minister
prompt: A prominent editor has accused your government of corruption in print. The minister can have him hauled up for contempt and sedition, jailing him and chilling the rest — or you can answer the charge with evidence and let him print.

left.label: Jail the editor for contempt.
left.tooltip: An example silences a chorus.
left.reveal: He is jailed and the newsrooms flinch. The corruption story is buried under his arrest, which becomes the bigger, longer-running scandal.

right.label: Rebut him with evidence, leave him free.
right.tooltip: A government that answers fears nothing.
right.reveal: You publish the records that refute him and let him keep printing. The base wanted him jailed, but you have won the argument instead of the arrest.

speaker: Constitutional Adviser
prompt: Article 70 already lets you expel any MP who votes against the party line, making your benches utterly obedient. The adviser proposes tightening it further, criminalising even abstention. It would end dissent — and end parliament as anything but a chorus.

left.label: Tighten the law against all dissent.
left.tooltip: A whip with teeth needs no second crack.
left.reveal: Abstention becomes career suicide and every vote is unanimous. Your benches are flawless and lifeless, a parliament that cannot think, only obey.

right.label: Loosen it; allow conscience votes.
right.tooltip: An MP who cannot ever say no is not a representative.
right.reveal: You permit dissent on matters of conscience. A few votes get messier, but your benches contain humans again, and the house earns a little respect.

speaker: Coalition Manager
prompt: Seat-sharing talks with your allies are deadlocked. You can bully the small partners into accepting scraps — keeping the lion's share for your own candidates — or share generously to keep the alliance genuinely broad and grateful.

left.label: Keep the lion's share for your own.
left.tooltip: An alliance is a pyramid, not a partnership.
left.reveal: Your candidates get the winnable seats and the partners get crumbs. You dominate the alliance and hollow it, leaving allies who campaign at half-effort.

right.label: Share seats generously with allies.
right.tooltip: A partner fed campaigns harder than one starved.
right.reveal: You give the small parties real winnable seats. Your own count is slimmer, but the alliance pulls together and turns out as one.

speaker: Student Wing President
prompt: A youth-wing cadre has died in a clash with the opposition. The president wants a massive politicised funeral procession through the capital to inflame the base and provoke a confrontation that paints the rivals as killers.

left.label: Stage the inflammatory funeral march.
left.tooltip: A martyr's coffin is a recruiting drum.
left.reveal: The procession swells into a riot and the rivals are branded murderers. The base burns hot, the city burns with it, and another coffin is already on its way.

right.label: Hold a solemn, contained funeral.
right.tooltip: I will not turn a boy's grave into a battlefield.
right.reveal: You give the cadre a dignified, guarded farewell. The president wanted a spectacle and a casus belli; you gave him neither, and the city stays calm.

speaker: Foreign Liaison Officer
prompt: Foreign and domestic election observers have applied for accreditation. You can stall and restrict them — limiting where they go, what they see — to keep the managed parts of the vote out of frame, or accredit them fully and run a vote that can bear watching.

left.label: Restrict and stall the observers.
left.tooltip: Witnesses are only invited to a clean house.
left.reveal: The observers are penned far from the action and issue damning reports anyway. The restrictions themselves become the headline: a government afraid of being watched.

right.label: Accredit observers fully.
right.tooltip: A vote that can be watched is a vote that is believed.
right.reveal: You let the monitors go where they please. It forces discipline on your machinery, and the clean bill of health they give is worth a hundred press releases.

speaker: Anti-Graft Commission Chair
prompt: The graft watchdog is yours to aim. The chair awaits a target list: pursue only the opposition and your own clean rivals, or let the Commission investigate without fear or favour — including, inevitably, your own ministers.

left.label: Aim the Commission at rivals only.
left.tooltip: An investigator off the leash bites the master.
left.reveal: The watchdog hounds your enemies and ignores your friends. It is devastatingly useful and visibly partisan, and your own grafters learn they are untouchable.

right.label: Let it investigate without favour.
right.tooltip: A watchdog that only bites the neighbour guards nothing.
right.reveal: You unleash it on all comers, your side included. A few ministers fall and the base panics, but the Commission gains teeth the public actually trusts.

speaker: Constitutional Adviser
prompt: You can amend away the caretaker-government provision permanently, ensuring every future election is held under a sitting government — yours, for now. It locks in a structural advantage forever, and locks in the grievance that fuels every boycott.

left.label: Abolish the caretaker system for good.
left.tooltip: Why concede each election what I can settle once?
left.reveal: The amendment passes and the advantage is permanent. So is the opposition's casus belli; every future poll now inherits this original sin.

right.label: Preserve a neutral caretaker mechanism.
right.tooltip: A rule that survives me protects me from my successors.
right.reveal: You keep — or restore — a neutral poll-time authority. You surrender a structural edge, but you drain the poison from every election to come.

speaker: Party Treasurer
prompt: Most of the campaign chest is undeclared cash from sources best left unexamined. An election audit looms. The treasurer can launder the trail through shell foundations, or you can declare a clean, smaller chest and fight poorer but legal.

left.label: Launder the chest through shells.
left.tooltip: Money has no smell once it is washed.
left.reveal: The cash is scrubbed and the audit finds nothing — for now. The shells are a time bomb, and the day one bursts it takes a cabinet with it.

right.label: Declare a clean, smaller chest.
right.tooltip: A washed rupee still stains the hand that spent it.
right.reveal: You run on declared money and fight the campaign lean. The shortfall hurts at every rally, but no laundering scandal waits in your second term.

speaker: Party Elders' Council
prompt: The extended family — cousins, in-laws, the founder's grandchildren — all want safe seats this cycle. The council frames it as keeping the bloodline central. The reformers warn the ballot is starting to look like a family reunion.

left.label: Hand safe seats to the family circle.
left.tooltip: Blood holds when everything else bends.
left.reveal: The relatives are slotted into winnable seats. The dynasty's grip tightens, and the photo of the new parliamentary party looks like a wedding album.

right.label: Limit the family to a few, open the rest.
right.tooltip: A party of cousins has no roots.
right.reveal: You cap the relatives and open seats to organisers. The family is wounded and loud, but the benches gain people the wards chose.

speaker: Hill Affairs Convenor
prompt: The hill peoples will back you if you finally implement the long-stalled peace accord — land rights, troop drawdown, autonomy. The settlers and the army want the accord buried. The convenor says you cannot satisfy both.

left.label: Side with the settlers and the army.
left.tooltip: The plains vote outnumbers the hills.
left.reveal: You shelve the accord again. The garrisons and settlers are pleased, and the hills add another broken promise to a decades-long ledger of them.

right.label: Implement the accord for the hill peoples.
right.tooltip: A promise twenty years deferred is a wound.
right.reveal: You move on land rights and a partial drawdown. The army and settlers resist hard, but the hills, for once, see the state keep its word.

speaker: Returning Officer Coordinator
prompt: The count is in and tighter than hoped. The coordinator can delay the announcement overnight to 'reconcile' the close seats — adjusting where needed — or declare the results as counted, the moment they are ready, blemishes and all.

left.label: Delay to 'reconcile' the close seats.
left.tooltip: A few hours buys a comfortable margin.
left.reveal: The overnight delay yields a tidier result and an obvious one. The gap between the late seats and the rest tells its own story to anyone counting.

right.label: Announce results as counted.
right.tooltip: A count that needs the night to fix was never counted.
right.reveal: You declare on schedule, narrow margins and all. Governing the thinner majority is harder, but the result lands before anyone can call it cooked.

speaker: Personal Physician
prompt: Your own health is failing faster than the public knows. The physician urges you to name and groom a successor now, while you can shape the transition — but doing so makes you a lame duck the moment it is announced, and the vultures will gather.

left.label: Conceal it; rule as though immortal.
left.tooltip: A wounded king shows no blood.
left.reveal: You hide the decline and grip the wheel. The succession stays unsettled, and the day the truth breaks the party will tear itself apart over a vacuum.

right.label: Name and groom a successor now.
right.tooltip: A transition I design beats one fought over my bed.
right.reveal: You begin the handover on your own terms. Your authority ebbs the moment you announce, but the dynasty gains a real chance of surviving you.

speaker: Election Commission Liaison
prompt: The opposition demands a recount in a flagship seat your candidate narrowly 'won'. The liaison can refuse on a technicality and let the result stand, or grant a transparent recount — which might overturn the seat and dent your majority.

left.label: Refuse the recount on a technicality.
left.tooltip: A win declared is a win kept.
left.reveal: You deny the recount and the seat stands, disputed forever. The technicality fools no one, and the seat becomes a permanent symbol of the stolen vote.

right.label: Grant a transparent recount.
right.tooltip: A seat that survives a recount is truly mine.
right.reveal: You allow the recount under cameras. You may lose the seat, but the seats you keep are now unassailable, and the gesture buys rare goodwill.

speaker: Information Minister
prompt: The national-day broadcasts are yours to script. You can rewrite the founding story to centre your family and erase rivals' contributions — cementing the dynasty as the nation's sole author — or tell an inclusive history that shares the credit.

left.label: Rewrite history around the family.
left.tooltip: He who tells the founding owns the nation.
left.reveal: The broadcasts crown your lineage as the sole begetter of the republic. Historians wince, rivals' heirs seethe, and the cult deepens by another generation.

right.label: Tell an inclusive founding story.
right.tooltip: A nation with one father is an orphan of the rest.
right.reveal: You share the credit across the founding generation. The family's mythmakers are disappointed, but the story gains the ring of truth and the nation's buy-in.

speaker: Inspector-General of Police
prompt: A fresh police intake is being recruited. The IGP can vet candidates for party loyalty — building a force personally bound to you — or recruit on merit and physical standards, building a force bound to the law.

left.label: Recruit for loyalty to you.
left.tooltip: A force that loves me will never aim at me.
left.reveal: Loyalists fill the ranks and the force becomes a praetorian guard. It will protect you faithfully — until the day loyalty to a person costs it the public's last trust.

right.label: Recruit on merit and standards.
right.tooltip: A police that serves the law outlasts the one that serves me.
right.reveal: You insist on a professional intake. The new constables answer to the badge, not your name — less pliable, but a force the public might one day respect.

speaker: Intelligence Chief
prompt: Whispers reach you that two senior ministers are quietly sounding out the army and the opposition about a post-you future. The chief offers proof through surveillance — or you can confront them in the open and force their hand.

left.label: Surveil and quietly neutralise them.
left.tooltip: A plot is best killed in the cradle.
left.reveal: You wiretap, gather leverage, and break them with it. The plot dies, but so does trust in your cabinet, where every minister now assumes he too is bugged.

right.label: Confront them openly, demand loyalty.
right.tooltip: Daylight scatters conspirators.
right.reveal: You face them down in front of the cabinet. The gamble forces a public reaffirmation of loyalty, and the cabinet sees a leader who fights in the open, not the shadows.

speaker: General Secretary
prompt: A corruption scandal has surfaced that leads, unmistakably, to a member of your own family. The Secretary can bury it with the usual machinery, or you can let an inquiry proceed and accept the wound to the dynasty's image.

left.label: Bury the scandal.
left.tooltip: Blood is not investigated.
left.reveal: The file vanishes and the family is shielded. The cover-up leaks in pieces, and 'one law for them' becomes the opposition's favourite chant.

right.label: Let the inquiry proceed.
right.tooltip: A dynasty above the law is a dynasty without one.
right.reveal: You allow the investigation, family or not. The relative is furious and the household tense, but the public sees, astonished, that the rules might apply to everyone.

speaker: Manifesto Committee Chair
prompt: The next manifesto is being drafted. The chair can load it with extravagant, undeliverable promises that thrill crowds and win the cycle — or write a sober, costed platform that under-promises and can actually be kept.

left.label: Promise the moon to win the cycle.
left.tooltip: A campaign is won on dreams, not spreadsheets.
left.reveal: The crowds roar at free electricity and a job for every graduate. You win, then spend the term explaining why the moon is still in the sky.

right.label: Write a sober, deliverable manifesto.
right.tooltip: A promise kept is worth ten promises made.
right.reveal: You run on what you can actually do. The rallies are less electric, but every pledge you redeem next term compounds into trust.

speaker: Student Wing President
prompt: Your student wing attacked a peaceful protest on campus, and the footage is everywhere. The president wants you to back the cadres and blame 'outside agitators'; the reformers want the attackers disciplined and an apology issued.

left.label: Back the cadres, blame outsiders.
left.tooltip: I do not abandon my own to a mob's verdict.
left.reveal: You defend the indefensible and the lie collapses on contact with the video. The campuses unite against you, and the wing learns it can attack with impunity.

right.label: Discipline the attackers, apologise.
right.tooltip: A wing that beats students is a liability, not an asset.
right.reveal: You suspend the cadres and apologise to the campus. The wing president seethes at the betrayal, but the wider student body, stunned, lowers its guard.

speaker: Elder Statesman
prompt: Through a respected intermediary, the opposition offers talks: end the boycott, accept your term, in exchange for electoral reforms and the release of jailed activists. The hardliners call it a trap; the statesman calls it the only exit from a deepening crisis.

left.label: Refuse the talks, govern alone.
left.tooltip: I do not negotiate with those I have already beaten.
left.reveal: You spurn the overture and rule unchallenged on paper. The crisis hardens underground, and the legitimacy gap you could have closed widens instead.

right.label: Open talks and trade reforms for peace.
right.tooltip: A rival at the table is not a rival in the street.
right.reveal: You negotiate, free some activists, and concede real reforms. The hardliners howl betrayal, but the country exhales, and your rule gains a legitimacy force never bought it.

speaker: General Secretary
prompt: At the upcoming party congress you can rewrite the internal rules to abolish elections for the top posts — appointing the leadership from above forever. The Secretary calls it stability; the cadres would call it the death of the party's last democracy.

left.label: Abolish internal elections.
left.tooltip: A party that votes on its head invites a coup.
left.reveal: The top posts become appointments and dissent within the party dies. You command total control of a machine that no longer argues — or thinks.

right.label: Keep internal elections alive.
right.tooltip: A party that cannot choose its own leaders cannot lead a country.
right.reveal: You preserve the internal vote. It means occasional surprises and rivals you did not pick, but the party stays a living thing with roots that hold.

speaker: General Secretary
prompt: With the rules settled, a charismatic provincial leader has won a groundswell of delegates and could challenge your chosen slate on the congress floor. You can disqualify him on a procedural pretext, or let the floor decide.

left.label: Disqualify him on a pretext.
left.tooltip: A challenge unanswered is a challenge avoided.
left.reveal: He is ruled ineligible and the slate passes unopposed. The delegates who backed him go home bitter, and the province cools toward you for a generation.

right.label: Let the floor decide.
right.tooltip: A leader the delegates chose serves stronger than one I imposed.
right.reveal: You let the contest run. He may win a post you did not plan to give, but the congress crackles with real life and the province feels seen.

speaker: Legal Affairs Secretary
prompt: The secretary proposes blanketing critical journalists and opposition figures with dozens of defamation suits across distant districts — not to win them, but to bury the targets in travel, lawyers, and fear. It is harassment dressed as law.

left.label: Launch the blanket defamation suits.
left.tooltip: A man in court has no time to campaign.
left.reveal: The critics drown in hearings across the country, exhausted and broke. The tactic is transparent, and 'judicial harassment' becomes the verdict on your rule abroad.

right.label: Forbid the lawfare campaign.
right.tooltip: A law used as a club is no longer a law.
right.reveal: You order the suits dropped. Your critics keep their freedom and their venom, but you are spared a reputation as a man who litigates dissent into silence.

speaker: Legal Affairs Secretary
prompt: One suit went ahead anyway, filed by an overzealous local leader, and a young reporter now sits in jail awaiting a hearing months away. The case is a national embarrassment. You can let it grind on, or intervene to free her.

left.label: Let the case grind on.
left.tooltip: To intervene is to admit the suit was mine.
left.reveal: You stay out and she stays in. Her detention becomes a cause célèbre, every passing week a fresh indictment of your tolerance for it.

right.label: Intervene to free her.
right.tooltip: A jailed reporter is a wound I can still close.
right.reveal: You quietly secure her release and rein in the local leader. The base mutters about weakness, but the embarrassment ends and a small debt of goodwill is repaid.

speaker: Delimitation Commissioner
prompt: The constituency boundaries are due for redrawing, and the commissioner can carve them to pack your opponents into a few seats and spread your voters thinly across many — a quiet, permanent advantage few will notice and none can easily prove.

left.label: Gerrymander the boundaries to your advantage.
left.tooltip: A map drawn well wins seats before a vote is cast.
left.reveal: The new map quietly tilts a dozen seats your way for a generation. The psephologists notice and cry foul, but the lines are set and the advantage banked.

right.label: Redraw the boundaries fairly on population.
right.tooltip: A rigged map is a confession printed in ink.
right.reveal: You insist on a neutral, population-based redraw. You forgo a structural edge, but the electoral map stops being one more piece of evidence against you.

speaker: Administrative Reforms Secretary
prompt: Local leaders clamour for their upazilas to be upgraded into full districts — a cascade of new offices, jobs, and contracts to hand out. The secretary calls it development; the treasury calls it a patronage machine paid for by everyone.

left.label: Create the new districts.
left.tooltip: A new district is a thousand new favours to grant.
left.reveal: You carve out the new districts and the patronage flows. Local bosses are delighted, and the bloated administration becomes one more permanent cost the budget never planned for.

right.label: Refuse the administrative inflation.
right.tooltip: I will not buy loyalty by the district.
right.reveal: You decline the upgrades. The disappointed local leaders sulk and some turn cold, but the state is spared a fresh layer of offices built purely to be staffed with friends.

speaker: Relief Distribution Coordinator
prompt: The food-relief and old-age allowance cards are distributed through your MPs, who hand them to loyalists and withhold them from opposition wards. The coordinator can keep the cards as a political weapon, or move to neutral, needs-based distribution.

left.label: Keep the relief cards as patronage.
left.tooltip: A card given is a vote owed.
left.reveal: The cards stay a tool of loyalty, rewarding your wards and starving the rest. The machine hums, and the genuinely hungry in the wrong wards learn that relief here wears a party colour.

right.label: Move to neutral needs-based distribution.
right.tooltip: Hunger does not check a voter card.
right.reveal: You strip the cards from the MPs and route them by need. Your local leaders lose a lever and rage, but the relief reaches the poorest regardless of how they vote.

speaker: City Campaign Manager
prompt: A clutch of city corporation elections looms — second-order contests that nonetheless read as a verdict on your rule. The manager can rig them quietly safe, or let them run clean as a controlled test of your real support in the towns.

left.label: Rig the city elections quietly.
left.tooltip: No verdict against me, however small, can stand.
left.reveal: You secure the cities the old way. The wins are hollow and the pattern noted, and you blind yourself to how the towns truly feel about you.

right.label: Let the city votes run clean.
right.tooltip: A town honestly won tells me where I stand.
right.reveal: You let the corporations vote freely. You lose a few mayoralties, but the honest results give you a true map of your support and a credibility the rigged wins never had.

speaker: Statistics Bureau Director
prompt: The census underpins everything — seats, funds, quotas — and the director hints the headcounts in key regions could be 'adjusted' to favour your strongholds. Honest numbers might cost you seats and subsidies; cooked ones poison every plan built on them.

left.label: Adjust the census in your favour.
left.tooltip: Numbers serve the state, and I am the state.
left.reveal: You tilt the headcounts toward your strongholds. The seats and funds flow your way, and every policy built on the false figures — schools, clinics, rations — is quietly misallocated for a decade.

right.label: Insist on an honest census.
right.tooltip: A nation that lies to itself in numbers cannot plan.
right.reveal: You demand accurate counts whatever they cost you. Some strongholds shrink on paper and lose seats, but the data the whole state relies on is finally trustworthy.

speaker: Liberation Affairs Secretary
prompt: The civil-service quota for descendants of liberation-war veterans — a third of all posts — was the spark of a national uprising. The secretary asks whether to defend it as honouring the founders, or reform it toward merit to defuse the youth's fury.

left.label: Defend the full veterans' quota.
left.tooltip: The children of those who bled deserve the state they built.
left.reveal: You keep the quota intact and honour the founding generation. Your base of veterans' families is loyal, but the merit-starved youth seethe, and the next spark may be larger.

right.label: Reform the quota toward merit.
right.tooltip: A republic staffed by birthright is no republic.
right.reveal: You slash the hereditary quota and open posts to merit. The veterans' lobby feels betrayed, but a generation of frustrated youth sees, astonished, that the state heard them.

speaker: Education Affairs Secretary
prompt: The campus student-union elections have not been held in decades, leaving the halls to your wing's unelected enforcers. Restoring real elections would democratise the campuses — and likely cost your wing its iron grip on them.

left.label: Keep the student elections suspended.
left.tooltip: An unelected wing is a controlled wing.
left.reveal: You leave the campuses to your appointed enforcers. The grip holds, but a generation graduates having never voted, learning democracy as something that happens to other people.

right.label: Restore real student-union elections.
right.tooltip: Democracy unlearned on campus is unlearned for life.
right.reveal: You bring back the campus vote. Your wing may lose its monopoly to independents and rivals, but the campuses come alive and a generation practises the franchise.

speaker: Religious Outreach Coordinator
prompt: A revered spiritual leader with millions of rural followers offers his endorsement — and his flock's votes — in exchange for state patronage for his shrine network and a blind eye to its finances. The coordinator says his word moves whole districts.

left.label: Take the pir's endorsement and his price.
left.tooltip: His word turns out the countryside.
left.reveal: You strike the bargain and his followers swing your way. The shrine network fattens on state favour beyond any audit, and you have rented a bloc you do not control.

right.label: Decline to trade patronage for a flock.
right.tooltip: A vote bought through a pir is a vote I never truly hold.
right.reveal: You pass on the deal. The rural bloc stays unmobilised for you and the pir cools, but you owe no shadowy shrine-empire a debt it will one day call in.

speaker: Labour Wing Organiser
prompt: The tea-garden workers — a captive, impoverished bloc bonded to the estates for generations — could be yours if you championed their derisory daily wage. But the estate owners are old donors who deliver the gardens' votes wholesale, their way.

left.label: Keep the estate owners onside.
left.tooltip: The gardens vote as the owners say.
left.reveal: You leave the bonded wage untouched to keep the owners happy. The estates deliver their votes as instructed, and a quarter-million workers stay trapped at a wage from another century.

right.label: Champion the tea workers' wage.
right.tooltip: A bonded vote is no foundation; a freed one is.
right.reveal: You back a real wage rise for the garden workers. The owner-donors retaliate with shut wallets, but the workers, stunned to be seen, become a bloc loyal to you and not the estate.

speaker: Electoral Reform Adviser
prompt: Reformers and small parties demand proportional representation to end the winner-takes-all distortions that hand you huge majorities on minority votes. The adviser notes PR is fairer — and would shrink your dominance to its true, modest size.

left.label: Keep the first-past-the-post system.
left.tooltip: Why trade a landslide for a coalition?
left.reveal: You preserve the system that magnifies your seats. The majorities stay commanding and the small parties shut out, and the gap between your seat count and your vote share stays a standing grievance.

right.label: Move toward proportional representation.
right.tooltip: A parliament that mirrors the vote is the only honest one.
right.reveal: You back a shift toward PR. Your dominance shrinks to its real proportion and coalition-building becomes the norm, but parliament finally reflects how the country actually voted.

speaker: General Secretary
prompt: A heavyweight regional leader threatens to break away and form a rival party, taking a chunk of your organisation with him. The Secretary can crush him pre-emptively with expulsions and cases, or accommodate his demands to keep the tent whole.

left.label: Crush him before he can split.
left.tooltip: A breakaway strangled in the cradle never breathes.
left.reveal: You expel him and bury him in cases before he can organise. The split is averted by force, and every ambitious leader learns the cost of independent thought.

right.label: Accommodate him to keep unity.
right.tooltip: A tent that loses a pole sags everywhere.
right.reveal: You meet his demands and keep him inside. Unity holds, but you have shown that a credible threat to leave is the surest way to win concessions from you.

speaker: Coalition Manager
prompt: A small but disciplined leftist party offers an alliance — useful street muscle and intellectual credibility — but its price is a swing left on labour, land, and privatisation that would spook your business donors. The manager weighs muscle against money.

left.label: Ally with the left, swing on policy.
left.tooltip: Their cadres and credibility are worth a tilt.
left.reveal: You bring the left in and shift your platform toward labour and land. The donors grumble and some drift, but you gain disciplined organisers and a sheen of conviction.

right.label: Keep the donors, decline the left.
right.tooltip: Ideas do not fund campaigns; businessmen do.
right.reveal: You pass on the alliance to keep your business backers content. The money stays, but you forgo the street muscle and the credibility, and the left turns its energy against you.

speaker: Tribunal Prosecutor
prompt: You can move to ban outright the religious party whose leaders collaborated in the 1971 atrocities — a genuine reckoning that also conveniently removes an opposition ally. The prosecutor notes the line between justice and political weapon is thin here.

left.label: Ban the collaborators' party.
left.tooltip: A party born of atrocity forfeits its place.
left.reveal: You dissolve the party and its symbol. The base roars at the reckoning, and the opposition loses an ally — but critics note how neatly justice served your politics, and the banned go underground.

right.label: Let it stand and defeat it politically.
right.tooltip: A martyr-party banned outlives a beaten one.
right.reveal: You leave the party legal and fight it at the ballot. It survives to needle you, but you deny it the banner of victimhood and keep the moral high ground over a real reckoning.

speaker: Rural Development Coordinator
prompt: The rural food-for-work and infrastructure funds are channelled through MPs who skim them and steer the projects to their own villages. The coordinator can keep the lucrative system or route the funds through transparent local government instead.

left.label: Keep the funds in the MPs' hands.
left.tooltip: An MP without projects to give is an MP without friends.
left.reveal: You leave the rural funds with your MPs. They build where it buys loyalty and skim the rest, and the genuinely needy villages with the wrong MP go without.

right.label: Route funds through local government.
right.tooltip: Roads should follow need, not the member's whim.
right.reveal: You hand the funds to elected local bodies with audits. The MPs lose a prized lever and resent it, but the works spread by need and the skim shrinks.

speaker: Religious Affairs Secretary
prompt: The state can fund imam allowances and pack the mosque management committees with your loyalists — turning the Friday pulpit into a quiet ally — or keep the mosques independent of the party's reach. The secretary says the pulpit moves more votes than any rally.

left.label: Capture the mosque committees.
left.tooltip: He who befriends the pulpit befriends the faithful.
left.reveal: You fund the imams and stack the committees with loyalists. The Friday sermons soften toward you, and the mosques become one more institution bent to the party's will.

right.label: Keep the mosques independent.
right.tooltip: A pulpit I own is a pulpit no one believes.
right.reveal: You leave the mosque committees free. You forgo the pulpit's quiet endorsement, but the faith keeps a distance from the party that lends its eventual support real weight.

speaker: Law Minister
prompt: The opposition's new rally chant mocks you savagely, and the minister can charge its authors with sedition — jailing them and chilling the streets — or laugh it off and let a slogan be a slogan.

left.label: Charge the chant's authors with sedition.
left.tooltip: Mockery, unanswered, becomes contempt.
left.reveal: You jail the sloganeers and the chant becomes infamous, sung at every rally now in defiance. The heavy hand turns a jingle into a movement and you into its punchline.

right.label: Laugh the slogan off.
right.tooltip: A leader who jails a chant has already lost to it.
right.reveal: You wave the mockery away with a joke of your own. The chant loses its charge for want of a martyr, and you look, for once, bigger than your critics.

speaker: Justice Reform Adviser
prompt: Pressure mounts for a truth commission into the abuses of the previous government — a genuine reckoning that would also, inconveniently, set a precedent for examining yours one day. The adviser asks how far to let it look.

left.label: Launch a far-reaching truth commission.
left.tooltip: A nation that cannot face its past repeats it.
left.reveal: You establish a real commission with teeth. The past regime's crimes are aired and victims heard, and you accept that the same mirror may one day be turned on you.

right.label: Keep any inquiry narrow and controlled.
right.tooltip: A precedent for digging up the dead is a shovel for my own grave.
right.reveal: You permit only a toothless, bounded review. The reckoning is stage-managed, the precedent contained, and the victims learn the truth was never really on offer.

speaker: Election Commission Liaison
prompt: A proposed law would force every party and candidate to disclose donors and spending — sunlight that cleans the system and exposes your own opaque, oversized war chest. The liaison asks whether to back it or quietly bury it.

left.label: Bury the disclosure law.
left.tooltip: A war chest examined is a war chest disarmed.
left.reveal: You quietly kill the bill. Your funding stays in the dark, and so does everyone's, and the suspicion that elections here are simply bought hardens into common knowledge.

right.label: Back full campaign-finance disclosure.
right.tooltip: Money in daylight buys less than money in shadow.
right.reveal: You support the sunlight law. Your own opaque chest faces scrutiny, but the system gains a check it never had, and you can claim a reform your rivals resisted.

speaker: Strategy Director
prompt: Reformers propose lowering the voting age to capture the vast, restless youth cohort. The director's polling says the young lean against you after the quota unrest — so enfranchising them is democratic, and electorally dangerous.

left.label: Lower the voting age.
left.tooltip: A franchise widened is a democracy deepened.
left.reveal: You enfranchise the youth despite their coolness toward you. It wins democratic credit at home and abroad, and you have handed the vote to a generation that may use it against you.

right.label: Keep the voting age as it is.
right.tooltip: I will not arm a cohort that means to vote me out.
right.reveal: You decline the reform. The status quo protects you from a hostile bloc, and the young add 'denied the vote' to a lengthening list of grievances against you.

speaker: Parliamentary Affairs Minister
prompt: With a real opposition finally in parliament, you can either grant its leader the traditional status, perks, and consultative role — a sign of a maturing democracy — or strip the position bare to keep your rival small and dependent.

left.label: Strip the opposition leader's role bare.
left.tooltip: A rival with a chair is a rival with a stage.
left.reveal: You hollow out the opposition leadership's perks and powers. Your rival is diminished, and parliament drifts further from the adversarial democracy it pretends to be.

right.label: Grant the opposition its proper status.
right.tooltip: A loyal opposition is the proof a democracy works.
right.reveal: You afford your rival the traditional standing and consultation. It legitimises an adversary, but it also legitimises the whole house, and the maturity is noticed abroad.

speaker: Reconciliation Adviser
prompt: The anniversary of a dark episode your party once perpetrated approaches. The adviser offers two paths: a genuine acknowledgment and apology that could begin to heal an old wound, or the usual silence that lets sleeping guilt lie.

left.label: Acknowledge and apologise.
left.tooltip: A wound named can finally close.
left.reveal: You offer a genuine acknowledgment of the old wrong. The base is uneasy and hardliners furious, but victims long ignored feel seen, and a festering grievance begins, at last, to heal.

right.label: Keep the customary silence.
right.tooltip: An apology is an admission, and admissions are weapons.
right.reveal: You let the anniversary pass unmarked. The party is spared an admission, and the unhealed wound goes on poisoning the well between you and those who remember.

speaker: Election Logistics Officer
prompt: In the far chars, haors, and hills, reaching every voter with a polling station is costly and slow — and the officer notes those remote areas lean against you. He can fund full access for all, or quietly let the awkward, hostile pockets go under-served.

left.label: Under-serve the hostile remote pockets.
left.tooltip: Why spend a fortune to be voted against?
left.reveal: You quietly thin the polling stations in the remote, unfriendly areas. Turnout there collapses for want of access, and the disenfranchisement, once noticed, becomes a quiet scandal.

right.label: Fund full access for every voter.
right.tooltip: A citizen unreachable by the ballot is a citizen abandoned.
right.reveal: You pay to reach every char and hill with a polling station. It costs dearly and may not help your numbers, but no Bangladeshi is denied the vote by geography on your watch.

speaker: Constitutional Adviser
prompt: A proposal floats to create an appointed upper house — billed as a chamber of elders and expertise, but in practice a soft landing for loyalists and a brake on any future reformist lower house. The adviser asks whether to build it.

left.label: Create the appointed upper house.
left.tooltip: A second chamber of friends is a second line of defence.
left.reveal: You establish the upper house and pack it with loyalists. It rewards the faithful and checks any unruly future majority, and critics rightly call it a parking lot for cronies.

right.label: Reject the appointed chamber.
right.tooltip: A house of cronies is not a check; it is a couch.
right.reveal: You decline to build a chamber of appointees. You forgo a tool of patronage and control, but you avoid bolting a permanent crony-park onto the constitution.

speaker: Home Affairs Coordinator
