prompt: Election-day clashes — some sparked by your own cadres — have left dozens dead and hundreds injured across the country. The coordinator asks whether to pay full, public compensation and investigate, or quietly pay off a few and move on.

left.label: Pay full compensation and investigate.
left.tooltip: Blood spilled at the ballot is the state's debt.
left.reveal: You compensate the victims openly and order real investigations, your own cadres included. The party is uneasy at the scrutiny, but the dead are honoured and the violence's authors face daylight.

right.label: Quietly pay off a few, move on.
right.tooltip: A long inquiry only keeps the bodies in the news.
right.reveal: You settle a handful of cases discreetly and let the rest fade. The headlines pass, the cadres learn there is no reckoning, and the families of the unacknowledged dead nurse a lasting grievance.

speaker: Campaign Data Director
prompt: A slick foreign consultancy offers to harvest the population's data and micro-target voters with tailored manipulation — the dark arts that have swung elections elsewhere. The director is dazzled; the catch is a scandal if the data harvesting ever leaks.

left.label: Hire the data-manipulation consultants.
left.tooltip: The other side will, if I do not.
left.reveal: You bring in the dark-arts firm and the targeting sharpens uncannily. It works — until the harvesting leaks, and your campaign becomes the local chapter of a global data-abuse scandal.

right.label: Run an honest, homegrown campaign.
right.tooltip: An election won by manipulation is a mandate built on sand.
right.reveal: You decline the manipulation machine and campaign on message and organisation. It is harder and slower, but no harvesting time-bomb sits ticking under your win.

speaker: Returning Officer Coordinator
prompt: On scrutiny day, returning officers can reject opposition candidates' nomination papers on hyper-technical grounds — a misplaced signature, a loan footnote — thinning the ballot before a vote is cast. The coordinator awaits the word on how aggressive to be.

left.label: Reject rivals' papers on technicalities.
left.tooltip: A candidate disqualified is a contest avoided.
left.reveal: Dozens of opposition hopefuls are struck off on trivia. Your candidates walk into emptier races, and the courts and observers log another way the contest was hollowed before polling.

right.label: Let valid nominations stand.
right.tooltip: A ballot thinned by clerks is a ballot no one trusts.
right.reveal: You order the officers to accept all proper nominations. The races are fuller and harder, but the contest cannot be dismissed as rigged at the starting line.

speaker: Strategy Director
prompt: Your polling is briefly riding high after a popular giveaway, and the director urges a snap election to lock in a fresh term before the economy sours. It is shrewd and opportunistic; it also reeks of cutting and running before the bill arrives.

left.label: Call the snap election now.
left.tooltip: Strike while the numbers smile.
left.reveal: You go to the polls on the sugar high and win comfortably. The fresh term is banked, and the soured economy and unpaid giveaway bill land squarely in your own new lap.

right.label: Serve the full term and face the music.
right.tooltip: A leader who runs from the bill admits he padded it.
right.reveal: You hold the scheduled date and govern through the hard patch. You forgo the easy win, but you cannot be accused of cynically timing the vote to dodge the consequences.

speaker: Chief Whip
prompt: A respected backbencher has voted his conscience against a flagship bill, and the constitution lets you expel him from parliament outright for crossing the line. The Whip wants him made an example; the alternative is to let one dissent pass.

left.label: Expel the dissenting MP.
left.tooltip: One conscience indulged becomes a chorus.
left.reveal: You strip him of his seat for a single honest vote. The benches fall silent and obedient, and the spectacle of a man expelled for conscience stains the parliament's name.

right.label: Let the single dissent pass.
right.tooltip: A parliament of automatons warns me of nothing.
right.reveal: You let the conscience vote stand without punishment. The hardliners fret about the precedent, but the benches keep a flicker of humanity and the house a shred of dignity.

speaker: Party Elders' Council
prompt: The ceremonial presidency is yours to fill. The council urges a pliant loyalist who will sign whatever you send and never use the office's reserve powers. A respected independent would lend the state dignity — and might, in a crisis, act on his own judgment.

left.label: Install a pliant loyalist as president.
left.tooltip: A rubber stamp does not develop a spine at midnight.
left.reveal: You elevate a reliable nonentity to the presidency. Every decree is signed on cue, and the one office that might restrain you in a crisis is neutered in advance.

right.label: Appoint a respected independent.
right.tooltip: A dignified head of state reflects on the whole house.
right.reveal: You choose a credible figure for the presidency. He lends the state gravity, and you accept that in a true crisis he might use the office's powers as his own conscience, not yours, dictates.

speaker: General Secretary
prompt: A small but well-organised regional party is open to merging into yours — instant reach in a region where you are weak, at the cost of absorbing its leaders, its baggage, and its demands for safe seats and posts.

left.label: Absorb the smaller party.
left.tooltip: Their region becomes mine overnight.
left.reveal: You merge them in and your map fills out in the weak region. Their leaders crowd your benches with new demands and old baggage, and your once-tight organisation grows lumpier.

right.label: Stay independent, build the region yourself.
right.tooltip: A merger buys a region and inherits its quarrels.
right.reveal: You decline the merger and invest in building your own base there. It is slower and the region stays thin for you, but your party keeps its coherence and owes no newcomers.

speaker: Manifesto Committee Chair
prompt: A draft of the manifesto has leaked, exposing an unpopular plan to cut a beloved subsidy that you meant to bury in the fine print. The chair asks whether to brazen it out and keep the plan, or disown the draft and reverse course.

left.label: Stand by the leaked plan.
left.tooltip: A policy abandoned at the first leak was never a policy.
left.reveal: You defend the subsidy cut openly. The honesty earns grudging respect in some quarters, but the beloved subsidy's defenders mobilise and the issue dogs the whole campaign.

right.label: Disown the draft and reverse.
right.tooltip: A leak is a chance to hear the room.
right.reveal: You bin the unpopular plan and recast the draft. The flip-flop draws mockery, but the beloved subsidy survives and the campaign's biggest landmine is defused.

speaker: Field Mobilisation Officer
prompt: Your machine can lay on free transport to ferry your voters to the booths on polling day — legal turnout muscle in your strongholds — while quietly ensuring the buses never reach the opposition's wards. The officer asks how clean to keep it.

left.label: Run partisan transport to your wards only.
left.tooltip: I move my voters; theirs can walk.
left.reveal: Your strongholds turn out in force on free buses while the opposition's wards wait for transport that never comes. The lopsided turnout flatters your win and fools no observer.

right.label: Provide transport neutrally or not at all.
right.tooltip: A bus that only carries my voters is a thumb on the scale.
right.reveal: You either drop the scheme or run it for all. Your raw turnout edge shrinks, but the poll-day logistics give your enemies nothing to point at.

speaker: Chief Whip
prompt: Your new majority is wafer-thin and a confidence vote looms in the first week. The Whip can secure it by buying a few independents with quiet promises, or you can win it on your genuine numbers and govern honestly from a fragile base.

left.label: Buy the independents for a safe margin.
left.tooltip: A government that wins its first vote narrowly never recovers.
left.reveal: You purchase the cushion and win comfortably. The bought independents will be back for more all term, and your government is mortgaged to them from its first day.

right.label: Win on your real numbers.
right.tooltip: A majority I had to buy is a majority I do not have.
right.reveal: You take the confidence vote on your honest count and scrape through. Governing the knife-edge is exhausting, but you owe no shadowy independents and the mandate is real.

speaker: Cabinet Secretary
prompt: Amid unrest, the secretary lays out the nuclear option: declare a state of emergency, suspend rights, rule by decree, and postpone the election. It would give you total control — and brand you, at home and abroad, as a ruler who tore up the rules to keep power.

left.label: Declare the state of emergency.
left.tooltip: Order first; legitimacy can be rebuilt.
left.reveal: You suspend rights and rule by decree. The streets fall silent under the clampdown, and the word 'dictator' attaches itself to you in every capital and every history book.

right.label: Govern through the crisis within the law.
right.tooltip: A leader who suspends the constitution has already abdicated.
right.reveal: You refuse the emergency and ride out the unrest within constitutional bounds. It is harder and more dangerous, but you remain a head of government, not a usurper.

speaker: Professional Wing Coordinator
prompt: The bar association, medical council, and engineers' bodies hold prestigious elections that shape professional opinion. The coordinator wants to capture them with party panels and patronage; the alternative is to let the professionals govern themselves.

left.label: Capture the professional bodies.
left.tooltip: The professions sway the educated; capture them.
left.reveal: Your panels sweep the professional bodies on patronage and pressure. The lawyers, doctors, and engineers' associations become party annexes, and their independent voice — and credibility — dies.

right.label: Leave the professions self-governing.
right.tooltip: A captured bar defends no one, least of all me.
right.reveal: You let the professional bodies run free. They will sometimes pass resolutions against you, but their independence keeps them respected, and a free bar is a check worth having.

speaker: Constitutional Adviser
prompt: If a neutral poll-time authority is to oversee the election, its chief would likely be the most recently retired chief justice. The adviser notes you could engineer the retirement timeline so that a pliable judge sits in that chair when it matters.

left.label: Engineer a pliable poll-time chief.
left.tooltip: Even a neutral office should hold a friendly face.
left.reveal: You quietly time the judicial retirements so your man heads the poll-time authority. The 'neutral' oversight is neutral in name only, and the opposition smells it instantly.

right.label: Let the chair fall where it falls.
right.tooltip: A poll umpire I picked is no umpire at all.
right.reveal: You leave the succession to run its natural course, friendly or not. You surrender a hidden lever, but the poll-time authority is genuinely impartial and its verdict will be believed.

speaker: Media Adviser
prompt: A beloved film and cricket star will endorse you — and stand for a safe seat — bringing glamour and youth appeal. The adviser is thrilled; the cynics note he knows nothing of governance and would be a hollow, gaffe-prone MP.

left.label: Field the celebrity candidate.
left.tooltip: Stardust wins seats that policy cannot.
left.reveal: The star joins to adoring crowds and wins his seat on charisma. He is a magnet for the young and a liability in the chamber, every gaffe a headline, every absence noted.

right.label: Use his endorsement, not his candidacy.
right.tooltip: A famous face is an asset; a famous fool is a cost.
right.reveal: You take the star's public backing but steer him away from a seat. You bank the glamour without saddling the benches with a celebrity who cannot legislate.

speaker: Coalition Manager
prompt: Your alliance must agree a common minimum programme, and the partners' wish-lists pull in opposite directions — secular reformers against the religious bloc, labour against business. The manager asks whether to paper over the cracks or force a coherent platform.

left.label: Paper over the contradictions.
left.tooltip: A vague platform offends no partner.
left.reveal: You fudge the programme into mush that everyone can sign. The alliance holds for the campaign, then tears itself apart in government over the contradictions you never resolved.

right.label: Force a coherent common programme.
right.tooltip: A coalition that cannot agree a page cannot govern a country.
right.reveal: You hammer out a real, consistent platform, losing a partner or two who cannot accept it. The alliance is smaller but governable, and voters know what it actually stands for.

speaker: Reconciliation Adviser
prompt: A party banned years ago — its grievance now a rallying cry — petitions to be restored to legal politics, promising to renounce violence. The adviser says readmitting it drains the poison of exclusion; the hardliners say it legitimises old enemies.

left.label: Keep the party banned.
left.tooltip: A banned enemy is a contained enemy.
left.reveal: You refuse restoration. The party stays underground, its exclusion a permanent grievance and recruiting tool, and the politics of the banned festers beyond the ballot's reach.

right.label: Restore it to legal politics.
right.tooltip: A grievance let into the chamber is a grievance out of the streets.
right.reveal: You readmit the party on conditions. The hardliners howl about legitimising enemies, but an underground grievance becomes a contestable political force, answerable to voters.

speaker: Field Mobilisation Officer
prompt: Turnout is your friend where you are strong and your enemy where you are weak. The officer can run a blanket national get-out-the-vote drive that lifts everyone's turnout, or a surgical one that wakes your wards and lets the opposition's sleep.

left.label: Run the surgical, partisan GOTV.
left.tooltip: I wake my voters and let theirs dream.
left.reveal: Your wards turn out heavily while the opposition's stay home, unprodded. The lopsided turnout pads your margins, and the selective mobilisation is one more managed feature of the result.

right.label: Run a blanket national turnout drive.
right.tooltip: A high honest turnout is a mandate money cannot buy.
right.reveal: You lift turnout everywhere, friend and foe alike. You forgo a turnout edge, but a high, genuine participation gives whatever result emerges a legitimacy nothing else can.

speaker: Local Government Secretary
prompt: Real devolution — money and power pushed down to elected local councils — would transform governance and accountability, but it would also loosen your central grip and empower local leaders, some of them rivals. The secretary asks how far to let go.

left.label: Keep power centralised.
left.tooltip: A capital that lets go of the purse lets go of the country.
left.reveal: You keep the money and decisions in the centre's fist. Your grip stays tight, and local government remains a hollow shell where accountability and initiative go to die.

right.label: Devolve real power and funds.
right.tooltip: A nation governed only from the capital is barely governed.
right.reveal: You push genuine money and authority down to elected councils. You loosen your grip and empower some rivals, but governance gets closer to the people and visibly better.

speaker: Culture Minister
prompt: A proposed law would make 'disrespect' of the flag and anthem a crime — wrapping you in patriotism and handing you a flexible cudgel against critics who could be charged for a gesture or a parody. The minister calls it honouring the nation.

left.label: Enact the flag-and-anthem law.
left.tooltip: The symbols of the nation are above mockery.
left.reveal: You criminalise disrespect of the national symbols. Patriots applaud, and the elastic offence becomes a convenient charge to level at any critic who steps out of line.

right.label: Leave patriotism uncompelled.
right.tooltip: Forced reverence is no reverence at all.
right.reveal: You reject the law. The hardliners grumble that you failed to honour the nation, but you decline a cudgel that would have dressed censorship as patriotism.

speaker: Election Tech Adviser
prompt: A new app would transmit every polling station's count straight to a public dashboard in real time — radical transparency that makes tampering nearly impossible, and equally makes any managed result impossible to manage. The adviser awaits your verdict.

left.label: Deploy the public real-time results app.
left.tooltip: A count the whole country watches is a count it believes.
left.reveal: You launch the transparent dashboard. Tampering becomes near-impossible and the result, whatever it is, is trusted — and you have closed off your own machine's options for good.

right.label: Keep results flowing through official channels.
right.tooltip: A dashboard that cannot be managed is a risk I need not take.
right.reveal: You stick with the slower official channels. The flexibility to 'reconcile' close seats survives, and so does the public's weary assumption that the count is never quite clean.

speaker: Education Affairs Secretary
prompt: The vast network of independent religious schools wants its degrees recognised as equal to university qualifications for government jobs — a huge bloc's long-held demand. The secretary says granting it wins the bloc; the secular establishment is appalled.

left.label: Recognise the religious degrees.
left.tooltip: A million graduates and their families are a bloc worth winning.
left.reveal: You grant equivalence to the madrasa degrees. The religious bloc swings gratefully behind you, and the secular establishment recoils as the civil service opens to a parallel education it cannot vouch for.

right.label: Require a bridging standard first.
right.tooltip: Equal recognition demands an equal standard.
right.reveal: You offer recognition conditional on bridging courses in core subjects. The bloc is disappointed at the half-measure, but the civil service keeps a baseline and the secularists are mollified.

speaker: Campaign Data Director
prompt: Your canvassers can quietly log every household's religion, income, leanings, and grievances into a vast voter database — a powerful targeting tool, and an intrusive dossier on the private lives of millions assembled without consent.

left.label: Build the household database.
left.tooltip: Knowledge of the voter is power over the vote.
left.reveal: Your canvassers compile an intimate map of every household. The targeting grows formidable, and a covert dossier on millions' private lives now sits on a party server, one breach from scandal.

right.label: Canvass without the covert profiling.
right.tooltip: A party that files its citizens is a state I do not want to lead.
right.reveal: You canvass for support without building the secret dossier. Your targeting is cruder, but you refuse to assemble a surveillance file on the people you ask to trust you.

speaker: Constitutional Adviser
prompt: You can dissolve parliament early to wrong-foot a fracturing opposition before it can organise — a sharp tactical move that also denies the house its full term and looks like manipulating the calendar for advantage. The adviser lays out the play.

left.label: Dissolve early to catch them unready.
left.tooltip: An opponent off-balance is an opponent beaten.
left.reveal: You dissolve the house and rush the polls. The disorganised opposition is caught flat-footed, and you wear the charge of gaming the calendar for the win it buys you.

right.label: Let parliament run its full term.
right.tooltip: A house cut short for tactics cheapens the next.
right.reveal: You let the term run out naturally. The opposition gets time to organise, but you cannot be accused of dissolving democracy's clock whenever it suited you.

speaker: Chief Whip
prompt: A bloc of your own backbenchers, closer to the public mood than the cabinet, is in open revolt over a deeply unpopular bill. The Whip can break them with threats and patronage, or you can heed them and amend the bill they say is poison on the doorstep.

left.label: Break the rebellion, pass the bill.
left.tooltip: A backbencher's job is to vote, not to think.
left.reveal: You whip the bill through over the rebels' objections. It passes, and so does the public anger they warned of, landing on the very MPs you forced to swallow it.

right.label: Heed the rebels, amend the bill.
right.tooltip: The benches closest to the people often see clearest.
right.reveal: You amend the bill to meet the backbenchers' concerns. The cabinet's pride is dented and the bill weakened, but you have averted a doorstep disaster and kept your MPs loyal.

speaker: Party Treasurer
prompt: A reform would cap corporate donations and require their disclosure — curbing the oligarchs' grip on policy, and choking the single biggest stream into your war chest. The treasurer warns the cut would hurt; the reformers say the influence is rotting the state.

left.label: Block the donation cap.
left.tooltip: A campaign starved of corporate cash is a campaign that loses.
left.reveal: You kill the cap and the corporate money keeps flowing. The war chest stays fat, and the oligarchs' hooks stay deep in your policy, exactly as the reformers feared.

right.label: Back the cap and disclosure.
right.tooltip: A party owned by donors governs for donors.
right.reveal: You support capping and disclosing corporate money. The war chest thins painfully, but you loosen the oligarchs' grip and can credibly claim to have cleaned a corner of the system.

speaker: Information Commissioner
prompt: A strengthened Right to Information law would force every office to open its files to any citizen who asks — a powerful anti-corruption tool, and a window into the very deals and decisions your government would rather keep dark. The commissioner awaits your stance.

left.label: Gut the Right to Information law.
left.tooltip: An open file is an open invitation to my enemies.
left.reveal: You quietly hollow out the RTI regime with exemptions and delays. The corruption-fighters lose their best tool, and 'a government with something to hide' becomes the obvious reading.

right.label: Strengthen and enforce the RTI law.
right.tooltip: Sunlight is the cheapest disinfectant a state can buy.
right.reveal: You give the RTI law teeth and enforce it. Some embarrassing files surface and ministers squirm, but corruption gets harder and your reform credentials grow real.

speaker: Parliamentary Affairs Minister
prompt: The parliamentary committee that scrutinises government spending is traditionally chaired by the opposition. You can deny them the chair and pack it with loyalists, neutering oversight, or honour the convention and let your own spending be examined.

left.label: Pack the oversight committee with loyalists.
left.tooltip: I will not hand my critics a microphone and my ledgers.
left.reveal: You take the chair and stack the committee. Government spending sails through unexamined, and the one institution meant to catch waste and graft is turned into a shield for it.

right.label: Give the opposition the chair.
right.tooltip: Spending watched is spending behaved.
right.reveal: You honour the convention and let the opposition scrutinise your ledgers. Some uncomfortable findings emerge, but waste is curbed and the house gains a working check.

speaker: Returning Officer Coordinator
prompt: The postal ballots of soldiers, police, and officials posted away from home are easy to bundle and steer. The coordinator can quietly manage these service votes as a reliable bloc, or process them transparently like any other ballot.

left.label: Manage the service ballots quietly.
left.tooltip: The men in uniform vote as the chain suggests.
left.reveal: You shepherd the postal votes into a reliable bloc. The numbers help, and the pattern — a service vote that breaks suspiciously one way — is logged by every observer watching.

right.label: Process service ballots transparently.
right.tooltip: A soldier's secret ballot is still a secret ballot.
right.reveal: You insist the postal votes be handled openly and freely. You forgo a managed bloc, but you cannot be accused of voting the barracks by proxy.

speaker: Coalition Manager
prompt: The election has produced no majority, and a handful of independents and tiny parties now hold the balance. The manager can assemble a government by lavishing them with ministries and money, or attempt a leaner minority government on issue-by-issue support.

left.label: Buy a majority with ministries and cash.
left.tooltip: A government formed is a government; how matters later.
left.reveal: You assemble the majority by buying the kingmakers wholesale. The cabinet bloats with bought men and their price tags, and your government begins life owing everyone everything.

right.label: Form a lean minority government.
right.tooltip: Better to govern honestly on a knife-edge than to buy a throne.
right.reveal: You build a minority government surviving vote by vote. It is precarious and exhausting, but it is not a bazaar, and each policy must be argued and won rather than purchased.

speaker: Women's Wing Chief
prompt: Women's groups demand that the reserved parliamentary seats be filled by direct election rather than party nomination, giving women real mandates instead of appointed tokens. It empowers women — and strips you of dozens of safe, controllable seats.

left.label: Make the reserved seats directly elected.
left.tooltip: A seat won is a voice; a seat granted is an echo.
left.reveal: You back direct election for the women's seats. You lose dozens of controllable appointments, but a cohort of women MPs arrives with real mandates and real independence.

right.label: Keep the reserved seats by nomination.
right.tooltip: Why give away seats I currently simply assign?
right.reveal: You preserve the nomination system. The women's wing is disappointed and the tokenism critique stands, but you keep dozens of safe, loyal seats firmly in your gift.

speaker: Election Logistics Officer
prompt: On the quiet, the officer can relocate polling stations in opposition strongholds to distant, hard-to-reach venues announced at the last minute — depressing their turnout without a single ballot touched. It is deniable, surgical voter suppression.

left.label: Relocate the opposition's polling stations.
left.tooltip: A booth moved is a vote not cast.
left.reveal: You scatter the opposition's booths to far-off venues at the last hour. Their turnout sags as planned, and the manoeuvre, once mapped by observers, becomes textbook documented suppression.

right.label: Keep polling stations fixed and local.
right.tooltip: A voter should not need a map to find democracy.
right.reveal: You keep every booth where the voters expect it. You forgo a quiet suppression tactic, but no one can chart a pattern of vanished polling stations in your enemies' wards.

speaker: Law Minister
prompt: The opposition has filed election petitions challenging results in dozens of seats, with real evidence in some. The minister can have the captured courts dismiss them all en masse, or let the genuine cases be heard, risking the loss of a few seats.

left.label: Have the courts dismiss all petitions.
left.tooltip: A result reopened is a result undone.
left.reveal: Your courts throw out every petition, evidence or not. The seats are safe and the message clear — the bench is yours — and the contested results stay forever disputed.

right.label: Let the genuine petitions be heard.
right.tooltip: A seat that survives the court is a seat I truly hold.
right.reveal: You let the courts weigh the real cases. You may lose a few seats to honest rulings, but the ones you keep are unassailable and the judiciary keeps a shred of credibility.

speaker: Cyber Cell Commander
prompt: The cyber cell has produced a convincing deepfake of the opposition leader saying something inflammatory and disqualifying. Released days before the vote, it could end her — until, perhaps, it is debunked, and the forgery is traced back to you.

left.label: Release the deepfake.
left.tooltip: A lie that wins the day outlives its debunking.
left.reveal: You loose the forgery and it rips through the timelines. It may swing the vote before it is exposed, and when it is, the trail back to your cell makes you the story for years.

right.label: Forbid the deepfake.
right.tooltip: A throne won by a forgery rots from the seat up.
right.reveal: You kill the operation. You forgo a devastating dirty trick, but you are spared the day a forensic analyst pins the fabrication on your government for all to see.

speaker: Strategy Director
prompt: A veteran MP from a safe seat has died, triggering a by-poll. His capable but independent-minded daughter wants the nomination on her own merit; the local fixer wants a pliable outsider who will owe you everything. The director needs the nominee.

left.label: Nominate the pliable outsider.
left.tooltip: A grateful nobody serves better than a proud somebody.
left.reveal: You parachute in the controllable outsider. He wins the safe seat and owes you all, and the late MP's daughter — and the constituency that loved her father — turn bitter.

right.label: Nominate the capable daughter.
right.tooltip: Merit and local roots win seats fixers cannot.
right.reveal: You give the daughter the nomination on merit. The fixer loses his pawn and grumbles, but the seat is won warmly and the benches gain a leader rather than a placeholder.

speaker: Digital Government Adviser
prompt: A digital one-stop service portal could let citizens get licences, certificates, and payments online — slashing the petty bribery that infests every counter. The clerks who live on those bribes, a vast and connected workforce, will quietly sabotage it.

left.label: Push the digital service rollout hard.
left.tooltip: A counter with no clerk has no palm to grease.
left.reveal: You force the e-services through against the clerks' sabotage. The petty bribery at a thousand counters withers, citizens save time and money, and you bank a visible, popular win.

right.label: Let the rollout stall.
right.tooltip: Do not pick a fight with a million clerks at once.
right.reveal: You let the digital push die quietly to keep the bureaucracy content. The counters stay manned and the palms stay greased, and the citizen keeps paying a small bribe for every stamp.

speaker: Leader of the House
prompt: Question hour, where ministers answer the house, has decayed into scripted softballs from your own backbenchers. You can revive it as real, unscripted scrutiny — sharpening your ministers but exposing their failings — or keep it the comfortable theatre it is.

left.label: Keep question hour scripted and safe.
left.tooltip: A minister cornered on live television is a wound.
left.reveal: You keep the softballs flying. Your ministers are spared embarrassment, and parliament's central ritual of accountability stays a hollow performance everyone sees through.

right.label: Revive real, unscripted scrutiny.
right.tooltip: A minister who can answer hard questions is a minister worth having.
right.reveal: You restore genuine question hour. Your weaker ministers flounder under real questioning, but the strong shine, the house comes alive, and accountability stops being a charade.

speaker: Labour Wing Secretary
prompt: You can bring the major trade unions under your party's wing — disciplined turnout and industrial muscle — at the price of either delivering on their wage demands or being seen to betray them. The secretary asks whether to court or keep your distance.

left.label: Bring the unions into the party fold.
left.tooltip: Organised labour, organised for me.
left.reveal: You affiliate the big unions and gain disciplined muscle. They now expect delivery on wages and conditions, and the day you cannot, their organised power turns squarely against you.

right.label: Keep the unions at arm's length.
right.tooltip: A union in the tent demands the tent's wealth.
right.reveal: You keep the unions independent. You forgo their organised turnout, but you are not held hostage to wage demands you may not be able to meet.

speaker: Civic Education Director
prompt: A neutral, state-funded voter-education drive would teach citizens their rights, how to spot fraud, and how to vote — strengthening democracy, and arming the very voters most likely to catch your machine cutting corners. The director asks whether to fund it.

left.label: Fund the neutral voter-education drive.
left.tooltip: An informed electorate is a legitimate election.
left.reveal: You fund honest voter education. Turnout and confidence rise, and so does the number of citizens who know exactly what an irregularity looks like — a price you accept for a credible vote.

right.label: Let voter education wither.
right.tooltip: Why teach the voter to catch me?
right.reveal: You quietly defund the programme. The electorate stays hazier on its rights and easier to manage, and the democracy you preside over grows a little thinner and more cynical.

speaker: General Secretary
prompt: Hundreds of your cadres face cases for past political violence — some genuine thugs, some scapegoats. The Secretary wants a blanket amnesty to reward loyalty; the alternative is to let the courts sort the guilty from the wronged.

left.label: Grant the cadres a blanket amnesty.
left.tooltip: I do not abandon the foot-soldiers who carried me.
left.reveal: You wipe the cadres' cases clean en masse. Loyalty is rewarded and the machine reassured, and genuine thugs walk free alongside the wronged, impunity stamped a little deeper.

right.label: Let the courts separate guilty from wronged.
right.tooltip: An amnesty for thugs is a licence for the next.
right.reveal: You refuse the blanket pardon and let cases proceed individually. The cadres feel betrayed and some desert, but the genuinely innocent are cleared and the guilty face the law.

speaker: Overseas Wing Secretary
prompt: If overseas voting is introduced at embassies, the thin oversight abroad makes the ballots ripe for manufacturing. The secretary can quietly pad the diaspora vote at friendly missions, or insist the overseas count be observed as rigorously as any at home.

left.label: Pad the overseas vote quietly.
left.tooltip: Distant ballots are the least watched of all.
left.reveal: You manufacture margins at the friendly embassies. The diaspora numbers swell suspiciously, and the watchdogs add 'phantom overseas votes' to the dossier on the election.

right.label: Observe the overseas count rigorously.
right.tooltip: A vote cast abroad must be as clean as one cast at home.
right.reveal: You insist on full observation of the embassy ballots. You forgo an easy padding, but the diaspora vote becomes a point of pride rather than another asterisk.

speaker: Strategy Director
prompt: In seats where your candidate is weak, the director proposes secretly funding 'independent' spoilers to split the opposition vote. It is cynical and effective, and a leak would expose you as manufacturing fake candidates to game the result.

left.label: Fund the spoiler independents.
left.tooltip: A split rival is a beaten rival.
left.reveal: You bankroll the spoilers and the opposition vote fractures in the target seats. The tactic delivers, and the day a funded 'independent' talks, it confirms every accusation of a rigged field.

right.label: Win the seats honestly or lose them.
right.tooltip: A manufactured candidate is a confession in waiting.
right.reveal: You refuse the spoiler scheme and fight the weak seats straight. You lose some you might have stolen, but no funded fake candidate can ever surface to sink you.

speaker: Constitutional Adviser
prompt: The constitution provides for an ombudsman to investigate official corruption and maladministration — an office every government since independence has conveniently left unfilled. The adviser asks whether to finally appoint a real one, or keep the chair empty.

left.label: Keep the ombudsman's chair empty.
left.tooltip: An office uncreated cannot investigate me.
left.reveal: You leave the post unfilled, as your predecessors did. The constitutional check stays a dead letter, and the corruption it was meant to catch goes on unmolested.

right.label: Appoint a real, empowered ombudsman.
right.tooltip: A check unfilled for fifty years shames us all.
right.reveal: You finally appoint a genuine ombudsman with teeth. Some of your own are caught in its early cases, but you accomplish what no government dared, and the reform is historic.

speaker: Home Affairs Coordinator
prompt: Executive magistrates with mobile courts roam the polls on election day, and the coordinator can deploy them as a partisan instrument — harassing opposition agents, waving through your irregularities — or as genuinely neutral keepers of the peace.

left.label: Deploy the magistrates as a partisan tool.
left.tooltip: Authority on the ground should answer to me.
left.reveal: Your magistrates lean on the opposition and overlook your side's tricks. The polls tilt under colour of law, and the partisan use of the bench becomes one more documented stain.

right.label: Deploy them as neutral peace-keepers.
right.tooltip: A magistrate with a thumb on the scale poisons the whole poll.
right.reveal: You instruct the magistrates to be strictly impartial. They keep order without favour, and the polls run with a fairness your fixers wanted to subvert.

speaker: Electoral Roll Officer
prompt: Tying the voter roll to the national ID database lets you purge 'unverifiable' voters — a clean-up that, applied selectively, can quietly strike opposition-leaning groups (the poor, migrants, minorities) off the list. The officer awaits your aim.

left.label: Purge selectively against opposition groups.
left.tooltip: A roll trimmed of my enemies is a roll improved.
left.reveal: You scrub the list with a partisan hand, and the poor, the migrant, and the minority find themselves voteless. The disenfranchisement is quiet, deniable, and — once mapped — damning.

right.label: Verify the roll fairly and add the missing.
right.tooltip: A purge that targets the poor is a coup with a database.
right.reveal: You clean the roll honestly and run a drive to register the missing. The list loses its phantoms without losing its poor, and the franchise widens rather than narrows.

speaker: Youth Wing Coordinator
prompt: To win back the youth alienated by the quota unrest, the coordinator proposes a vast national youth festival — concerts, job fairs, sports — at considerable cost. The cynics call it a bribe; the optimists call it the only bridge left to a hostile generation.

left.label: Fund the national youth festival.
left.tooltip: A bridge to the young is worth its price in taka.
left.reveal: You bankroll the festival and reach a generation that had written you off. It is costly and some sneer at the gloss, but the outreach softens the youth's hostility a little.

right.label: Skip the festival, save the money.
right.tooltip: A concert does not undo a killing.
right.reveal: You decline the spending as hollow. The treasury is spared, but the alienated youth see no gesture at all, and the generational rift hardens another notch.

speaker: Parliamentary Affairs Minister
prompt: An MP wants to haul a journalist before the house on a 'breach of privilege' for an unflattering report — a quasi-judicial summons that intimidates the whole press gallery. The minister asks whether to back the motion or quash it.

left.label: Back the privilege motion.
left.tooltip: The dignity of the house must be defended.
left.reveal: You let the house summon and censure the journalist. The press gallery gets the message and softens, and parliament adds 'cudgel against reporters' to its repertoire.

right.label: Quash the motion.
right.tooltip: A parliament that bullies the press fears it.
right.reveal: You kill the privilege motion. The thin-skinned MP fumes, but the press keeps its independence and you are spared a needless fight with the entire media.

speaker: Cabinet Secretary
prompt: Several ministers are visibly enriching themselves and their families on the public dime. The secretary proposes a real, enforced code of conduct with asset declarations and resignations for breaches — a clean-up that would gut some of your most loyal grandees.

left.label: Enforce a real ministerial code.
left.tooltip: A cabinet of looters is a scandal with portfolios.
left.reveal: You impose asset declarations and enforce resignations. A few loyal grandees fall and the cabinet trembles, but the public sees, astonished, that office is no longer a licence to loot.

right.label: Leave the ministers to their ways.
right.tooltip: A loyal grandee's loyalty is bought with a free hand.
right.reveal: You decline to bind your ministers. They stay loyal and enriched, and 'one law for them' becomes the defining grievance of your government.

speaker: Chief Whip
prompt: The president is elected by parliament, and a faction of your MPs is flirting with backing a rival candidate to send you a message. The Whip can crush the cross-voting with threats, or let the secret ballot speak and read the result as a warning.

left.label: Crush the cross-voting with threats.
left.tooltip: A secret ballot is no excuse for disloyalty.
left.reveal: You lean hard on the waverers and your candidate wins clean. The dissent is suppressed, but you have learned nothing about the discontent that prompted it, and it festers unspoken.

right.label: Let the secret ballot speak.
right.tooltip: A warning shot heard is a rebellion averted.
right.reveal: You let the MPs vote freely and read the bruised result as the message it is. Your candidate squeaks in, and you act on the discontent before it grows into something you cannot whip.

speaker: General Secretary
prompt: A reshuffle looms, and you can either reward raw loyalty — keeping the faithful incompetents who will never plot — or promote on competence, building a cabinet that governs well but contains ambitious talents who might one day eclipse you.

left.label: Reshuffle for loyalty over competence.
left.tooltip: A loyal fool never sharpens a knife for me.
left.reveal: You keep the faithful and the mediocre in post. The cabinet is safe and pliant, and the country is governed by people chosen for their harmlessness rather than their skill.

right.label: Reshuffle for competence.
right.tooltip: A government that cannot govern is loyal to nothing.
right.reveal: You promote the capable, ambition and all. Governance sharpens and the public notices, and you accept the risk that one of your talents may grow large enough to threaten you.

speaker: Strategy Director
prompt: Doubts about your mandate are corroding your authority. The director floats a bold gamble: a national referendum on your continued rule — a chance to silence the doubters with a fresh popular seal, or a noose if the country says no.

left.label: Call the referendum.
left.tooltip: A fresh mandate silences every whisper.
left.reveal: You put your rule to the people. It is a genuine gamble, and the campaign consumes the country; whatever the result, you have staked your legitimacy on a single roll of the dice.

right.label: Refuse to gamble on a referendum.
right.tooltip: A vote I might lose is a weapon I hand my enemies.
right.reveal: You decline the plebiscite. You keep the doubts unresolved rather than risk a public verdict, and the legitimacy question goes on quietly eating at your authority.

speaker: Strategy Director
prompt: You called it, and the referendum is tainted by a low, suspicious turnout and a result too lopsided to believe. The director asks whether to trumpet the landslide as vindication or admit the obvious and temper your claims.

left.label: Trumpet the landslide as vindication.
left.tooltip: A win is a win; let the doubters choke on it.
left.reveal: You proclaim the implausible result a ringing endorsement. Your base cheers, but no one outside it believes a 98 percent on a 30 percent turnout, and the 'vindication' becomes a punchline.

right.label: Admit the flaws, temper your claims.
right.tooltip: A boast no one believes is worse than silence.
right.reveal: You acknowledge the weak turnout and claim only a modest mandate. The base wanted triumphalism, but the candour spares you the ridicule and salvages a sliver of credibility.

speaker: Strategy Director
prompt: The referendum gambit, whatever its result, has emboldened the opposition to demand a real, internationally-observed election as the only true test. The director asks whether to concede the clean election or dig in behind the referendum's verdict.

left.label: Dig in behind the referendum.
left.tooltip: I have had my plebiscite; I owe them no more.
left.reveal: You refuse the clean election and hide behind the tainted referendum. The opposition's demand hardens into a movement, and your legitimacy gap, papered over, widens beneath the paper.

right.label: Concede a clean, observed election.
right.tooltip: Only a real vote ends a real doubt.
right.reveal: You agree to the genuine, observed election. The hardliners call it surrender, but you gamble that a clean mandate, even a narrow one, is worth more than a hollow one defended forever.

speaker: General Secretary
prompt: A term later, the same fault line tears open again. The opposition will boycott unless you hand the election to a neutral caretaker administration, as the old custom demanded. "Concede the caretaker," the Secretary warns, "and you might lose. Refuse, and you win a vote the world calls a sham."

left.label: Refuse the caretaker, hold the vote under your government.
left.tooltip: A contest I run is a contest I survive.
left.reveal: You keep control of the machinery and win the seats. Turnout is thin, the opposition cries fraud, and your mandate carries an asterisk abroad.

right.label: Concede a neutral caretaker administration.
right.tooltip: A legitimate loss beats an illegitimate win.
right.reveal: You gamble on a clean vote. The world applauds the rare concession; your own machine panics that you have bet the house on the people.

speaker: General Secretary
prompt: Years into your rule, the matter you thought buried resurfaces — harder. Having kept control of the vote, the count in a dozen swing seats is 'adjustable' — the returning officers await a signal. A clean count risks your majority; a managed one cements it but leaves fingerprints.

left.label: Order the count managed in the swing seats.
left.tooltip: A majority is not left to chance.
left.reveal: Your majority is secured and unmistakably engineered. Observers document the irregularities; the result is yours and rotten at the root.

right.label: Let the count stand, whatever it gives.
right.tooltip: A stolen seat is a debt that comes due.
right.reveal: You accept the honest tally and a slimmer majority. Governing is harder; the legitimacy you keep may matter more than the seats you lost.

speaker: Election Commission Liaison
prompt: A new season of power, an old problem with sharper teeth. The terms of the election commissioners are up and you choose their replacements. The liaison presents two lists: pliable loyalists who will see nothing, or respected independents who might rule against you when it counts.

left.label: Appoint the loyalist commissioners.
left.tooltip: An umpire I pick does not call me out.
left.reveal: The Commission becomes an extension of your office. It serves you flawlessly — and the institution's credibility, once spent, will not return when you need it.

right.label: Appoint credible independents.
right.tooltip: A referee everyone trusts protects the winner too.
right.reveal: You stack the body with respected names. They will sometimes rule against you, but the elections they bless will be believed.

speaker: Street Operations Coordinator
prompt: The crisis returns, and this time the cabinet is watching. The opposition has called a week of hartals and transport blockades; petrol bombs are landing on buses and the economy is freezing by the day. The coordinator wants to flood the streets with your own cadres to 'break' the blockade by force.

left.label: Deploy party cadres to break the blockade.
left.tooltip: Meet their streets with ours.
left.reveal: Your cadres clear the roads in running battles. Commerce resumes amid the wreckage, and the cycle of street violence now belongs to you both.

right.label: Keep cadres home, secure transport with police.
right.tooltip: Two mobs are worse than one.
right.reveal: You refuse to militarise your supporters and let the police protect the routes. The blockade drags on and the economy bleeds, but no partisan blood is on your hands.

speaker: Chief Whip
prompt: Once more the issue lands on your desk, the stakes higher than before. Your majority is wobbling; a bloc of independent MPs will cross to your side — for ministries, contracts, and quiet immunity from their pending cases. The Whip says it is the cheapest way to lock the floor of the house.

left.label: Buy the defectors with posts and immunity.
left.tooltip: A secured floor is worth a few favours.
left.reveal: The MPs cross and your majority is iron. The cabinet bloats with bought men, their cases frozen, and the public reads the price tag.

right.label: Refuse to trade immunity for votes.
right.tooltip: I will not staff a government with men I had to bribe.
right.reveal: You decline the deal and govern on a knife-edge. Every vote is now a fight, but no minister sits in your cabinet to dodge a courtroom.

speaker: Religious Front Emissary
prompt: Another year survived, and the reckoning circles back. A powerful religious movement offers its street power and its voters — in exchange for a blasphemy law, control of textbook content, and a quiet end to certain women's-rights reforms. The emissary smiles: "We can fill any square in the country."

left.label: Take the alliance and its demands.
left.tooltip: Their squares are full and my margins are thin.
left.reveal: Their mobilisation is yours now, and so are their conditions. The curriculum narrows, reformers despair, and your secular base feels sold.

right.label: Decline and hold the secular line.
right.tooltip: I will not trade women's rights for a turnout.
right.reveal: You refuse their terms. They can now march against you instead of with you, but the constitution's spine, and your liberal base, hold.

speaker: Tribunal Prosecutor
prompt: The ground has shifted; the same demand arrives with new menace. The tribunal trying the 1971 war criminals is both a genuine reckoning and a useful weapon against today's opposition. The prosecutor asks how far to push — strict evidence and slow justice, or fast verdicts that thrill the base and decimate your rivals.

left.label: Push fast, sweeping verdicts.
left.tooltip: Justice delayed is the guilty escaping.
left.reveal: The convictions land quickly to roaring crowds, and your opposition is gutted. Legal observers question the haste, and the verdicts carry that shadow.

right.label: Insist on slow, airtight due process.
right.tooltip: A reckoning built on shortcuts collapses.
right.reveal: You demand rigorous trials. The base grows impatient and the weapon is blunter, but the verdicts that come will withstand history's scrutiny.

speaker: Student Wing President
prompt: History rhymes: the conflict reopens deeper than last time. Your student wing controls the campuses through guest-room torture, tender extortion, and seat-control of the halls. The president wants more autonomy and more money. A reformist faction inside the party wants the wing reined in before it becomes a scandal.

left.label: Empower the student wing further.
left.tooltip: The campuses are a fortress I cannot lose.
left.reveal: The wing tightens its grip and delivers the campuses. Its abuses grow more brazen, a powder keg awaiting one viral video.

right.label: Rein it in and curb the worst leaders.
right.tooltip: A wing that tortures students will one day burn me.
right.reveal: You discipline the bosses and cut the extortion. The campuses loosen from your grip, but you have defused a bomb under your own house.

speaker: Party Elders' Council
prompt: Mid-reign, the old wound is torn afresh. The grey-haired council that has run the party for decades wants you to anoint your child as heir-apparent now, to 'preserve unity'. The reformers warn that a dynastic coronation will hollow the party of every talent who is not your blood.

left.label: Anoint your child as successor.
left.tooltip: A clear heir prevents a war of knives.
left.reveal: The dynasty is secured and the council placated. Capable non-family leaders drift away, and the party's depth thins to a single bloodline.

right.label: Open the succession to merit.
right.tooltip: A party is not an inheritance.
right.reveal: You refuse to crown an heir and invite a real contest. The family is wounded and the council nervous, but the party keeps its best minds.

speaker: Attorney General
prompt: The pressure you deferred has come due, larger now. A graft case decades old has finally ripened against the opposition leader. Convict her and she is barred from the next election; the bench will rule whichever way you nod. "One judgment," the Attorney General murmurs, "and your only rival watches the vote from a cell."

left.label: Push the conviction and the ban.
left.tooltip: Remove the rival, remove the race.
left.reveal: She is sentenced and disqualified. Her party erupts and the chanceries issue statements, but the ballot now has no name on it but yours.

right.label: Let the case run honestly, ban or no ban.
right.tooltip: A throne with no challenger is a target.
right.reveal: You refuse to script the verdict. She may yet stand against you, but no one can say you jailed your way to power.

speaker: Attorney General
prompt: A later term, and the quarrel has hardened into a test. With the leader jailed, her elderly frame ailing in custody, the prison doctor warns that a death behind your bars would make her a martyr forever. Her family begs for hospital release; your hardliners call it weakness.

left.label: Keep her confined, deny the release.
left.tooltip: A martyr in a cell is safer than a saint set free.
left.reveal: You hold firm and the cell stays locked. Her health worsens in the headlines, and every cough becomes an indictment of you.

right.label: Grant medical release on humane terms.
right.tooltip: I will not let a corpse defeat me.
right.reveal: You let her convalesce under guard at home. The base grumbles that the snake is loose, but you have denied her the one victory captivity offered.

speaker: Telecom Regulator
prompt: The country has changed, but this fault line has not — only worsened. On the eve of the vote the regulator can throttle mobile data nationwide — 'to stop rumours,' officially; to blind the observers and opposition pollwatchers, in truth. A dark net makes a managed count invisible.

left.label: Throttle data through election day.
left.tooltip: What the cameras cannot upload did not happen.
left.reveal: The country goes dark and the count proceeds unwatched. The result is clean-looking and universally disbelieved; the outage itself becomes the story.

right.label: Keep the networks open.
right.tooltip: A vote no one can film is a vote no one believes.
right.reveal: You let the signal flow. Every irregularity is livestreamed, but so is every honest queue, and the result keeps its credibility.

speaker: District Organiser
prompt: It returns, as these things do, with the patience gone. In the marginal districts the organiser proposes the old arithmetic: envelopes of cash, sarees, and tin sheets distributed ward by ward the night before polling. "Hunger votes for whoever fed it last," he shrugs.

left.label: Fund the ward-level handouts.
left.tooltip: An empty stomach has a short memory.
left.reveal: The trucks roll out and the margins firm up. The treasury thins, the habit deepens, and your win smells of the bazaar.

right.label: Ban handouts, run on delivery instead.
right.tooltip: Buy a vote once and you rent it forever.
right.reveal: You forbid the envelopes and campaign on roads and clinics built. The organisers fret over thin margins, but the votes you win, you keep.

speaker: Electoral Roll Officer
prompt: Years on, the matter is no longer private; the press is already circling. The rolls in your strongholds carry tens of thousands of ghosts — the dead, the duplicated, the never-born. The officer can purge them for a credible list, or leave them as a reserve of votes that never argue back.

left.label: Leave the ghost voters on the rolls.
left.tooltip: A loyal voter need not be a living one.
left.reveal: The phantom names stay, ready to be voted. Auditors will find them eventually, and when they do the whole tally is tainted.

right.label: Purge the rolls to a clean count.
right.tooltip: Ghosts vote, but they also haunt.
right.reveal: You scrub the list to the living. Your stronghold margins shrink honestly, and the register can finally survive a recount.

speaker: Nominations Board Chair
prompt: The same knot, retied tighter, lands before you once more. Party tickets for the winnable seats are the most valuable commodity in the country. The chair can auction them to the highest bidders — funding the whole campaign overnight — or award them on grassroots strength and clean records.

left.label: Sell the tickets to the richest bidders.
left.tooltip: A campaign runs on cash, not character.
left.reveal: The war chest fills in a week with businessmen-MPs who bought their seats. They will legislate for their own ledgers, and the grassroots see who was passed over.

right.label: Award tickets on grassroots merit.
right.tooltip: A bought candidate serves the buyer, not the booth.
right.reveal: You hand seats to organisers who earned them. The campaign chest stays lean, but your benches fill with people the wards actually know.

speaker: Media Adviser
prompt: Another monsoon, another return of an argument you cannot end. A loyal tycoon offers to buy up the three biggest private TV channels and run them as your chorus, drowning out the critical press. The adviser calls it 'narrative dominance'; the cost is the last pretence of a free media.

left.label: Bless the takeover of the channels.
left.tooltip: He who owns the screen owns the story.
left.reveal: The airwaves turn to praise overnight. Dissent migrates to the shadows of the internet, and 'press freedom' becomes a foreign talking point against you.

right.label: Decline; let the channels stay independent.
right.tooltip: A choir of yes-men cannot warn me of fire.
right.reveal: You wave the tycoon off. The critical broadcasts sting nightly, but they are also the only honest mirror your government has.

speaker: Cyber Cell Commander
prompt: Deep into the reign, the issue demands a final-sounding answer. Your party's online cell wants a budget to flood the networks with fake personas — smearing critics, manufacturing trends, and burying inconvenient news under coordinated noise. The commander promises 'we win the feed before they wake up.'

left.label: Fund the troll army.
left.tooltip: The first lie of the morning sets the day.
left.reveal: Ten thousand sock-puppets march on command. The discourse rots, journalists are doxxed, and when the operation leaks it confirms every accusation of manipulation.

right.label: Shut the cell down.
right.tooltip: An army of lies eventually turns its guns inward.
right.reveal: You disband the operation. Your opponents own more of the timeline now, but your name is off the next disinformation exposé.

speaker: Coalition Manager
prompt: The reckoning you postponed has matured into a crisis. Your largest junior partner threatens to walk unless it gets a deputy-premiership and three more ministries — a price that would let its leader posture as co-ruler. Lose them and your majority cracks; keep them and you feed a rival under your roof.

left.label: Pay the price and keep the coalition whole.
left.tooltip: A government standing is worth a crowded cabinet.
left.reveal: You hand over the posts and the floor holds. Your partner struts as near-equal, and your own MPs resent the ministries they were promised.

right.label: Call their bluff and let them walk.
right.tooltip: I will not be governed by my own junior.
right.reveal: You refuse, and they storm out. The arithmetic tightens to a knife's edge, but every remaining minister knows you do not bend to blackmail.

speaker: Chief Whip
prompt: A new chapter, the same antagonists, less room to manoeuvre. A no-confidence motion is on the order paper, and a dozen of your backbenchers are wavering. The Whip can break it with a mix of threats, frozen contracts, and midnight visits — or you can win the floor by actually answering the grievance behind the revolt.

left.label: Crush the revolt with the Whip's methods.
left.tooltip: Discipline is taught in the dark.
left.reveal: The waverers fall back into line, cowed. The motion dies, but a dozen MPs now serve you out of fear, and fear is a thin glue.

right.label: Address the grievance, win the vote openly.
right.tooltip: A backbencher heard is a backbencher kept.
right.reveal: You meet the rebels, concede a real reform, and win the floor in daylight. It cost you policy ground, but the loyalty you bought is genuine.

speaker: Constitutional Adviser
prompt: The old fault line widens; this time there is no quiet option. Your jurists can draft an amendment scrapping term limits altogether, clearing the way for indefinite rule. The two-thirds majority is within reach. The adviser notes the obvious: it is also the clearest possible signal that you intend never to leave.

left.label: Abolish term limits.
left.tooltip: Reform takes longer than a term allows.
left.reveal: The amendment passes and the horizon clears. At home it reads as ambition unmasked; abroad, as one more strongman erasing his own exit.

right.label: Leave the limits in place.
right.tooltip: A door I cannot lock behind me keeps me honest.
right.reveal: You let the constraint stand. The dynasty's grip looks less total, but you have spared yourself the label of president-for-life.

speaker: Home Secretary
prompt: Time has not healed it — the conflict comes back armed. You have the legal pretext to ban a troublesome rival party outright — dissolve it, seize its offices, bar its symbol. It would erase a threat in a stroke. It would also tell every other party that the law is just your hammer.

left.label: Ban the rival party.
left.tooltip: A weed pulled does not seed.
left.reveal: The party is dissolved and its cadres scattered or jailed. Your dominance is near-total, and so is the proof that you fear a fair fight.

right.label: Let it stand and beat it at the polls.
right.tooltip: A banned party becomes a legend; a beaten one becomes a footnote.
right.reveal: You leave the rival legal and defeat it in the open. It survives to fight again, but your win carries no asterisk.

speaker: Party Treasurer
prompt: Once again, and with the whole house holding its breath. The industrialist who has bankrolled three of your campaigns now wants his return: a state bank licence, a tax holiday, and a port concession. The treasurer reminds you the next campaign cannot be fought without him.

left.label: Grant the patron his rewards.
left.tooltip: The hand that feeds expects to be kissed.
left.reveal: He gets his licence and his concession. The campaign chest is safe for another cycle, and a single oligarch now owns a wing of your economy.

right.label: Refuse and find cleaner money.
right.tooltip: A party owned by one man has only one voter.
right.reveal: You turn him down and scramble for funding elsewhere. The next campaign will be leaner and harder, but no single patron holds your leash.

speaker: Ward Boss
prompt: The matter resurfaces in a harder year, patience exhausted. Your most effective ward boss runs his neighbourhood through extortion, land-grabbing, and a leash on the local police. He delivers turnout like clockwork. A rival within the party says he is a scandal waiting to detonate.

left.label: Keep the strongman and his turnout.
left.tooltip: A made ward is a won ward.
left.reveal: He keeps delivering, and keeps grabbing. His enemies pile up, and one of his victims will eventually find a camera.

right.label: Cut him loose and clean the ward.
right.tooltip: A boss who owns the booth will one day own me.
right.reveal: You strip him of cover and let the cases proceed. The ward's turnout machine sputters, but a liability has been removed before it blew.

speaker: Women's Wing Chief
prompt: A later season of rule, the stakes now openly existential. The reserved women's seats are yours to fill. The wing chief urges you to seat genuine grassroots women organisers; the fixers want them handed to wives and daughters of male donors as a courtesy. The seats are a signal either way.

left.label: Reward donors' kin with the seats.
left.tooltip: The seats are a thank-you, not a ladder.
left.reveal: Donors' wives take the benches and vote as told. The women's wing seethes, and the reform you boast of abroad rings hollow at home.

right.label: Seat real women organisers.
right.tooltip: A reserved seat should hold a leader, not a placeholder.
right.reveal: You elevate women who built the wards. The donors grumble at the snub, but your benches gain voices that actually represent someone.

speaker: Chief Whip
prompt: It comes around again, and the margin for error is gone. One of your own MPs — popular, principled, and increasingly loud about corruption — is being courted by the opposition. The Whip offers two routes: ruin him pre-emptively with a leaked file, or win him back with a real hearing for his complaints.

left.label: Pre-empt him with a smear file.
left.tooltip: A loud man is silenced before he sings.
left.reveal: The file leaks and his reputation buckles before he can cross. He is neutralised, and every honest MP learns what conscience costs in your party.

right.label: Win him back with a genuine hearing.
right.tooltip: An honest critic kept is worth ten bought loyalists.
right.reveal: You hear him out and act on one grievance. He stays, vocal but yours, and the benches see that integrity has a place after all.

speaker: Rally Coordinator
prompt: The same demand, but the reign is older and the knives sharper. The party's national council is your chance for a million-strong show of force in the capital. The coordinator wants every district to bus in cadres — paid, fed, and housed at the party's cost — to fill the maidan and overawe rivals.

left.label: Stage the million-strong spectacle.
left.tooltip: Power unseen is power doubted.
left.reveal: The maidan overflows and the footage dominates the week. The cost is staggering and the city paralysed, but no one questions your reach.

right.label: Hold a lean, disciplined council instead.
right.tooltip: A crowd I had to rent proves nothing.
right.reveal: You convene a focused, modest gathering. The spectacle is smaller, but the treasury and the city's patience are spared, and the agenda actually gets done.

speaker: Tribunal Prosecutor
prompt: Years deep, the unresolved returns to be resolved for good. A war-criminal's final appeal is exhausted and the gallows wait. Carrying out the sentence on the eve of the election would electrify your base and dare the opposition to defend a collaborator. The prosecutor asks: justice on its own clock, or yours?

left.label: Time the execution for maximum advantage.
left.tooltip: Justice and the calendar can keep step.
left.reveal: The hanging lands days before the vote and the base roars. The cynicism is noted at home and abroad — a reckoning turned into a campaign prop.

right.label: Let the sentence run on the law's own clock.
right.tooltip: A gallows is not a campaign poster.
right.reveal: You let the process conclude when it concludes. The base would have preferred the theatre, but the verdict keeps its dignity intact.

speaker: Culture Minister
prompt: The conflict reignites, and the world is watching this time. A proposal lands to rename the largest airport, a university, and a string of bridges after your family's founding figures. It would stamp the dynasty on the map for generations. Critics call it the cult swallowing the state.

left.label: Approve the renamings.
left.tooltip: A name on the map is a claim on the future.
left.reveal: The signs change across the country. Your lineage is everywhere now — and so is the evidence, for critics, of a personality cult.

right.label: Decline the wholesale renaming.
right.tooltip: A dynasty secure in itself need not shout.
right.reveal: You let the airports keep their names. The family's elders sulk at the modesty, but the state stays larger than your bloodline.

speaker: Polling Agent Coordinator
prompt: A familiar crisis, escalated by everything you let slide. On polling day, your cadres in a contested district can seize the booths in the last hour, eject the rival's agents, and stuff the boxes. The coordinator says it locks twelve seats. It also produces twelve seats no one will believe.

left.label: Order the booth captures.
left.tooltip: The last hour decides the count.
left.reveal: The boxes fill and the seats are yours. Phone videos of ejected agents go viral by nightfall, and the dozen seats become a dozen scandals.

right.label: Forbid it; hold the booths clean.
right.tooltip: A captured booth is a confession on film.
right.reveal: You order the agents to stand down and the count to stand. You may lose some of the twelve, but you keep the only thing a margin is worth — belief.

speaker: Party Elders' Council
prompt: The fault line cracks wider; the decision can no longer wait. The party's gerontocracy clings to every committee chair while a generation of capable forty-somethings stalls beneath them. The elders warn that pushing them out invites chaos; the young warn that keeping them invites irrelevance.

left.label: Keep the old guard in their chairs.
left.tooltip: Loyalty earned over decades is not lightly retired.
left.reveal: The veterans stay and the peace holds. The talented young drift toward NGOs and business, and the party ages with its leaders.

right.label: Promote the next generation now.
right.tooltip: A party that cannot renew itself buries itself.
right.reveal: You move the forty-somethings into real power. The elders are wounded and noisy, but the benches suddenly have a future tense.

speaker: Parliamentary Affairs Minister
prompt: Late in the reign, the old question demands a heavier answer. The Speaker's chair is yours to fill. A partisan Speaker would gag the opposition, ram bills through, and rule every point of order your way. A genuinely neutral one would let the house breathe — and occasionally let your rivals land a blow.

left.label: Install a partisan Speaker.
left.tooltip: The chair should sit on my side of the aisle.
left.reveal: Bills sail through and the opposition is muzzled at every turn. The house becomes a rubber stamp, and parliament's last shred of theatre dies.

right.label: Appoint a credibly neutral Speaker.
right.tooltip: A fair umpire makes my wins count.
right.reveal: The house gets a Speaker both sides respect. Your rivals get their moments, but the chamber's rulings carry weight again.

speaker: Overseas Wing Secretary
prompt: It returns once more — and this round may be the one remembered. Your overseas chapters can funnel large, lightly-documented donations home through hundi channels. The secretary calls it patriotic generosity; the law calls it illegal foreign funding. It would fatten the chest and tie you to money you cannot explain.

left.label: Accept the diaspora hundi money.
left.tooltip: A son abroad still pays his mother's bills.
left.reveal: The funds arrive off the books and the chest swells. Somewhere a paper trail survives, and when it surfaces it will read as foreign money buying your government.

right.label: Refuse the undocumented channels.
right.tooltip: Money I cannot name is a leash I cannot see.
right.reveal: You insist on clean, declared donations only. The chest grows slower, but no foreign-funding exposé waits in your future.

speaker: Minorities Cell Convenor
prompt: The reckoning circles back, grown teeth in the waiting. The Hindu and minority vote has long been a reliable bloc for you against the religious right — but only if you protect it. After a spate of temple attacks, the convenor warns that token sympathy will not hold them; visible protection will, at a cost with the majority bloc.

left.label: Offer words, avoid antagonising the majority.
left.tooltip: A bloc that has nowhere else to go waits patiently.
left.reveal: You issue statements and little else. The minorities feel taken for granted, and the next attack erodes a loyalty you assumed was permanent.

right.label: Protect them visibly, prosecute the attackers.
right.tooltip: A protected vote is a kept vote.
right.reveal: You deploy security and jail the ringleaders. The hardline preachers rage, but the minority bloc rallies to you with real gratitude.

speaker: Strategy Director
prompt: Another term, the same antagonist, the stakes raised again. To make an uncontested vote look competitive, the director proposes fielding 'independent' dummy candidates loyal to you — fake opposition to manufacture the appearance of a real race after the true opposition boycotts.

left.label: Field the dummy candidates.
left.tooltip: An empty stage needs painted rivals.
left.reveal: The ballot fills with your own men pretending to oppose you. Observers see through it instantly, and the charade becomes shorthand for the whole farce.

right.label: Run the real race, boycott and all.
right.tooltip: A staged fight fools no one and shames me.
right.reveal: You let the contest be what it is — thin, but honest. The optics of a low-turnout win are bad, but not as bad as being caught faking your own opposition.

speaker: Cabinet Secretary
prompt: The matter, long deferred, lands with the force of a verdict. The bureaucracy is full of officers who served the last government. The secretary offers a sweeping reshuffle — sideline the suspect, promote the loyal — to make the machine answer to you alone. It would be efficient, and it would gut the service's neutrality.

left.label: Purge and pack the civil service.
left.tooltip: A machine must answer to one hand.
left.reveal: Loyalists rise and capable neutrals rot in OSD limbo. The government does as told, and the institution's professionalism bleeds out with the purged.

right.label: Keep the service neutral and merit-based.
right.tooltip: A bureaucracy I gut once will fail me later.
right.reveal: You promote on competence and let officers serve the office, not the person. Some will drag their feet on your worst ideas — which is rather the point.

speaker: Party Historian
prompt: Years on and unhealed, the conflict returns to claim its answer. The anniversary of the party's founding massacre approaches. The historian wants the commemoration weaponised — every speech aimed at branding today's opposition as the heirs of the killers. It rouses the faithful and poisons the well further.

left.label: Weaponise the commemoration.
left.tooltip: Yesterday's blood is today's mandate.
left.reveal: The speeches land like blows and the base is electrified. The opposition is painted as murderers' heirs, and any hope of national reconciliation dies on the dais.

right.label: Commemorate with dignity, not as a cudgel.
right.tooltip: A grave is not a campaign stop.
right.reveal: You honour the dead without naming the living as villains. The base wanted more fire, but the day keeps its solemnity and you keep the high ground.

speaker: Law Minister
prompt: The same fault line, the harder hour, the narrower path. Two seats on the apex court are vacant and a major case on your government's legality is pending. The minister can elevate two reliable judges in time to hear it — or appoint on seniority and merit and let the case fall where it may.

left.label: Pack the bench before the case.
left.tooltip: A verdict is safest decided before the hearing.
left.reveal: Your judges take their seats and the ruling goes your way. The judgment is sound on paper and stillborn in credibility; the court is now visibly yours.

right.label: Appoint on merit, accept the risk.
right.tooltip: A court I own cannot protect me from the one who owns me next.
right.reveal: You elevate the senior, respected names. They may rule against you, but a judiciary that can say no is the only one whose yes means anything.

speaker: District President
prompt: It comes back sharpened by every season you survived. The grassroots of a key district are in open revolt against their sitting MP — your man — for years of arrogance and graft. They threaten to sit out the campaign. You can back the incumbent and his machine, or back the workers and dump him.

left.label: Stand by the incumbent.
left.tooltip: A sitting MP's machine is not lightly broken.
left.reveal: You back your man and the machine grinds on. The workers feel betrayed and stay home, and the seat that looked safe wobbles.

right.label: Dump the MP, back the grassroots.
right.tooltip: A party that won't hear its base goes deaf.
right.reveal: You drop the incumbent and let the workers pick. He fumes and threatens to bolt, but the district roars back to life around its own choice.

speaker: Establishment Minister
prompt: A later year of power, the old crisis now a true test of it. Tens of thousands of government jobs are about to be advertised. The minister can quietly reserve a large share for party families and youth-wing cadres — buying a generation of loyalty — or run them on open merit and let the chips fall.

left.label: Reserve the jobs for the faithful.
left.tooltip: A job given is a vote owned for life.
left.reveal: Cadres' sons fill the rolls and a generation is bound to you. The merit-passed-over seethe, and the civil service inherits a cohort hired for loyalty, not skill.

right.label: Hire on open merit.
right.tooltip: A government staffed by favours governs like one.
right.reveal: You run a clean recruitment. The youth wing howls at the lost spoils, but you gain a competent intake and the credit for a fair process.

speaker: General Secretary
prompt: The unresolved returns, and this time it will not be deferred. A senior leader who broke with you years ago — taking a faction with him — now seeks to return. He brings votes and organisers, but also ambition and a grudge. The Secretary asks whether to welcome him or let him rot outside.

left.label: Welcome him back with honours.
left.tooltip: A returned rival fights for you, not against.
left.reveal: He rejoins to fanfare, faction and all, and your numbers swell. He also resumes manoeuvring from the inside, an old knife back in the drawer.

right.label: Leave him in the cold.
right.tooltip: A man who left once leaves twice.
right.reveal: You refuse the reunion. His faction stays outside and occasionally bites, but your inner circle is spared a practised intriguer.

speaker: Religious Front Emissary
