prompt: A term later, the same fault line tears open again. The religious bloc you allied with returns for more: the public execution of a blasphemy convict and a ban on a women's rights NGO. The emissary reminds you their street power kept you standing last winter.

left.label: Concede the new demands.
left.tooltip: An ally's price rises with his usefulness.
left.reveal: You give them the ban and the spectacle. The streets stay yours for now, but each concession shifts the country further from the constitution you swore to.

right.label: Refuse and absorb their anger.
right.tooltip: An ally who owns my conscience is my master.
right.reveal: You decline, and they threaten to turn their squares against you. The winter ahead is colder, but you have stopped feeding the thing that would devour you.

speaker: Leader of the House
prompt: Years into your rule, the matter you thought buried resurfaces — harder. With the opposition boycotting parliament entirely, you can ram through a year's worth of contentious bills against empty benches — no debate, no scrutiny. The leader of the house calls it efficient; the press calls it one-party rule by another name.

left.label: Pass the bills against empty benches.
left.tooltip: An absent opposition forfeits its say.
left.reveal: Laws pour through unopposed in days. The efficiency is real and the legitimacy is gone; you have legislated alone and it shows on every statute.

right.label: Slow down, leave room for return.
right.tooltip: Laws made in an empty room echo hollow.
right.reveal: You hold the most divisive bills and leave the door ajar for the opposition's return. Less gets done, but parliament keeps a pulse.

speaker: Election Tech Adviser
prompt: A new season of power, an old problem with sharper teeth. The Commission wants to roll out electronic voting machines nationwide. Your technicians can configure them to be auditable and transparent — or quietly opaque, with no paper trail to contradict the reported count. The opposition already distrusts the boxes.

left.label: Deploy opaque machines, no paper trail.
left.tooltip: A count with no receipt cannot be challenged.
left.reveal: The machines report whatever they report and nothing can check them. The opposition's distrust hardens into certainty, and every result is now disputed by design.

right.label: Insist on an auditable paper trail.
right.tooltip: A vote no one can recount is no vote at all.
right.reveal: Every machine prints a verifiable receipt. The technology stops being a conspiracy and starts being a count people might actually believe.

speaker: Field Security Chief
prompt: The crisis returns, and this time the cabinet is watching. The chief proposes a quiet campaign of pressure on the opposition's polling agents in the marginals — late-night visits, false cases filed, families warned — so their booths go unwatched on the day. No watcher, no challenge.

left.label: Approve the intimidation campaign.
left.tooltip: An unwatched booth is an obedient booth.
left.reveal: The agents melt away and the marginals go unobserved. The pattern is unmistakable to anyone looking, and many are looking.

right.label: Forbid it; let their agents work.
right.tooltip: Fear at the booth poisons the whole well.
right.reveal: You order the pressure stopped. The opposition watches every box, catches you on nothing, and the contest is cleaner than your fixers wanted.

speaker: Chief Whip
prompt: Once more the issue lands on your desk, the stakes higher than before. A charismatic opposition MP — clean, articulate, a future leader on their side — is privately willing to cross to you, for a ministry and a free hand in his district. Poaching him weakens them and strengthens you, but signals that everything is for sale.

left.label: Poach him with a ministry and a free hand.
left.tooltip: Their best man is better as mine.
left.reveal: He crosses to fanfare and the opposition loses a future. He governs his district as a fief, and the message lands: in your house, talent is bought.

right.label: Decline; let him rise where he is.
right.tooltip: A man I had to buy will be bought from me.
right.reveal: You pass on the poach. He stays a thorn across the aisle, but your benches are not staffed by mercenaries, and your district bosses keep their turf.

speaker: Election Commission Liaison
prompt: Another year survived, and the reckoning circles back. The liaison can rig the small things that move the illiterate vote: your symbol first on every ballot, the opposition's buried, your candidates listed at the top. Tiny thumbs on the scale, individually deniable, collectively decisive.

left.label: Stack the ballot in your favour.
left.tooltip: A small edge, multiplied by millions.
left.reveal: Your symbol sits first on every paper and the marginal voter's thumb drifts your way. It is petty and effective, and observers log every tilted ballot.

right.label: Insist on a neutral, randomised ballot.
right.tooltip: A win bought by ballot order is not a win.
right.reveal: You order the symbols randomised and fair. You forgo a quiet edge, but the ballot itself stops being evidence against you.

speaker: City Campaign Manager
prompt: The ground has shifted; the same demand arrives with new menace. The capital's mayoral race is a referendum on your rule, and your candidate is a lacklustre loyalist. The manager can pour state resources into rigging it safe, or you can swap in a genuinely popular outsider who might owe you less.

left.label: Rig the loyalist's win with state muscle.
left.tooltip: The capital cannot fall to a rival.
left.reveal: Your man takes the city hall on a tide of state largesse and managed counts. He governs the capital you can no longer claim was freely won.

right.label: Field a popular outsider instead.
right.tooltip: A mayor the city chose serves the city — and reflects on me.
right.reveal: You back a candidate people actually like. He owes you little and may freelance, but a clean win in the capital buys you legitimacy money cannot.

speaker: Street Operations Coordinator
prompt: History rhymes: the conflict reopens deeper than last time. A renewed blockade has turned lethal — buses torched with passengers inside, a child among the dead. The coordinator wants to answer fire with fire and send your cadres to torch the opposition's offices in return. The cycle would consume the season.

left.label: Retaliate in kind against their offices.
left.tooltip: Blood answers blood, or it never stops.
left.reveal: Your cadres burn back and the season descends into mutual arson. The economy seizes, the morgues fill, and both sides own the ashes.

right.label: Hold your cadres; let the law pursue the arsonists.
right.tooltip: I will not turn a blockade into a civil war.
right.reveal: You refuse to escalate and let the police hunt the bombers. The blockade still bites, but the bloodshed has only one author, and it is not you.

speaker: Party Historian
prompt: Mid-reign, the old wound is torn afresh. A grand mausoleum-museum complex for the party's founder is proposed at vast expense, to be the obligatory pilgrimage of every schoolchild and official. The historian calls it national memory; critics call it the cult cast in marble.

left.label: Build the grand complex.
left.tooltip: A founder's shrine anchors a movement forever.
left.reveal: The marble rises and the pilgrimages begin. The reverence is enforced and total, and the treasury and the country's pluralism both pay for it.

right.label: Fund a modest memorial instead.
right.tooltip: A legacy that needs a palace was never solid.
right.reveal: You approve a dignified, smaller tribute. The family's elders feel slighted, but the funds and the breathing room for other histories survive.

speaker: Labour Wing Secretary
prompt: The pressure you deferred has come due, larger now. Your party's labour wing wants to lead the garment workers' wage agitation — a million votes if you deliver, but a collision with the factory owners who fund you. The secretary asks whose side the party is really on.

left.label: Side with the factory owners.
left.tooltip: The cheques come from the gate, not the floor.
left.reveal: You quietly throttle the wage push to keep the owners happy. The chest stays full, and a million workers learn whose party this really is.

right.label: Lead the workers' wage demand.
right.tooltip: A million floor votes outweigh a hundred gate cheques.
right.reveal: Your wing fronts the agitation and wins a real raise. The owners fume and trim their donations, but the workers' districts are yours for a generation.

speaker: Strategy Director
prompt: A later term, and the quarrel has hardened into a test. A by-election in a swing seat is a live test of your standing. The director can sandbag it with the usual machinery for a safe win that tells you nothing, or let it run clean as an honest barometer — and risk a humiliating loss that emboldens the opposition.

left.label: Sandbag the by-election safe.
left.tooltip: A loss now is a flood later.
left.reveal: You secure the seat the old way and avoid the bad headline. You also blind yourself to how thin your support has worn, governing on a number you faked.

right.label: Let it run clean as a true test.
right.tooltip: Better to read the ground than to pave over it.
right.reveal: You run it honestly. Win or lose, you finally know where you stand — and if you lose, you learn it now, while there is time to act.

speaker: Law Minister
prompt: The country has changed, but this fault line has not — only worsened. A prominent editor has accused your government of corruption in print. The minister can have him hauled up for contempt and sedition, jailing him and chilling the rest — or you can answer the charge with evidence and let him print.

left.label: Jail the editor for contempt.
left.tooltip: An example silences a chorus.
left.reveal: He is jailed and the newsrooms flinch. The corruption story is buried under his arrest, which becomes the bigger, longer-running scandal.

right.label: Rebut him with evidence, leave him free.
right.tooltip: A government that answers fears nothing.
right.reveal: You publish the records that refute him and let him keep printing. The base wanted him jailed, but you have won the argument instead of the arrest.

speaker: Constitutional Adviser
prompt: It returns, as these things do, with the patience gone. Article 70 already lets you expel any MP who votes against the party line, making your benches utterly obedient. The adviser proposes tightening it further, criminalising even abstention. It would end dissent — and end parliament as anything but a chorus.

left.label: Tighten the law against all dissent.
left.tooltip: A whip with teeth needs no second crack.
left.reveal: Abstention becomes career suicide and every vote is unanimous. Your benches are flawless and lifeless, a parliament that cannot think, only obey.

right.label: Loosen it; allow conscience votes.
right.tooltip: An MP who cannot ever say no is not a representative.
right.reveal: You permit dissent on matters of conscience. A few votes get messier, but your benches contain humans again, and the house earns a little respect.

speaker: Coalition Manager
prompt: Years on, the matter is no longer private; the press is already circling. Seat-sharing talks with your allies are deadlocked. You can bully the small partners into accepting scraps — keeping the lion's share for your own candidates — or share generously to keep the alliance genuinely broad and grateful.

left.label: Keep the lion's share for your own.
left.tooltip: An alliance is a pyramid, not a partnership.
left.reveal: Your candidates get the winnable seats and the partners get crumbs. You dominate the alliance and hollow it, leaving allies who campaign at half-effort.

right.label: Share seats generously with allies.
right.tooltip: A partner fed campaigns harder than one starved.
right.reveal: You give the small parties real winnable seats. Your own count is slimmer, but the alliance pulls together and turns out as one.

speaker: Student Wing President
prompt: The same knot, retied tighter, lands before you once more. A youth-wing cadre has died in a clash with the opposition. The president wants a massive politicised funeral procession through the capital to inflame the base and provoke a confrontation that paints the rivals as killers.

left.label: Stage the inflammatory funeral march.
left.tooltip: A martyr's coffin is a recruiting drum.
left.reveal: The procession swells into a riot and the rivals are branded murderers. The base burns hot, the city burns with it, and another coffin is already on its way.

right.label: Hold a solemn, contained funeral.
right.tooltip: I will not turn a boy's grave into a battlefield.
right.reveal: You give the cadre a dignified, guarded farewell. The president wanted a spectacle and a casus belli; you gave him neither, and the city stays calm.

speaker: Foreign Liaison Officer
prompt: Another monsoon, another return of an argument you cannot end. Foreign and domestic election observers have applied for accreditation. You can stall and restrict them — limiting where they go, what they see — to keep the managed parts of the vote out of frame, or accredit them fully and run a vote that can bear watching.

left.label: Restrict and stall the observers.
left.tooltip: Witnesses are only invited to a clean house.
left.reveal: The observers are penned far from the action and issue damning reports anyway. The restrictions themselves become the headline: a government afraid of being watched.

right.label: Accredit observers fully.
right.tooltip: A vote that can be watched is a vote that is believed.
right.reveal: You let the monitors go where they please. It forces discipline on your machinery, and the clean bill of health they give is worth a hundred press releases.

speaker: Anti-Graft Commission Chair
prompt: Deep into the reign, the issue demands a final-sounding answer. The graft watchdog is yours to aim. The chair awaits a target list: pursue only the opposition and your own clean rivals, or let the Commission investigate without fear or favour — including, inevitably, your own ministers.

left.label: Aim the Commission at rivals only.
left.tooltip: An investigator off the leash bites the master.
left.reveal: The watchdog hounds your enemies and ignores your friends. It is devastatingly useful and visibly partisan, and your own grafters learn they are untouchable.

right.label: Let it investigate without favour.
right.tooltip: A watchdog that only bites the neighbour guards nothing.
right.reveal: You unleash it on all comers, your side included. A few ministers fall and the base panics, but the Commission gains teeth the public actually trusts.

speaker: Constitutional Adviser
prompt: The reckoning you postponed has matured into a crisis. You can amend away the caretaker-government provision permanently, ensuring every future election is held under a sitting government — yours, for now. It locks in a structural advantage forever, and locks in the grievance that fuels every boycott.

left.label: Abolish the caretaker system for good.
left.tooltip: Why concede each election what I can settle once?
left.reveal: The amendment passes and the advantage is permanent. So is the opposition's casus belli; every future poll now inherits this original sin.

right.label: Preserve a neutral caretaker mechanism.
right.tooltip: A rule that survives me protects me from my successors.
right.reveal: You keep — or restore — a neutral poll-time authority. You surrender a structural edge, but you drain the poison from every election to come.

speaker: Party Treasurer
prompt: A new chapter, the same antagonists, less room to manoeuvre. Most of the campaign chest is undeclared cash from sources best left unexamined. An election audit looms. The treasurer can launder the trail through shell foundations, or you can declare a clean, smaller chest and fight poorer but legal.

left.label: Launder the chest through shells.
left.tooltip: Money has no smell once it is washed.
left.reveal: The cash is scrubbed and the audit finds nothing — for now. The shells are a time bomb, and the day one bursts it takes a cabinet with it.

right.label: Declare a clean, smaller chest.
right.tooltip: A washed rupee still stains the hand that spent it.
right.reveal: You run on declared money and fight the campaign lean. The shortfall hurts at every rally, but no laundering scandal waits in your second term.

speaker: Party Elders' Council
prompt: The old fault line widens; this time there is no quiet option. The extended family — cousins, in-laws, the founder's grandchildren — all want safe seats this cycle. The council frames it as keeping the bloodline central. The reformers warn the ballot is starting to look like a family reunion.

left.label: Hand safe seats to the family circle.
left.tooltip: Blood holds when everything else bends.
left.reveal: The relatives are slotted into winnable seats. The dynasty's grip tightens, and the photo of the new parliamentary party looks like a wedding album.

right.label: Limit the family to a few, open the rest.
right.tooltip: A party of cousins has no roots.
right.reveal: You cap the relatives and open seats to organisers. The family is wounded and loud, but the benches gain people the wards chose.

speaker: Hill Affairs Convenor
prompt: Time has not healed it — the conflict comes back armed. The hill peoples will back you if you finally implement the long-stalled peace accord — land rights, troop drawdown, autonomy. The settlers and the army want the accord buried. The convenor says you cannot satisfy both.

left.label: Side with the settlers and the army.
left.tooltip: The plains vote outnumbers the hills.
left.reveal: You shelve the accord again. The garrisons and settlers are pleased, and the hills add another broken promise to a decades-long ledger of them.

right.label: Implement the accord for the hill peoples.
right.tooltip: A promise twenty years deferred is a wound.
right.reveal: You move on land rights and a partial drawdown. The army and settlers resist hard, but the hills, for once, see the state keep its word.

speaker: Returning Officer Coordinator
prompt: Once again, and with the whole house holding its breath. The count is in and tighter than hoped. The coordinator can delay the announcement overnight to 'reconcile' the close seats — adjusting where needed — or declare the results as counted, the moment they are ready, blemishes and all.

left.label: Delay to 'reconcile' the close seats.
left.tooltip: A few hours buys a comfortable margin.
left.reveal: The overnight delay yields a tidier result and an obvious one. The gap between the late seats and the rest tells its own story to anyone counting.

right.label: Announce results as counted.
right.tooltip: A count that needs the night to fix was never counted.
right.reveal: You declare on schedule, narrow margins and all. Governing the thinner majority is harder, but the result lands before anyone can call it cooked.

speaker: Personal Physician
prompt: The matter resurfaces in a harder year, patience exhausted. Your own health is failing faster than the public knows. The physician urges you to name and groom a successor now, while you can shape the transition — but doing so makes you a lame duck the moment it is announced, and the vultures will gather.

left.label: Conceal it; rule as though immortal.
left.tooltip: A wounded king shows no blood.
left.reveal: You hide the decline and grip the wheel. The succession stays unsettled, and the day the truth breaks the party will tear itself apart over a vacuum.

right.label: Name and groom a successor now.
right.tooltip: A transition I design beats one fought over my bed.
right.reveal: You begin the handover on your own terms. Your authority ebbs the moment you announce, but the dynasty gains a real chance of surviving you.

speaker: Election Commission Liaison
prompt: A later season of rule, the stakes now openly existential. The opposition demands a recount in a flagship seat your candidate narrowly 'won'. The liaison can refuse on a technicality and let the result stand, or grant a transparent recount — which might overturn the seat and dent your majority.

left.label: Refuse the recount on a technicality.
left.tooltip: A win declared is a win kept.
left.reveal: You deny the recount and the seat stands, disputed forever. The technicality fools no one, and the seat becomes a permanent symbol of the stolen vote.

right.label: Grant a transparent recount.
right.tooltip: A seat that survives a recount is truly mine.
right.reveal: You allow the recount under cameras. You may lose the seat, but the seats you keep are now unassailable, and the gesture buys rare goodwill.

speaker: Information Minister
prompt: It comes around again, and the margin for error is gone. The national-day broadcasts are yours to script. You can rewrite the founding story to centre your family and erase rivals' contributions — cementing the dynasty as the nation's sole author — or tell an inclusive history that shares the credit.

left.label: Rewrite history around the family.
left.tooltip: He who tells the founding owns the nation.
left.reveal: The broadcasts crown your lineage as the sole begetter of the republic. Historians wince, rivals' heirs seethe, and the cult deepens by another generation.

right.label: Tell an inclusive founding story.
right.tooltip: A nation with one father is an orphan of the rest.
right.reveal: You share the credit across the founding generation. The family's mythmakers are disappointed, but the story gains the ring of truth and the nation's buy-in.

speaker: Inspector-General of Police
prompt: The same demand, but the reign is older and the knives sharper. A fresh police intake is being recruited. The IGP can vet candidates for party loyalty — building a force personally bound to you — or recruit on merit and physical standards, building a force bound to the law.

left.label: Recruit for loyalty to you.
left.tooltip: A force that loves me will never aim at me.
left.reveal: Loyalists fill the ranks and the force becomes a praetorian guard. It will protect you faithfully — until the day loyalty to a person costs it the public's last trust.

right.label: Recruit on merit and standards.
right.tooltip: A police that serves the law outlasts the one that serves me.
right.reveal: You insist on a professional intake. The new constables answer to the badge, not your name — less pliable, but a force the public might one day respect.

speaker: Intelligence Chief
prompt: Years deep, the unresolved returns to be resolved for good. Whispers reach you that two senior ministers are quietly sounding out the army and the opposition about a post-you future. The chief offers proof through surveillance — or you can confront them in the open and force their hand.

left.label: Surveil and quietly neutralise them.
left.tooltip: A plot is best killed in the cradle.
left.reveal: You wiretap, gather leverage, and break them with it. The plot dies, but so does trust in your cabinet, where every minister now assumes he too is bugged.

right.label: Confront them openly, demand loyalty.
right.tooltip: Daylight scatters conspirators.
right.reveal: You face them down in front of the cabinet. The gamble forces a public reaffirmation of loyalty, and the cabinet sees a leader who fights in the open, not the shadows.

speaker: General Secretary
prompt: The conflict reignites, and the world is watching this time. A corruption scandal has surfaced that leads, unmistakably, to a member of your own family. The Secretary can bury it with the usual machinery, or you can let an inquiry proceed and accept the wound to the dynasty's image.

left.label: Bury the scandal.
left.tooltip: Blood is not investigated.
left.reveal: The file vanishes and the family is shielded. The cover-up leaks in pieces, and 'one law for them' becomes the opposition's favourite chant.

right.label: Let the inquiry proceed.
right.tooltip: A dynasty above the law is a dynasty without one.
right.reveal: You allow the investigation, family or not. The relative is furious and the household tense, but the public sees, astonished, that the rules might apply to everyone.

speaker: Manifesto Committee Chair
prompt: A familiar crisis, escalated by everything you let slide. The next manifesto is being drafted. The chair can load it with extravagant, undeliverable promises that thrill crowds and win the cycle — or write a sober, costed platform that under-promises and can actually be kept.

left.label: Promise the moon to win the cycle.
left.tooltip: A campaign is won on dreams, not spreadsheets.
left.reveal: The crowds roar at free electricity and a job for every graduate. You win, then spend the term explaining why the moon is still in the sky.

right.label: Write a sober, deliverable manifesto.
right.tooltip: A promise kept is worth ten promises made.
right.reveal: You run on what you can actually do. The rallies are less electric, but every pledge you redeem next term compounds into trust.

speaker: Student Wing President
prompt: The fault line cracks wider; the decision can no longer wait. Your student wing attacked a peaceful protest on campus, and the footage is everywhere. The president wants you to back the cadres and blame 'outside agitators'; the reformers want the attackers disciplined and an apology issued.

left.label: Back the cadres, blame outsiders.
left.tooltip: I do not abandon my own to a mob's verdict.
left.reveal: You defend the indefensible and the lie collapses on contact with the video. The campuses unite against you, and the wing learns it can attack with impunity.

right.label: Discipline the attackers, apologise.
right.tooltip: A wing that beats students is a liability, not an asset.
right.reveal: You suspend the cadres and apologise to the campus. The wing president seethes at the betrayal, but the wider student body, stunned, lowers its guard.

speaker: Elder Statesman
prompt: Late in the reign, the old question demands a heavier answer. Through a respected intermediary, the opposition offers talks: end the boycott, accept your term, in exchange for electoral reforms and the release of jailed activists. The hardliners call it a trap; the statesman calls it the only exit from a deepening crisis.

left.label: Refuse the talks, govern alone.
left.tooltip: I do not negotiate with those I have already beaten.
left.reveal: You spurn the overture and rule unchallenged on paper. The crisis hardens underground, and the legitimacy gap you could have closed widens instead.

right.label: Open talks and trade reforms for peace.
right.tooltip: A rival at the table is not a rival in the street.
right.reveal: You negotiate, free some activists, and concede real reforms. The hardliners howl betrayal, but the country exhales, and your rule gains a legitimacy force never bought it.

speaker: General Secretary
prompt: It returns once more — and this round may be the one remembered. At the upcoming party congress you can rewrite the internal rules to abolish elections for the top posts — appointing the leadership from above forever. The Secretary calls it stability; the cadres would call it the death of the party's last democracy.

left.label: Abolish internal elections.
left.tooltip: A party that votes on its head invites a coup.
left.reveal: The top posts become appointments and dissent within the party dies. You command total control of a machine that no longer argues — or thinks.

right.label: Keep internal elections alive.
right.tooltip: A party that cannot choose its own leaders cannot lead a country.
right.reveal: You preserve the internal vote. It means occasional surprises and rivals you did not pick, but the party stays a living thing with roots that hold.

speaker: General Secretary
prompt: The reckoning circles back, grown teeth in the waiting. With the rules settled, a charismatic provincial leader has won a groundswell of delegates and could challenge your chosen slate on the congress floor. You can disqualify him on a procedural pretext, or let the floor decide.

left.label: Disqualify him on a pretext.
left.tooltip: A challenge unanswered is a challenge avoided.
left.reveal: He is ruled ineligible and the slate passes unopposed. The delegates who backed him go home bitter, and the province cools toward you for a generation.

right.label: Let the floor decide.
right.tooltip: A leader the delegates chose serves stronger than one I imposed.
right.reveal: You let the contest run. He may win a post you did not plan to give, but the congress crackles with real life and the province feels seen.

speaker: Legal Affairs Secretary
prompt: Another term, the same antagonist, the stakes raised again. The secretary proposes blanketing critical journalists and opposition figures with dozens of defamation suits across distant districts — not to win them, but to bury the targets in travel, lawyers, and fear. It is harassment dressed as law.

left.label: Launch the blanket defamation suits.
left.tooltip: A man in court has no time to campaign.
left.reveal: The critics drown in hearings across the country, exhausted and broke. The tactic is transparent, and 'judicial harassment' becomes the verdict on your rule abroad.

right.label: Forbid the lawfare campaign.
right.tooltip: A law used as a club is no longer a law.
right.reveal: You order the suits dropped. Your critics keep their freedom and their venom, but you are spared a reputation as a man who litigates dissent into silence.

speaker: Legal Affairs Secretary
prompt: The matter, long deferred, lands with the force of a verdict. One suit went ahead anyway, filed by an overzealous local leader, and a young reporter now sits in jail awaiting a hearing months away. The case is a national embarrassment. You can let it grind on, or intervene to free her.

left.label: Let the case grind on.
left.tooltip: To intervene is to admit the suit was mine.
left.reveal: You stay out and she stays in. Her detention becomes a cause célèbre, every passing week a fresh indictment of your tolerance for it.

right.label: Intervene to free her.
right.tooltip: A jailed reporter is a wound I can still close.
right.reveal: You quietly secure her release and rein in the local leader. The base mutters about weakness, but the embarrassment ends and a small debt of goodwill is repaid.

speaker: Delimitation Commissioner
prompt: Years on and unhealed, the conflict returns to claim its answer. The constituency boundaries are due for redrawing, and the commissioner can carve them to pack your opponents into a few seats and spread your voters thinly across many — a quiet, permanent advantage few will notice and none can easily prove.

left.label: Gerrymander the boundaries to your advantage.
left.tooltip: A map drawn well wins seats before a vote is cast.
left.reveal: The new map quietly tilts a dozen seats your way for a generation. The psephologists notice and cry foul, but the lines are set and the advantage banked.

right.label: Redraw the boundaries fairly on population.
right.tooltip: A rigged map is a confession printed in ink.
right.reveal: You insist on a neutral, population-based redraw. You forgo a structural edge, but the electoral map stops being one more piece of evidence against you.

speaker: Administrative Reforms Secretary
prompt: The same fault line, the harder hour, the narrower path. Local leaders clamour for their upazilas to be upgraded into full districts — a cascade of new offices, jobs, and contracts to hand out. The secretary calls it development; the treasury calls it a patronage machine paid for by everyone.

left.label: Create the new districts.
left.tooltip: A new district is a thousand new favours to grant.
left.reveal: You carve out the new districts and the patronage flows. Local bosses are delighted, and the bloated administration becomes one more permanent cost the budget never planned for.

right.label: Refuse the administrative inflation.
right.tooltip: I will not buy loyalty by the district.
right.reveal: You decline the upgrades. The disappointed local leaders sulk and some turn cold, but the state is spared a fresh layer of offices built purely to be staffed with friends.

speaker: Relief Distribution Coordinator
prompt: It comes back sharpened by every season you survived. The food-relief and old-age allowance cards are distributed through your MPs, who hand them to loyalists and withhold them from opposition wards. The coordinator can keep the cards as a political weapon, or move to neutral, needs-based distribution.

left.label: Keep the relief cards as patronage.
left.tooltip: A card given is a vote owed.
left.reveal: The cards stay a tool of loyalty, rewarding your wards and starving the rest. The machine hums, and the genuinely hungry in the wrong wards learn that relief here wears a party colour.

right.label: Move to neutral needs-based distribution.
right.tooltip: Hunger does not check a voter card.
right.reveal: You strip the cards from the MPs and route them by need. Your local leaders lose a lever and rage, but the relief reaches the poorest regardless of how they vote.

speaker: City Campaign Manager
prompt: A later year of power, the old crisis now a true test of it. A clutch of city corporation elections looms — second-order contests that nonetheless read as a verdict on your rule. The manager can rig them quietly safe, or let them run clean as a controlled test of your real support in the towns.

left.label: Rig the city elections quietly.
left.tooltip: No verdict against me, however small, can stand.
left.reveal: You secure the cities the old way. The wins are hollow and the pattern noted, and you blind yourself to how the towns truly feel about you.

right.label: Let the city votes run clean.
right.tooltip: A town honestly won tells me where I stand.
right.reveal: You let the corporations vote freely. You lose a few mayoralties, but the honest results give you a true map of your support and a credibility the rigged wins never had.

speaker: Statistics Bureau Director
prompt: The unresolved returns, and this time it will not be deferred. The census underpins everything — seats, funds, quotas — and the director hints the headcounts in key regions could be 'adjusted' to favour your strongholds. Honest numbers might cost you seats and subsidies; cooked ones poison every plan built on them.

left.label: Adjust the census in your favour.
left.tooltip: Numbers serve the state, and I am the state.
left.reveal: You tilt the headcounts toward your strongholds. The seats and funds flow your way, and every policy built on the false figures — schools, clinics, rations — is quietly misallocated for a decade.

right.label: Insist on an honest census.
right.tooltip: A nation that lies to itself in numbers cannot plan.
right.reveal: You demand accurate counts whatever they cost you. Some strongholds shrink on paper and lose seats, but the data the whole state relies on is finally trustworthy.

speaker: Liberation Affairs Secretary
prompt: A term later, the same fault line tears open again. The civil-service quota for descendants of liberation-war veterans — a third of all posts — was the spark of a national uprising. The secretary asks whether to defend it as honouring the founders, or reform it toward merit to defuse the youth's fury.

left.label: Defend the full veterans' quota.
left.tooltip: The children of those who bled deserve the state they built.
left.reveal: You keep the quota intact and honour the founding generation. Your base of veterans' families is loyal, but the merit-starved youth seethe, and the next spark may be larger.

right.label: Reform the quota toward merit.
right.tooltip: A republic staffed by birthright is no republic.
right.reveal: You slash the hereditary quota and open posts to merit. The veterans' lobby feels betrayed, but a generation of frustrated youth sees, astonished, that the state heard them.

speaker: Education Affairs Secretary
prompt: Years into your rule, the matter you thought buried resurfaces — harder. The campus student-union elections have not been held in decades, leaving the halls to your wing's unelected enforcers. Restoring real elections would democratise the campuses — and likely cost your wing its iron grip on them.

left.label: Keep the student elections suspended.
left.tooltip: An unelected wing is a controlled wing.
left.reveal: You leave the campuses to your appointed enforcers. The grip holds, but a generation graduates having never voted, learning democracy as something that happens to other people.

right.label: Restore real student-union elections.
right.tooltip: Democracy unlearned on campus is unlearned for life.
right.reveal: You bring back the campus vote. Your wing may lose its monopoly to independents and rivals, but the campuses come alive and a generation practises the franchise.

speaker: Religious Outreach Coordinator
prompt: A new season of power, an old problem with sharper teeth. A revered spiritual leader with millions of rural followers offers his endorsement — and his flock's votes — in exchange for state patronage for his shrine network and a blind eye to its finances. The coordinator says his word moves whole districts.

left.label: Take the pir's endorsement and his price.
left.tooltip: His word turns out the countryside.
left.reveal: You strike the bargain and his followers swing your way. The shrine network fattens on state favour beyond any audit, and you have rented a bloc you do not control.

right.label: Decline to trade patronage for a flock.
right.tooltip: A vote bought through a pir is a vote I never truly hold.
right.reveal: You pass on the deal. The rural bloc stays unmobilised for you and the pir cools, but you owe no shadowy shrine-empire a debt it will one day call in.

speaker: Labour Wing Organiser
prompt: The crisis returns, and this time the cabinet is watching. The tea-garden workers — a captive, impoverished bloc bonded to the estates for generations — could be yours if you championed their derisory daily wage. But the estate owners are old donors who deliver the gardens' votes wholesale, their way.

left.label: Keep the estate owners onside.
left.tooltip: The gardens vote as the owners say.
left.reveal: You leave the bonded wage untouched to keep the owners happy. The estates deliver their votes as instructed, and a quarter-million workers stay trapped at a wage from another century.

right.label: Champion the tea workers' wage.
right.tooltip: A bonded vote is no foundation; a freed one is.
right.reveal: You back a real wage rise for the garden workers. The owner-donors retaliate with shut wallets, but the workers, stunned to be seen, become a bloc loyal to you and not the estate.

speaker: Electoral Reform Adviser
prompt: Once more the issue lands on your desk, the stakes higher than before. Reformers and small parties demand proportional representation to end the winner-takes-all distortions that hand you huge majorities on minority votes. The adviser notes PR is fairer — and would shrink your dominance to its true, modest size.

left.label: Keep the first-past-the-post system.
left.tooltip: Why trade a landslide for a coalition?
left.reveal: You preserve the system that magnifies your seats. The majorities stay commanding and the small parties shut out, and the gap between your seat count and your vote share stays a standing grievance.

right.label: Move toward proportional representation.
right.tooltip: A parliament that mirrors the vote is the only honest one.
right.reveal: You back a shift toward PR. Your dominance shrinks to its real proportion and coalition-building becomes the norm, but parliament finally reflects how the country actually voted.

speaker: General Secretary
prompt: Another year survived, and the reckoning circles back. A heavyweight regional leader threatens to break away and form a rival party, taking a chunk of your organisation with him. The Secretary can crush him pre-emptively with expulsions and cases, or accommodate his demands to keep the tent whole.

left.label: Crush him before he can split.
left.tooltip: A breakaway strangled in the cradle never breathes.
left.reveal: You expel him and bury him in cases before he can organise. The split is averted by force, and every ambitious leader learns the cost of independent thought.

right.label: Accommodate him to keep unity.
right.tooltip: A tent that loses a pole sags everywhere.
right.reveal: You meet his demands and keep him inside. Unity holds, but you have shown that a credible threat to leave is the surest way to win concessions from you.

speaker: Coalition Manager
prompt: The ground has shifted; the same demand arrives with new menace. A small but disciplined leftist party offers an alliance — useful street muscle and intellectual credibility — but its price is a swing left on labour, land, and privatisation that would spook your business donors. The manager weighs muscle against money.

left.label: Ally with the left, swing on policy.
left.tooltip: Their cadres and credibility are worth a tilt.
left.reveal: You bring the left in and shift your platform toward labour and land. The donors grumble and some drift, but you gain disciplined organisers and a sheen of conviction.

right.label: Keep the donors, decline the left.
right.tooltip: Ideas do not fund campaigns; businessmen do.
right.reveal: You pass on the alliance to keep your business backers content. The money stays, but you forgo the street muscle and the credibility, and the left turns its energy against you.

speaker: Tribunal Prosecutor
prompt: History rhymes: the conflict reopens deeper than last time. You can move to ban outright the religious party whose leaders collaborated in the 1971 atrocities — a genuine reckoning that also conveniently removes an opposition ally. The prosecutor notes the line between justice and political weapon is thin here.

left.label: Ban the collaborators' party.
left.tooltip: A party born of atrocity forfeits its place.
left.reveal: You dissolve the party and its symbol. The base roars at the reckoning, and the opposition loses an ally — but critics note how neatly justice served your politics, and the banned go underground.

right.label: Let it stand and defeat it politically.
right.tooltip: A martyr-party banned outlives a beaten one.
right.reveal: You leave the party legal and fight it at the ballot. It survives to needle you, but you deny it the banner of victimhood and keep the moral high ground over a real reckoning.

speaker: Rural Development Coordinator
prompt: Mid-reign, the old wound is torn afresh. The rural food-for-work and infrastructure funds are channelled through MPs who skim them and steer the projects to their own villages. The coordinator can keep the lucrative system or route the funds through transparent local government instead.

left.label: Keep the funds in the MPs' hands.
left.tooltip: An MP without projects to give is an MP without friends.
left.reveal: You leave the rural funds with your MPs. They build where it buys loyalty and skim the rest, and the genuinely needy villages with the wrong MP go without.

right.label: Route funds through local government.
right.tooltip: Roads should follow need, not the member's whim.
right.reveal: You hand the funds to elected local bodies with audits. The MPs lose a prized lever and resent it, but the works spread by need and the skim shrinks.

speaker: Religious Affairs Secretary
prompt: The pressure you deferred has come due, larger now. The state can fund imam allowances and pack the mosque management committees with your loyalists — turning the Friday pulpit into a quiet ally — or keep the mosques independent of the party's reach. The secretary says the pulpit moves more votes than any rally.

left.label: Capture the mosque committees.
left.tooltip: He who befriends the pulpit befriends the faithful.
left.reveal: You fund the imams and stack the committees with loyalists. The Friday sermons soften toward you, and the mosques become one more institution bent to the party's will.

right.label: Keep the mosques independent.
right.tooltip: A pulpit I own is a pulpit no one believes.
right.reveal: You leave the mosque committees free. You forgo the pulpit's quiet endorsement, but the faith keeps a distance from the party that lends its eventual support real weight.

speaker: Law Minister
prompt: A later term, and the quarrel has hardened into a test. The opposition's new rally chant mocks you savagely, and the minister can charge its authors with sedition — jailing them and chilling the streets — or laugh it off and let a slogan be a slogan.

left.label: Charge the chant's authors with sedition.
left.tooltip: Mockery, unanswered, becomes contempt.
left.reveal: You jail the sloganeers and the chant becomes infamous, sung at every rally now in defiance. The heavy hand turns a jingle into a movement and you into its punchline.

right.label: Laugh the slogan off.
right.tooltip: A leader who jails a chant has already lost to it.
right.reveal: You wave the mockery away with a joke of your own. The chant loses its charge for want of a martyr, and you look, for once, bigger than your critics.

speaker: Justice Reform Adviser
prompt: The country has changed, but this fault line has not — only worsened. Pressure mounts for a truth commission into the abuses of the previous government — a genuine reckoning that would also, inconveniently, set a precedent for examining yours one day. The adviser asks how far to let it look.

left.label: Launch a far-reaching truth commission.
left.tooltip: A nation that cannot face its past repeats it.
left.reveal: You establish a real commission with teeth. The past regime's crimes are aired and victims heard, and you accept that the same mirror may one day be turned on you.

right.label: Keep any inquiry narrow and controlled.
right.tooltip: A precedent for digging up the dead is a shovel for my own grave.
right.reveal: You permit only a toothless, bounded review. The reckoning is stage-managed, the precedent contained, and the victims learn the truth was never really on offer.

speaker: Election Commission Liaison
prompt: It returns, as these things do, with the patience gone. A proposed law would force every party and candidate to disclose donors and spending — sunlight that cleans the system and exposes your own opaque, oversized war chest. The liaison asks whether to back it or quietly bury it.

left.label: Bury the disclosure law.
left.tooltip: A war chest examined is a war chest disarmed.
left.reveal: You quietly kill the bill. Your funding stays in the dark, and so does everyone's, and the suspicion that elections here are simply bought hardens into common knowledge.

right.label: Back full campaign-finance disclosure.
right.tooltip: Money in daylight buys less than money in shadow.
right.reveal: You support the sunlight law. Your own opaque chest faces scrutiny, but the system gains a check it never had, and you can claim a reform your rivals resisted.

speaker: Strategy Director
prompt: Years on, the matter is no longer private; the press is already circling. Reformers propose lowering the voting age to capture the vast, restless youth cohort. The director's polling says the young lean against you after the quota unrest — so enfranchising them is democratic, and electorally dangerous.

left.label: Lower the voting age.
left.tooltip: A franchise widened is a democracy deepened.
left.reveal: You enfranchise the youth despite their coolness toward you. It wins democratic credit at home and abroad, and you have handed the vote to a generation that may use it against you.

right.label: Keep the voting age as it is.
right.tooltip: I will not arm a cohort that means to vote me out.
right.reveal: You decline the reform. The status quo protects you from a hostile bloc, and the young add 'denied the vote' to a lengthening list of grievances against you.

speaker: Parliamentary Affairs Minister
prompt: The same knot, retied tighter, lands before you once more. With a real opposition finally in parliament, you can either grant its leader the traditional status, perks, and consultative role — a sign of a maturing democracy — or strip the position bare to keep your rival small and dependent.

left.label: Strip the opposition leader's role bare.
left.tooltip: A rival with a chair is a rival with a stage.
left.reveal: You hollow out the opposition leadership's perks and powers. Your rival is diminished, and parliament drifts further from the adversarial democracy it pretends to be.

right.label: Grant the opposition its proper status.
right.tooltip: A loyal opposition is the proof a democracy works.
right.reveal: You afford your rival the traditional standing and consultation. It legitimises an adversary, but it also legitimises the whole house, and the maturity is noticed abroad.

speaker: Reconciliation Adviser
prompt: Another monsoon, another return of an argument you cannot end. The anniversary of a dark episode your party once perpetrated approaches. The adviser offers two paths: a genuine acknowledgment and apology that could begin to heal an old wound, or the usual silence that lets sleeping guilt lie.

left.label: Acknowledge and apologise.
left.tooltip: A wound named can finally close.
left.reveal: You offer a genuine acknowledgment of the old wrong. The base is uneasy and hardliners furious, but victims long ignored feel seen, and a festering grievance begins, at last, to heal.

right.label: Keep the customary silence.
right.tooltip: An apology is an admission, and admissions are weapons.
right.reveal: You let the anniversary pass unmarked. The party is spared an admission, and the unhealed wound goes on poisoning the well between you and those who remember.

speaker: Election Logistics Officer
prompt: Deep into the reign, the issue demands a final-sounding answer. In the far chars, haors, and hills, reaching every voter with a polling station is costly and slow — and the officer notes those remote areas lean against you. He can fund full access for all, or quietly let the awkward, hostile pockets go under-served.

left.label: Under-serve the hostile remote pockets.
left.tooltip: Why spend a fortune to be voted against?
left.reveal: You quietly thin the polling stations in the remote, unfriendly areas. Turnout there collapses for want of access, and the disenfranchisement, once noticed, becomes a quiet scandal.

right.label: Fund full access for every voter.
right.tooltip: A citizen unreachable by the ballot is a citizen abandoned.
right.reveal: You pay to reach every char and hill with a polling station. It costs dearly and may not help your numbers, but no Bangladeshi is denied the vote by geography on your watch.

speaker: Constitutional Adviser
prompt: The reckoning you postponed has matured into a crisis. A proposal floats to create an appointed upper house — billed as a chamber of elders and expertise, but in practice a soft landing for loyalists and a brake on any future reformist lower house. The adviser asks whether to build it.

left.label: Create the appointed upper house.
left.tooltip: A second chamber of friends is a second line of defence.
left.reveal: You establish the upper house and pack it with loyalists. It rewards the faithful and checks any unruly future majority, and critics rightly call it a parking lot for cronies.

right.label: Reject the appointed chamber.
right.tooltip: A house of cronies is not a check; it is a couch.
right.reveal: You decline to build a chamber of appointees. You forgo a tool of patronage and control, but you avoid bolting a permanent crony-park onto the constitution.

speaker: Home Affairs Coordinator
prompt: A new chapter, the same antagonists, less room to manoeuvre. Election-day clashes — some sparked by your own cadres — have left dozens dead and hundreds injured across the country. The coordinator asks whether to pay full, public compensation and investigate, or quietly pay off a few and move on.

left.label: Pay full compensation and investigate.
left.tooltip: Blood spilled at the ballot is the state's debt.
left.reveal: You compensate the victims openly and order real investigations, your own cadres included. The party is uneasy at the scrutiny, but the dead are honoured and the violence's authors face daylight.

right.label: Quietly pay off a few, move on.
right.tooltip: A long inquiry only keeps the bodies in the news.
right.reveal: You settle a handful of cases discreetly and let the rest fade. The headlines pass, the cadres learn there is no reckoning, and the families of the unacknowledged dead nurse a lasting grievance.

speaker: Campaign Data Director
prompt: The old fault line widens; this time there is no quiet option. A slick foreign consultancy offers to harvest the population's data and micro-target voters with tailored manipulation — the dark arts that have swung elections elsewhere. The director is dazzled; the catch is a scandal if the data harvesting ever leaks.

left.label: Hire the data-manipulation consultants.
left.tooltip: The other side will, if I do not.
left.reveal: You bring in the dark-arts firm and the targeting sharpens uncannily. It works — until the harvesting leaks, and your campaign becomes the local chapter of a global data-abuse scandal.

right.label: Run an honest, homegrown campaign.
right.tooltip: An election won by manipulation is a mandate built on sand.
right.reveal: You decline the manipulation machine and campaign on message and organisation. It is harder and slower, but no harvesting time-bomb sits ticking under your win.

speaker: Returning Officer Coordinator
prompt: Time has not healed it — the conflict comes back armed. On scrutiny day, returning officers can reject opposition candidates' nomination papers on hyper-technical grounds — a misplaced signature, a loan footnote — thinning the ballot before a vote is cast. The coordinator awaits the word on how aggressive to be.

left.label: Reject rivals' papers on technicalities.
left.tooltip: A candidate disqualified is a contest avoided.
left.reveal: Dozens of opposition hopefuls are struck off on trivia. Your candidates walk into emptier races, and the courts and observers log another way the contest was hollowed before polling.

right.label: Let valid nominations stand.
right.tooltip: A ballot thinned by clerks is a ballot no one trusts.
right.reveal: You order the officers to accept all proper nominations. The races are fuller and harder, but the contest cannot be dismissed as rigged at the starting line.

speaker: Strategy Director
prompt: Once again, and with the whole house holding its breath. Your polling is briefly riding high after a popular giveaway, and the director urges a snap election to lock in a fresh term before the economy sours. It is shrewd and opportunistic; it also reeks of cutting and running before the bill arrives.

left.label: Call the snap election now.
left.tooltip: Strike while the numbers smile.
left.reveal: You go to the polls on the sugar high and win comfortably. The fresh term is banked, and the soured economy and unpaid giveaway bill land squarely in your own new lap.

right.label: Serve the full term and face the music.
right.tooltip: A leader who runs from the bill admits he padded it.
right.reveal: You hold the scheduled date and govern through the hard patch. You forgo the easy win, but you cannot be accused of cynically timing the vote to dodge the consequences.

speaker: Chief Whip
prompt: The matter resurfaces in a harder year, patience exhausted. A respected backbencher has voted his conscience against a flagship bill, and the constitution lets you expel him from parliament outright for crossing the line. The Whip wants him made an example; the alternative is to let one dissent pass.

left.label: Expel the dissenting MP.
left.tooltip: One conscience indulged becomes a chorus.
left.reveal: You strip him of his seat for a single honest vote. The benches fall silent and obedient, and the spectacle of a man expelled for conscience stains the parliament's name.

right.label: Let the single dissent pass.
right.tooltip: A parliament of automatons warns me of nothing.
right.reveal: You let the conscience vote stand without punishment. The hardliners fret about the precedent, but the benches keep a flicker of humanity and the house a shred of dignity.

speaker: Party Elders' Council
prompt: A later season of rule, the stakes now openly existential. The ceremonial presidency is yours to fill. The council urges a pliant loyalist who will sign whatever you send and never use the office's reserve powers. A respected independent would lend the state dignity — and might, in a crisis, act on his own judgment.

left.label: Install a pliant loyalist as president.
left.tooltip: A rubber stamp does not develop a spine at midnight.
left.reveal: You elevate a reliable nonentity to the presidency. Every decree is signed on cue, and the one office that might restrain you in a crisis is neutered in advance.

right.label: Appoint a respected independent.
right.tooltip: A dignified head of state reflects on the whole house.
right.reveal: You choose a credible figure for the presidency. He lends the state gravity, and you accept that in a true crisis he might use the office's powers as his own conscience, not yours, dictates.

speaker: General Secretary
prompt: It comes around again, and the margin for error is gone. A small but well-organised regional party is open to merging into yours — instant reach in a region where you are weak, at the cost of absorbing its leaders, its baggage, and its demands for safe seats and posts.

left.label: Absorb the smaller party.
left.tooltip: Their region becomes mine overnight.
left.reveal: You merge them in and your map fills out in the weak region. Their leaders crowd your benches with new demands and old baggage, and your once-tight organisation grows lumpier.

right.label: Stay independent, build the region yourself.
right.tooltip: A merger buys a region and inherits its quarrels.
right.reveal: You decline the merger and invest in building your own base there. It is slower and the region stays thin for you, but your party keeps its coherence and owes no newcomers.

speaker: Manifesto Committee Chair
prompt: The same demand, but the reign is older and the knives sharper. A draft of the manifesto has leaked, exposing an unpopular plan to cut a beloved subsidy that you meant to bury in the fine print. The chair asks whether to brazen it out and keep the plan, or disown the draft and reverse course.

left.label: Stand by the leaked plan.
left.tooltip: A policy abandoned at the first leak was never a policy.
left.reveal: You defend the subsidy cut openly. The honesty earns grudging respect in some quarters, but the beloved subsidy's defenders mobilise and the issue dogs the whole campaign.

right.label: Disown the draft and reverse.
right.tooltip: A leak is a chance to hear the room.
right.reveal: You bin the unpopular plan and recast the draft. The flip-flop draws mockery, but the beloved subsidy survives and the campaign's biggest landmine is defused.

speaker: Field Mobilisation Officer
prompt: Years deep, the unresolved returns to be resolved for good. Your machine can lay on free transport to ferry your voters to the booths on polling day — legal turnout muscle in your strongholds — while quietly ensuring the buses never reach the opposition's wards. The officer asks how clean to keep it.

left.label: Run partisan transport to your wards only.
left.tooltip: I move my voters; theirs can walk.
left.reveal: Your strongholds turn out in force on free buses while the opposition's wards wait for transport that never comes. The lopsided turnout flatters your win and fools no observer.

right.label: Provide transport neutrally or not at all.
right.tooltip: A bus that only carries my voters is a thumb on the scale.
right.reveal: You either drop the scheme or run it for all. Your raw turnout edge shrinks, but the poll-day logistics give your enemies nothing to point at.

speaker: Chief Whip
prompt: The conflict reignites, and the world is watching this time. Your new majority is wafer-thin and a confidence vote looms in the first week. The Whip can secure it by buying a few independents with quiet promises, or you can win it on your genuine numbers and govern honestly from a fragile base.

left.label: Buy the independents for a safe margin.
left.tooltip: A government that wins its first vote narrowly never recovers.
left.reveal: You purchase the cushion and win comfortably. The bought independents will be back for more all term, and your government is mortgaged to them from its first day.

right.label: Win on your real numbers.
right.tooltip: A majority I had to buy is a majority I do not have.
right.reveal: You take the confidence vote on your honest count and scrape through. Governing the knife-edge is exhausting, but you owe no shadowy independents and the mandate is real.

speaker: Cabinet Secretary
prompt: A familiar crisis, escalated by everything you let slide. Amid unrest, the secretary lays out the nuclear option: declare a state of emergency, suspend rights, rule by decree, and postpone the election. It would give you total control — and brand you, at home and abroad, as a ruler who tore up the rules to keep power.

left.label: Declare the state of emergency.
left.tooltip: Order first; legitimacy can be rebuilt.
left.reveal: You suspend rights and rule by decree. The streets fall silent under the clampdown, and the word 'dictator' attaches itself to you in every capital and every history book.

right.label: Govern through the crisis within the law.
right.tooltip: A leader who suspends the constitution has already abdicated.
right.reveal: You refuse the emergency and ride out the unrest within constitutional bounds. It is harder and more dangerous, but you remain a head of government, not a usurper.

speaker: Professional Wing Coordinator
prompt: The fault line cracks wider; the decision can no longer wait. The bar association, medical council, and engineers' bodies hold prestigious elections that shape professional opinion. The coordinator wants to capture them with party panels and patronage; the alternative is to let the professionals govern themselves.

left.label: Capture the professional bodies.
left.tooltip: The professions sway the educated; capture them.
left.reveal: Your panels sweep the professional bodies on patronage and pressure. The lawyers, doctors, and engineers' associations become party annexes, and their independent voice — and credibility — dies.

right.label: Leave the professions self-governing.
right.tooltip: A captured bar defends no one, least of all me.
right.reveal: You let the professional bodies run free. They will sometimes pass resolutions against you, but their independence keeps them respected, and a free bar is a check worth having.

speaker: Constitutional Adviser
prompt: Late in the reign, the old question demands a heavier answer. If a neutral poll-time authority is to oversee the election, its chief would likely be the most recently retired chief justice. The adviser notes you could engineer the retirement timeline so that a pliable judge sits in that chair when it matters.

left.label: Engineer a pliable poll-time chief.
left.tooltip: Even a neutral office should hold a friendly face.
left.reveal: You quietly time the judicial retirements so your man heads the poll-time authority. The 'neutral' oversight is neutral in name only, and the opposition smells it instantly.

right.label: Let the chair fall where it falls.
right.tooltip: A poll umpire I picked is no umpire at all.
right.reveal: You leave the succession to run its natural course, friendly or not. You surrender a hidden lever, but the poll-time authority is genuinely impartial and its verdict will be believed.

speaker: Media Adviser
prompt: It returns once more — and this round may be the one remembered. A beloved film and cricket star will endorse you — and stand for a safe seat — bringing glamour and youth appeal. The adviser is thrilled; the cynics note he knows nothing of governance and would be a hollow, gaffe-prone MP.

left.label: Field the celebrity candidate.
left.tooltip: Stardust wins seats that policy cannot.
left.reveal: The star joins to adoring crowds and wins his seat on charisma. He is a magnet for the young and a liability in the chamber, every gaffe a headline, every absence noted.

right.label: Use his endorsement, not his candidacy.
right.tooltip: A famous face is an asset; a famous fool is a cost.
right.reveal: You take the star's public backing but steer him away from a seat. You bank the glamour without saddling the benches with a celebrity who cannot legislate.

speaker: Coalition Manager
prompt: The reckoning circles back, grown teeth in the waiting. Your alliance must agree a common minimum programme, and the partners' wish-lists pull in opposite directions — secular reformers against the religious bloc, labour against business. The manager asks whether to paper over the cracks or force a coherent platform.

left.label: Paper over the contradictions.
left.tooltip: A vague platform offends no partner.
left.reveal: You fudge the programme into mush that everyone can sign. The alliance holds for the campaign, then tears itself apart in government over the contradictions you never resolved.

right.label: Force a coherent common programme.
right.tooltip: A coalition that cannot agree a page cannot govern a country.
right.reveal: You hammer out a real, consistent platform, losing a partner or two who cannot accept it. The alliance is smaller but governable, and voters know what it actually stands for.

speaker: Reconciliation Adviser
prompt: Another term, the same antagonist, the stakes raised again. A party banned years ago — its grievance now a rallying cry — petitions to be restored to legal politics, promising to renounce violence. The adviser says readmitting it drains the poison of exclusion; the hardliners say it legitimises old enemies.

left.label: Keep the party banned.
left.tooltip: A banned enemy is a contained enemy.
left.reveal: You refuse restoration. The party stays underground, its exclusion a permanent grievance and recruiting tool, and the politics of the banned festers beyond the ballot's reach.

right.label: Restore it to legal politics.
right.tooltip: A grievance let into the chamber is a grievance out of the streets.
right.reveal: You readmit the party on conditions. The hardliners howl about legitimising enemies, but an underground grievance becomes a contestable political force, answerable to voters.

speaker: Field Mobilisation Officer
prompt: The matter, long deferred, lands with the force of a verdict. Turnout is your friend where you are strong and your enemy where you are weak. The officer can run a blanket national get-out-the-vote drive that lifts everyone's turnout, or a surgical one that wakes your wards and lets the opposition's sleep.

left.label: Run the surgical, partisan GOTV.
left.tooltip: I wake my voters and let theirs dream.
left.reveal: Your wards turn out heavily while the opposition's stay home, unprodded. The lopsided turnout pads your margins, and the selective mobilisation is one more managed feature of the result.

right.label: Run a blanket national turnout drive.
right.tooltip: A high honest turnout is a mandate money cannot buy.
right.reveal: You lift turnout everywhere, friend and foe alike. You forgo a turnout edge, but a high, genuine participation gives whatever result emerges a legitimacy nothing else can.

speaker: Local Government Secretary
prompt: Years on and unhealed, the conflict returns to claim its answer. Real devolution — money and power pushed down to elected local councils — would transform governance and accountability, but it would also loosen your central grip and empower local leaders, some of them rivals. The secretary asks how far to let go.

left.label: Keep power centralised.
left.tooltip: A capital that lets go of the purse lets go of the country.
left.reveal: You keep the money and decisions in the centre's fist. Your grip stays tight, and local government remains a hollow shell where accountability and initiative go to die.

right.label: Devolve real power and funds.
right.tooltip: A nation governed only from the capital is barely governed.
right.reveal: You push genuine money and authority down to elected councils. You loosen your grip and empower some rivals, but governance gets closer to the people and visibly better.

speaker: Culture Minister
prompt: The same fault line, the harder hour, the narrower path. A proposed law would make 'disrespect' of the flag and anthem a crime — wrapping you in patriotism and handing you a flexible cudgel against critics who could be charged for a gesture or a parody. The minister calls it honouring the nation.

left.label: Enact the flag-and-anthem law.
left.tooltip: The symbols of the nation are above mockery.
left.reveal: You criminalise disrespect of the national symbols. Patriots applaud, and the elastic offence becomes a convenient charge to level at any critic who steps out of line.

right.label: Leave patriotism uncompelled.
right.tooltip: Forced reverence is no reverence at all.
right.reveal: You reject the law. The hardliners grumble that you failed to honour the nation, but you decline a cudgel that would have dressed censorship as patriotism.

speaker: Election Tech Adviser
prompt: It comes back sharpened by every season you survived. A new app would transmit every polling station's count straight to a public dashboard in real time — radical transparency that makes tampering nearly impossible, and equally makes any managed result impossible to manage. The adviser awaits your verdict.

left.label: Deploy the public real-time results app.
left.tooltip: A count the whole country watches is a count it believes.
left.reveal: You launch the transparent dashboard. Tampering becomes near-impossible and the result, whatever it is, is trusted — and you have closed off your own machine's options for good.

right.label: Keep results flowing through official channels.
right.tooltip: A dashboard that cannot be managed is a risk I need not take.
right.reveal: You stick with the slower official channels. The flexibility to 'reconcile' close seats survives, and so does the public's weary assumption that the count is never quite clean.

speaker: Education Affairs Secretary
prompt: A later year of power, the old crisis now a true test of it. The vast network of independent religious schools wants its degrees recognised as equal to university qualifications for government jobs — a huge bloc's long-held demand. The secretary says granting it wins the bloc; the secular establishment is appalled.

left.label: Recognise the religious degrees.
left.tooltip: A million graduates and their families are a bloc worth winning.
left.reveal: You grant equivalence to the madrasa degrees. The religious bloc swings gratefully behind you, and the secular establishment recoils as the civil service opens to a parallel education it cannot vouch for.

right.label: Require a bridging standard first.
right.tooltip: Equal recognition demands an equal standard.
right.reveal: You offer recognition conditional on bridging courses in core subjects. The bloc is disappointed at the half-measure, but the civil service keeps a baseline and the secularists are mollified.

speaker: Campaign Data Director
prompt: The unresolved returns, and this time it will not be deferred. Your canvassers can quietly log every household's religion, income, leanings, and grievances into a vast voter database — a powerful targeting tool, and an intrusive dossier on the private lives of millions assembled without consent.

left.label: Build the household database.
left.tooltip: Knowledge of the voter is power over the vote.
left.reveal: Your canvassers compile an intimate map of every household. The targeting grows formidable, and a covert dossier on millions' private lives now sits on a party server, one breach from scandal.

right.label: Canvass without the covert profiling.
right.tooltip: A party that files its citizens is a state I do not want to lead.
right.reveal: You canvass for support without building the secret dossier. Your targeting is cruder, but you refuse to assemble a surveillance file on the people you ask to trust you.

speaker: Constitutional Adviser
prompt: A term later, the same fault line tears open again. You can dissolve parliament early to wrong-foot a fracturing opposition before it can organise — a sharp tactical move that also denies the house its full term and looks like manipulating the calendar for advantage. The adviser lays out the play.

left.label: Dissolve early to catch them unready.
left.tooltip: An opponent off-balance is an opponent beaten.
left.reveal: You dissolve the house and rush the polls. The disorganised opposition is caught flat-footed, and you wear the charge of gaming the calendar for the win it buys you.

right.label: Let parliament run its full term.
right.tooltip: A house cut short for tactics cheapens the next.
right.reveal: You let the term run out naturally. The opposition gets time to organise, but you cannot be accused of dissolving democracy's clock whenever it suited you.

speaker: Chief Whip
prompt: Years into your rule, the matter you thought buried resurfaces — harder. A bloc of your own backbenchers, closer to the public mood than the cabinet, is in open revolt over a deeply unpopular bill. The Whip can break them with threats and patronage, or you can heed them and amend the bill they say is poison on the doorstep.

left.label: Break the rebellion, pass the bill.
left.tooltip: A backbencher's job is to vote, not to think.
left.reveal: You whip the bill through over the rebels' objections. It passes, and so does the public anger they warned of, landing on the very MPs you forced to swallow it.

right.label: Heed the rebels, amend the bill.
right.tooltip: The benches closest to the people often see clearest.
right.reveal: You amend the bill to meet the backbenchers' concerns. The cabinet's pride is dented and the bill weakened, but you have averted a doorstep disaster and kept your MPs loyal.

speaker: Party Treasurer
prompt: A new season of power, an old problem with sharper teeth. A reform would cap corporate donations and require their disclosure — curbing the oligarchs' grip on policy, and choking the single biggest stream into your war chest. The treasurer warns the cut would hurt; the reformers say the influence is rotting the state.

left.label: Block the donation cap.
left.tooltip: A campaign starved of corporate cash is a campaign that loses.
left.reveal: You kill the cap and the corporate money keeps flowing. The war chest stays fat, and the oligarchs' hooks stay deep in your policy, exactly as the reformers feared.

right.label: Back the cap and disclosure.
right.tooltip: A party owned by donors governs for donors.
right.reveal: You support capping and disclosing corporate money. The war chest thins painfully, but you loosen the oligarchs' grip and can credibly claim to have cleaned a corner of the system.

speaker: Information Commissioner
prompt: The crisis returns, and this time the cabinet is watching. A strengthened Right to Information law would force every office to open its files to any citizen who asks — a powerful anti-corruption tool, and a window into the very deals and decisions your government would rather keep dark. The commissioner awaits your stance.

left.label: Gut the Right to Information law.
left.tooltip: An open file is an open invitation to my enemies.
left.reveal: You quietly hollow out the RTI regime with exemptions and delays. The corruption-fighters lose their best tool, and 'a government with something to hide' becomes the obvious reading.

right.label: Strengthen and enforce the RTI law.
right.tooltip: Sunlight is the cheapest disinfectant a state can buy.
right.reveal: You give the RTI law teeth and enforce it. Some embarrassing files surface and ministers squirm, but corruption gets harder and your reform credentials grow real.

speaker: Parliamentary Affairs Minister
prompt: Once more the issue lands on your desk, the stakes higher than before. The parliamentary committee that scrutinises government spending is traditionally chaired by the opposition. You can deny them the chair and pack it with loyalists, neutering oversight, or honour the convention and let your own spending be examined.

left.label: Pack the oversight committee with loyalists.
left.tooltip: I will not hand my critics a microphone and my ledgers.
left.reveal: You take the chair and stack the committee. Government spending sails through unexamined, and the one institution meant to catch waste and graft is turned into a shield for it.

right.label: Give the opposition the chair.
right.tooltip: Spending watched is spending behaved.
right.reveal: You honour the convention and let the opposition scrutinise your ledgers. Some uncomfortable findings emerge, but waste is curbed and the house gains a working check.

speaker: Returning Officer Coordinator
prompt: Another year survived, and the reckoning circles back. The postal ballots of soldiers, police, and officials posted away from home are easy to bundle and steer. The coordinator can quietly manage these service votes as a reliable bloc, or process them transparently like any other ballot.

left.label: Manage the service ballots quietly.
left.tooltip: The men in uniform vote as the chain suggests.
left.reveal: You shepherd the postal votes into a reliable bloc. The numbers help, and the pattern — a service vote that breaks suspiciously one way — is logged by every observer watching.

right.label: Process service ballots transparently.
right.tooltip: A soldier's secret ballot is still a secret ballot.
right.reveal: You insist the postal votes be handled openly and freely. You forgo a managed bloc, but you cannot be accused of voting the barracks by proxy.

speaker: Coalition Manager
prompt: The ground has shifted; the same demand arrives with new menace. The election has produced no majority, and a handful of independents and tiny parties now hold the balance. The manager can assemble a government by lavishing them with ministries and money, or attempt a leaner minority government on issue-by-issue support.

left.label: Buy a majority with ministries and cash.
left.tooltip: A government formed is a government; how matters later.
left.reveal: You assemble the majority by buying the kingmakers wholesale. The cabinet bloats with bought men and their price tags, and your government begins life owing everyone everything.

right.label: Form a lean minority government.
right.tooltip: Better to govern honestly on a knife-edge than to buy a throne.
right.reveal: You build a minority government surviving vote by vote. It is precarious and exhausting, but it is not a bazaar, and each policy must be argued and won rather than purchased.

speaker: Women's Wing Chief
prompt: History rhymes: the conflict reopens deeper than last time. Women's groups demand that the reserved parliamentary seats be filled by direct election rather than party nomination, giving women real mandates instead of appointed tokens. It empowers women — and strips you of dozens of safe, controllable seats.

left.label: Make the reserved seats directly elected.
left.tooltip: A seat won is a voice; a seat granted is an echo.
left.reveal: You back direct election for the women's seats. You lose dozens of controllable appointments, but a cohort of women MPs arrives with real mandates and real independence.

right.label: Keep the reserved seats by nomination.
