right.tooltip: Why give away seats I currently simply assign?
right.reveal: You preserve the nomination system. The women's wing is disappointed and the tokenism critique stands, but you keep dozens of safe, loyal seats firmly in your gift.

speaker: Election Logistics Officer
prompt: Mid-reign, the old wound is torn afresh. On the quiet, the officer can relocate polling stations in opposition strongholds to distant, hard-to-reach venues announced at the last minute — depressing their turnout without a single ballot touched. It is deniable, surgical voter suppression.

left.label: Relocate the opposition's polling stations.
left.tooltip: A booth moved is a vote not cast.
left.reveal: You scatter the opposition's booths to far-off venues at the last hour. Their turnout sags as planned, and the manoeuvre, once mapped by observers, becomes textbook documented suppression.

right.label: Keep polling stations fixed and local.
right.tooltip: A voter should not need a map to find democracy.
right.reveal: You keep every booth where the voters expect it. You forgo a quiet suppression tactic, but no one can chart a pattern of vanished polling stations in your enemies' wards.

speaker: Law Minister
prompt: The pressure you deferred has come due, larger now. The opposition has filed election petitions challenging results in dozens of seats, with real evidence in some. The minister can have the captured courts dismiss them all en masse, or let the genuine cases be heard, risking the loss of a few seats.

left.label: Have the courts dismiss all petitions.
left.tooltip: A result reopened is a result undone.
left.reveal: Your courts throw out every petition, evidence or not. The seats are safe and the message clear — the bench is yours — and the contested results stay forever disputed.

right.label: Let the genuine petitions be heard.
right.tooltip: A seat that survives the court is a seat I truly hold.
right.reveal: You let the courts weigh the real cases. You may lose a few seats to honest rulings, but the ones you keep are unassailable and the judiciary keeps a shred of credibility.

speaker: Cyber Cell Commander
prompt: A later term, and the quarrel has hardened into a test. The cyber cell has produced a convincing deepfake of the opposition leader saying something inflammatory and disqualifying. Released days before the vote, it could end her — until, perhaps, it is debunked, and the forgery is traced back to you.

left.label: Release the deepfake.
left.tooltip: A lie that wins the day outlives its debunking.
left.reveal: You loose the forgery and it rips through the timelines. It may swing the vote before it is exposed, and when it is, the trail back to your cell makes you the story for years.

right.label: Forbid the deepfake.
right.tooltip: A throne won by a forgery rots from the seat up.
right.reveal: You kill the operation. You forgo a devastating dirty trick, but you are spared the day a forensic analyst pins the fabrication on your government for all to see.

speaker: Strategy Director
prompt: The country has changed, but this fault line has not — only worsened. A veteran MP from a safe seat has died, triggering a by-poll. His capable but independent-minded daughter wants the nomination on her own merit; the local fixer wants a pliable outsider who will owe you everything. The director needs the nominee.

left.label: Nominate the pliable outsider.
left.tooltip: A grateful nobody serves better than a proud somebody.
left.reveal: You parachute in the controllable outsider. He wins the safe seat and owes you all, and the late MP's daughter — and the constituency that loved her father — turn bitter.

right.label: Nominate the capable daughter.
right.tooltip: Merit and local roots win seats fixers cannot.
right.reveal: You give the daughter the nomination on merit. The fixer loses his pawn and grumbles, but the seat is won warmly and the benches gain a leader rather than a placeholder.

speaker: Digital Government Adviser
prompt: It returns, as these things do, with the patience gone. A digital one-stop service portal could let citizens get licences, certificates, and payments online — slashing the petty bribery that infests every counter. The clerks who live on those bribes, a vast and connected workforce, will quietly sabotage it.

left.label: Push the digital service rollout hard.
left.tooltip: A counter with no clerk has no palm to grease.
left.reveal: You force the e-services through against the clerks' sabotage. The petty bribery at a thousand counters withers, citizens save time and money, and you bank a visible, popular win.

right.label: Let the rollout stall.
right.tooltip: Do not pick a fight with a million clerks at once.
right.reveal: You let the digital push die quietly to keep the bureaucracy content. The counters stay manned and the palms stay greased, and the citizen keeps paying a small bribe for every stamp.

speaker: Leader of the House
prompt: Years on, the matter is no longer private; the press is already circling. Question hour, where ministers answer the house, has decayed into scripted softballs from your own backbenchers. You can revive it as real, unscripted scrutiny — sharpening your ministers but exposing their failings — or keep it the comfortable theatre it is.

left.label: Keep question hour scripted and safe.
left.tooltip: A minister cornered on live television is a wound.
left.reveal: You keep the softballs flying. Your ministers are spared embarrassment, and parliament's central ritual of accountability stays a hollow performance everyone sees through.

right.label: Revive real, unscripted scrutiny.
right.tooltip: A minister who can answer hard questions is a minister worth having.
right.reveal: You restore genuine question hour. Your weaker ministers flounder under real questioning, but the strong shine, the house comes alive, and accountability stops being a charade.

speaker: Labour Wing Secretary
prompt: The same knot, retied tighter, lands before you once more. You can bring the major trade unions under your party's wing — disciplined turnout and industrial muscle — at the price of either delivering on their wage demands or being seen to betray them. The secretary asks whether to court or keep your distance.

left.label: Bring the unions into the party fold.
left.tooltip: Organised labour, organised for me.
left.reveal: You affiliate the big unions and gain disciplined muscle. They now expect delivery on wages and conditions, and the day you cannot, their organised power turns squarely against you.

right.label: Keep the unions at arm's length.
right.tooltip: A union in the tent demands the tent's wealth.
right.reveal: You keep the unions independent. You forgo their organised turnout, but you are not held hostage to wage demands you may not be able to meet.

speaker: Civic Education Director
prompt: Another monsoon, another return of an argument you cannot end. A neutral, state-funded voter-education drive would teach citizens their rights, how to spot fraud, and how to vote — strengthening democracy, and arming the very voters most likely to catch your machine cutting corners. The director asks whether to fund it.

left.label: Fund the neutral voter-education drive.
left.tooltip: An informed electorate is a legitimate election.
left.reveal: You fund honest voter education. Turnout and confidence rise, and so does the number of citizens who know exactly what an irregularity looks like — a price you accept for a credible vote.

right.label: Let voter education wither.
right.tooltip: Why teach the voter to catch me?
right.reveal: You quietly defund the programme. The electorate stays hazier on its rights and easier to manage, and the democracy you preside over grows a little thinner and more cynical.

speaker: General Secretary
prompt: Deep into the reign, the issue demands a final-sounding answer. Hundreds of your cadres face cases for past political violence — some genuine thugs, some scapegoats. The Secretary wants a blanket amnesty to reward loyalty; the alternative is to let the courts sort the guilty from the wronged.

left.label: Grant the cadres a blanket amnesty.
left.tooltip: I do not abandon the foot-soldiers who carried me.
left.reveal: You wipe the cadres' cases clean en masse. Loyalty is rewarded and the machine reassured, and genuine thugs walk free alongside the wronged, impunity stamped a little deeper.

right.label: Let the courts separate guilty from wronged.
right.tooltip: An amnesty for thugs is a licence for the next.
right.reveal: You refuse the blanket pardon and let cases proceed individually. The cadres feel betrayed and some desert, but the genuinely innocent are cleared and the guilty face the law.

speaker: Overseas Wing Secretary
prompt: The reckoning you postponed has matured into a crisis. If overseas voting is introduced at embassies, the thin oversight abroad makes the ballots ripe for manufacturing. The secretary can quietly pad the diaspora vote at friendly missions, or insist the overseas count be observed as rigorously as any at home.

left.label: Pad the overseas vote quietly.
left.tooltip: Distant ballots are the least watched of all.
left.reveal: You manufacture margins at the friendly embassies. The diaspora numbers swell suspiciously, and the watchdogs add 'phantom overseas votes' to the dossier on the election.

right.label: Observe the overseas count rigorously.
right.tooltip: A vote cast abroad must be as clean as one cast at home.
right.reveal: You insist on full observation of the embassy ballots. You forgo an easy padding, but the diaspora vote becomes a point of pride rather than another asterisk.

speaker: Strategy Director
prompt: A new chapter, the same antagonists, less room to manoeuvre. In seats where your candidate is weak, the director proposes secretly funding 'independent' spoilers to split the opposition vote. It is cynical and effective, and a leak would expose you as manufacturing fake candidates to game the result.

left.label: Fund the spoiler independents.
left.tooltip: A split rival is a beaten rival.
left.reveal: You bankroll the spoilers and the opposition vote fractures in the target seats. The tactic delivers, and the day a funded 'independent' talks, it confirms every accusation of a rigged field.

right.label: Win the seats honestly or lose them.
right.tooltip: A manufactured candidate is a confession in waiting.
right.reveal: You refuse the spoiler scheme and fight the weak seats straight. You lose some you might have stolen, but no funded fake candidate can ever surface to sink you.

speaker: Constitutional Adviser
prompt: The old fault line widens; this time there is no quiet option. The constitution provides for an ombudsman to investigate official corruption and maladministration — an office every government since independence has conveniently left unfilled. The adviser asks whether to finally appoint a real one, or keep the chair empty.

left.label: Keep the ombudsman's chair empty.
left.tooltip: An office uncreated cannot investigate me.
left.reveal: You leave the post unfilled, as your predecessors did. The constitutional check stays a dead letter, and the corruption it was meant to catch goes on unmolested.

right.label: Appoint a real, empowered ombudsman.
right.tooltip: A check unfilled for fifty years shames us all.
right.reveal: You finally appoint a genuine ombudsman with teeth. Some of your own are caught in its early cases, but you accomplish what no government dared, and the reform is historic.

speaker: Home Affairs Coordinator
prompt: Time has not healed it — the conflict comes back armed. Executive magistrates with mobile courts roam the polls on election day, and the coordinator can deploy them as a partisan instrument — harassing opposition agents, waving through your irregularities — or as genuinely neutral keepers of the peace.

left.label: Deploy the magistrates as a partisan tool.
left.tooltip: Authority on the ground should answer to me.
left.reveal: Your magistrates lean on the opposition and overlook your side's tricks. The polls tilt under colour of law, and the partisan use of the bench becomes one more documented stain.

right.label: Deploy them as neutral peace-keepers.
right.tooltip: A magistrate with a thumb on the scale poisons the whole poll.
right.reveal: You instruct the magistrates to be strictly impartial. They keep order without favour, and the polls run with a fairness your fixers wanted to subvert.

speaker: Electoral Roll Officer
prompt: Once again, and with the whole house holding its breath. Tying the voter roll to the national ID database lets you purge 'unverifiable' voters — a clean-up that, applied selectively, can quietly strike opposition-leaning groups (the poor, migrants, minorities) off the list. The officer awaits your aim.

left.label: Purge selectively against opposition groups.
left.tooltip: A roll trimmed of my enemies is a roll improved.
left.reveal: You scrub the list with a partisan hand, and the poor, the migrant, and the minority find themselves voteless. The disenfranchisement is quiet, deniable, and — once mapped — damning.

right.label: Verify the roll fairly and add the missing.
right.tooltip: A purge that targets the poor is a coup with a database.
right.reveal: You clean the roll honestly and run a drive to register the missing. The list loses its phantoms without losing its poor, and the franchise widens rather than narrows.

speaker: Youth Wing Coordinator
prompt: The matter resurfaces in a harder year, patience exhausted. To win back the youth alienated by the quota unrest, the coordinator proposes a vast national youth festival — concerts, job fairs, sports — at considerable cost. The cynics call it a bribe; the optimists call it the only bridge left to a hostile generation.

left.label: Fund the national youth festival.
left.tooltip: A bridge to the young is worth its price in taka.
left.reveal: You bankroll the festival and reach a generation that had written you off. It is costly and some sneer at the gloss, but the outreach softens the youth's hostility a little.

right.label: Skip the festival, save the money.
right.tooltip: A concert does not undo a killing.
right.reveal: You decline the spending as hollow. The treasury is spared, but the alienated youth see no gesture at all, and the generational rift hardens another notch.

speaker: Parliamentary Affairs Minister
prompt: A later season of rule, the stakes now openly existential. An MP wants to haul a journalist before the house on a 'breach of privilege' for an unflattering report — a quasi-judicial summons that intimidates the whole press gallery. The minister asks whether to back the motion or quash it.

left.label: Back the privilege motion.
left.tooltip: The dignity of the house must be defended.
left.reveal: You let the house summon and censure the journalist. The press gallery gets the message and softens, and parliament adds 'cudgel against reporters' to its repertoire.

right.label: Quash the motion.
right.tooltip: A parliament that bullies the press fears it.
right.reveal: You kill the privilege motion. The thin-skinned MP fumes, but the press keeps its independence and you are spared a needless fight with the entire media.

speaker: Cabinet Secretary
prompt: It comes around again, and the margin for error is gone. Several ministers are visibly enriching themselves and their families on the public dime. The secretary proposes a real, enforced code of conduct with asset declarations and resignations for breaches — a clean-up that would gut some of your most loyal grandees.

left.label: Enforce a real ministerial code.
left.tooltip: A cabinet of looters is a scandal with portfolios.
left.reveal: You impose asset declarations and enforce resignations. A few loyal grandees fall and the cabinet trembles, but the public sees, astonished, that office is no longer a licence to loot.

right.label: Leave the ministers to their ways.
right.tooltip: A loyal grandee's loyalty is bought with a free hand.
right.reveal: You decline to bind your ministers. They stay loyal and enriched, and 'one law for them' becomes the defining grievance of your government.

speaker: Chief Whip
prompt: The same demand, but the reign is older and the knives sharper. The president is elected by parliament, and a faction of your MPs is flirting with backing a rival candidate to send you a message. The Whip can crush the cross-voting with threats, or let the secret ballot speak and read the result as a warning.

left.label: Crush the cross-voting with threats.
left.tooltip: A secret ballot is no excuse for disloyalty.
left.reveal: You lean hard on the waverers and your candidate wins clean. The dissent is suppressed, but you have learned nothing about the discontent that prompted it, and it festers unspoken.

right.label: Let the secret ballot speak.
right.tooltip: A warning shot heard is a rebellion averted.
right.reveal: You let the MPs vote freely and read the bruised result as the message it is. Your candidate squeaks in, and you act on the discontent before it grows into something you cannot whip.

speaker: General Secretary
prompt: Years deep, the unresolved returns to be resolved for good. A reshuffle looms, and you can either reward raw loyalty — keeping the faithful incompetents who will never plot — or promote on competence, building a cabinet that governs well but contains ambitious talents who might one day eclipse you.

left.label: Reshuffle for loyalty over competence.
left.tooltip: A loyal fool never sharpens a knife for me.
left.reveal: You keep the faithful and the mediocre in post. The cabinet is safe and pliant, and the country is governed by people chosen for their harmlessness rather than their skill.

right.label: Reshuffle for competence.
right.tooltip: A government that cannot govern is loyal to nothing.
right.reveal: You promote the capable, ambition and all. Governance sharpens and the public notices, and you accept the risk that one of your talents may grow large enough to threaten you.

speaker: Strategy Director
prompt: The conflict reignites, and the world is watching this time. Doubts about your mandate are corroding your authority. The director floats a bold gamble: a national referendum on your continued rule — a chance to silence the doubters with a fresh popular seal, or a noose if the country says no.

left.label: Call the referendum.
left.tooltip: A fresh mandate silences every whisper.
left.reveal: You put your rule to the people. It is a genuine gamble, and the campaign consumes the country; whatever the result, you have staked your legitimacy on a single roll of the dice.

right.label: Refuse to gamble on a referendum.
right.tooltip: A vote I might lose is a weapon I hand my enemies.
right.reveal: You decline the plebiscite. You keep the doubts unresolved rather than risk a public verdict, and the legitimacy question goes on quietly eating at your authority.

speaker: Strategy Director
prompt: A familiar crisis, escalated by everything you let slide. You called it, and the referendum is tainted by a low, suspicious turnout and a result too lopsided to believe. The director asks whether to trumpet the landslide as vindication or admit the obvious and temper your claims.

left.label: Trumpet the landslide as vindication.
left.tooltip: A win is a win; let the doubters choke on it.
left.reveal: You proclaim the implausible result a ringing endorsement. Your base cheers, but no one outside it believes a 98 percent on a 30 percent turnout, and the 'vindication' becomes a punchline.

right.label: Admit the flaws, temper your claims.
right.tooltip: A boast no one believes is worse than silence.
right.reveal: You acknowledge the weak turnout and claim only a modest mandate. The base wanted triumphalism, but the candour spares you the ridicule and salvages a sliver of credibility.

speaker: Strategy Director
prompt: The fault line cracks wider; the decision can no longer wait. The referendum gambit, whatever its result, has emboldened the opposition to demand a real, internationally-observed election as the only true test. The director asks whether to concede the clean election or dig in behind the referendum's verdict.

left.label: Dig in behind the referendum.
left.tooltip: I have had my plebiscite; I owe them no more.
left.reveal: You refuse the clean election and hide behind the tainted referendum. The opposition's demand hardens into a movement, and your legitimacy gap, papered over, widens beneath the paper.

right.label: Concede a clean, observed election.
right.tooltip: Only a real vote ends a real doubt.
right.reveal: You agree to the genuine, observed election. The hardliners call it surrender, but you gamble that a clean mandate, even a narrow one, is worth more than a hollow one defended forever.
